This  is  a  digital  copy  of  a  book  that  was  preserved  for  generations  on  library  shelves  before  it  was  carefully  scanned  by  Google  as  part  of  a  project 

to  make  the  world's  books  discoverable  online. 

It  has  survived  long  enough  for  the  copyright  to  expire  and  the  book  to  enter  the  public  domain.  A  public  domain  book  is  one  that  was  never  subject 

to  copyright  or  whose  legal  copyright  term  has  expired.  Whether  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  may  vary  country  to  country.  Public  domain  books 

are  our  gateways  to  the  past,  representing  a  wealth  of  history,  culture  and  knowledge  that's  often  difficult  to  discover. 

Marks,  notations  and  other  maiginalia  present  in  the  original  volume  will  appear  in  this  file  -  a  reminder  of  this  book's  long  journey  from  the 

publisher  to  a  library  and  finally  to  you. 

Usage  guidelines 

Google  is  proud  to  partner  with  libraries  to  digitize  public  domain  materials  and  make  them  widely  accessible.  Public  domain  books  belong  to  the 
public  and  we  are  merely  their  custodians.  Nevertheless,  this  work  is  expensive,  so  in  order  to  keep  providing  tliis  resource,  we  liave  taken  steps  to 
prevent  abuse  by  commercial  parties,  including  placing  technical  restrictions  on  automated  querying. 
We  also  ask  that  you: 

+  Make  non-commercial  use  of  the  files  We  designed  Google  Book  Search  for  use  by  individuals,  and  we  request  that  you  use  these  files  for 
personal,  non-commercial  purposes. 

+  Refrain  fivm  automated  querying  Do  not  send  automated  queries  of  any  sort  to  Google's  system:  If  you  are  conducting  research  on  machine 
translation,  optical  character  recognition  or  other  areas  where  access  to  a  large  amount  of  text  is  helpful,  please  contact  us.  We  encourage  the 
use  of  public  domain  materials  for  these  purposes  and  may  be  able  to  help. 

+  Maintain  attributionTht  GoogXt  "watermark"  you  see  on  each  file  is  essential  for  in  forming  people  about  this  project  and  helping  them  find 
additional  materials  through  Google  Book  Search.  Please  do  not  remove  it. 

+  Keep  it  legal  Whatever  your  use,  remember  that  you  are  responsible  for  ensuring  that  what  you  are  doing  is  legal.  Do  not  assume  that  just 
because  we  believe  a  book  is  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  the  United  States,  that  the  work  is  also  in  the  public  domain  for  users  in  other 
countries.  Whether  a  book  is  still  in  copyright  varies  from  country  to  country,  and  we  can't  offer  guidance  on  whether  any  specific  use  of 
any  specific  book  is  allowed.  Please  do  not  assume  that  a  book's  appearance  in  Google  Book  Search  means  it  can  be  used  in  any  manner 
anywhere  in  the  world.  Copyright  infringement  liabili^  can  be  quite  severe. 

About  Google  Book  Search 

Google's  mission  is  to  organize  the  world's  information  and  to  make  it  universally  accessible  and  useful.   Google  Book  Search  helps  readers

NEW  YORK,  July  7.  1883.  Whole  No.  598. 


TO  BOOKS. 


We  desire  to  call  the  attention  of  the  book- 
trade  in  the  We»t  and  South,  who  would  find 
it  more  convenient  and  profitable  to  obtain 
their  Bupplies  from  this  point,  to  the  fact 
that  we  have  establtahed  in  Cincinnati  a  Depot 
for  the  aale  of  the  publieationa  of  Harper  <£  Brothers,  of  2fetv  York, 
Their  large  list  of  Publications,  embracing  Miscellaneous  Sdtool 
and  College  Text-Book,  we  keep  in  large  quantities,  enabling  us  to 
fill  orders  promptly,  without  the  delay  experienced  in  ordering  from 
Sew  York,  our  purpose  being  to  bring  the  source  of  supply  nearer  to 
Western  dealers. 

We  beg  the  trade  to  notice  particularly,  that  we  furnish  these  publi- 
cations at  the  same  rates  as  obtained  in  New  York,  thus  giving  to  Western 
buyers  the  advantage  of  a  saviitg  in  freights,  and  of  obtaining  goods 
in  less  time  than  from  the  East. 

We  shall  have  on  sate,  in  Cfindnnati,  sin^ultaneous  with  their  issue 
in  2/ew  York,  aU  new  publications  of  the  Messrs.  Harper,  enabling  us 
to  supply  them  at  the  earliest  possible  moment. 
Soliciting  your  orders,  we  are. 

Yours,  Very  respectfUUy, 

H.  W.  DBRBX^  ( 
200  We»t  4th  St.,  Cincinnati,  O.

NOTES  IN  SEASON. 
Henry  Holt  &  Co.  have  jiut  published  Berth- 
old  Aucrbach's  last  novel,  "Master  Bielaad 
and  his  Workmen. "  Thejr  have  under  way  a  new 
Mries  entitled  Stern's  Stittleii  Girman  CamedUi, 
giviag  tables  of  difficulties  (in  Gertnan.)  pre- 
pared for  atndents.  The  initial  volume  will  be 
■  Etn  Knopf,"  by  Julius  von  Rosen,  and  other 
volumes  will  be  announced  in  the  fall. 


Lee&  Shepard  have  publiiihed  a  "  Hand-book 
of  Universal  Phonography  ;  or,  Shorthand  by 
the  Allen  Method,"  by  G.  G.  Allen,  Principal 
of  [he  Allen  Stenographic  Inst.,  Boston.  They 
have  in  preparation  a  new  noveUby  the  author 
of  "That  Husband  of  Mine,"  entitled  "His 
Triumphs." 

.  CuppLES.  Upham  &  Co.  will  publish  imme- 
diately a  memorial  of  the  late  Alonzo  Prouty,  in 
the  shape  of  a  worit  on  "Fish,  their  Habits, 
Haunts,  and  the  best  methods  of  taking  ihem" 
together  with  descriptions  of  Irips  made  by  Mr, 
Prouly  in  the  woods  of  Maine  and  Nova  Scotia. 
The  work  is  in  part  written  by  Mr.  Prouty  him- 
self, and  in  part  compiled  from  his  journal,  by 
his  widow.  It  will  be  a  capital  guide  for  fisher- 
men, as  it  gives  valuable  information  about  all 
the  details  of  Eshing. 

P.  Blakiston,  Son  &  Co.  have  just  issued  a 
a  novel  and  useful  "  Record  for  the  Sick-Room," 
in  which  are  spaces  for  lime  of  observation, 
slate  of  pulse,  temperature,  respiration,  medi- 
cines, nourishment,  condition  of  bowels,  appli- 
cation of  bath  or  lotion,  tempetalure  of  room, 
general  remarks  on  patient,  physicians'  direc* 
tlons.  memoranda  (or  nurse,  etc.,  also  apage  of 
directions  (or  nurses  as  to  administration  of 
remedies,  table  of  poisons  and  antidotes,  in- 
structions in  emergencies, 
matters.  Thebookissoar] 
history  of  a  case  in  detail  r 
future  use  by  either  physici 
books  being  uniform,  a  number  of  ca 
bound  in  one  volume.  They  hav 
ready  a  new  edition  of  Proctor's  Pharmacy, 
much  enlarged  and  containing  wood  and  litho- 
graphic illustrations.

Dillingham  ;  Vice  President,  Tbos.  R.  Knox ; 
Second  Vice  President,  Robert  Mortis  ;  Treas-

way,  N.  V.

Harper  &  Bros.

May  we  so  (ar  ditiurb  Ihe  minds  of  the  fortu- 
oate  publishers  who  are  preparing  for  [beir  sum- 
mer's rest,  as  to  remind  them  that  at  no  time  of 
Ihe  year  is  more  bard  work  done  at  this  office 
than  during  the  heated  term.  It  is  not  our  choice 
but  necessitated  by  the  fact  that  the  material  we 
work  up  does  not  reach  us  until  the  summer  has 
well'set  in.  Tools  must  be  ready  when  Ibe  rest- 
ed workers  return  to  handle  them  ;  they  make 
no  allowance  for  delay  when  the  rush  of  business 
Is  upon  the  trade,  and  the  wane  of  worked-up, 
ready,  revised  lists,  would  be  not  only  an  incon- 
venience, but  a  serious  loss  of  time  and  business 
to  the  bookseller. 

And  yel,  these  facts  being  known,  every  year, 
without  e^icepiion,  the  whole  trade  is  inconveni- 
enced by  a  few  publishers  who,  in  their  eagerness 
to  get  away  from  work  and  worry,  rush  oH  without 
remembering  our  requests,  and  without  reflecting 
thai  a  slight  neglect,  which  seems  so  trivial  at 
the  dead  season,  maybe  fell  later  OD  in  Che  busy 
season  by  themselves  as  well  as  others.  We 
therefore  beg  (or  prompt  action  and  co-operalion 
for  their  own  sakes,  and  put  in  a.  little  plea  for 
mercy  for  ourselves,  not  to  have  our  summer 
work  made  more  irksome  than  its  peculiar  char- 
acter makes  unavoidable.  First  there  is  the 
EntCATiONAL  Catalogue,  which  we  have  faith. 
fully  and  promptly  published  since  tSyo.  Every 
year  It  has  to  be  made  from  the  beginning,  every 
title  compared  and  Ihe  prices  checked  off,  for  it 
seems  there  are  few  things  more  unstable  than 
[he  prices  of  school-books.  Several  publishers 
have  not  yel  notified  us  of  their  decision,  though 
as  a  rule  the  educational  publishers  have  re- 
sponded more  promptly  and  handsomely  than  ! 
for  many  a  past  year.  Then  follows  the  Trade 
List  Anm-ai.,  which,  since  lis  d£but  in  1S73,  1 


has  grown  stronger  every  year,  and  which  this 
year  promises  to  exceed  all  its  former  issues  ia 
its  representative  value.  All  the  more  reason  to 
have  it  appear  in  good  time.  The  more  In- 
terests it  represents  the  more  iatcrests  it  appeals 
to  and  Ibe  more  should  Ihese  interests  be  respect- 
ed by  each  individtul  concerned.  It  is  certainly 
not  fair  thai  the  majority  of  ihe  trade  and  those 
too  who  are  the  most  prompt,  should  suffer  from 
the  tardiness  and  inde  jision  of  the  few.  If  it  were 
not  that  we  realize  that  the  absence  of  certaio 
lists  would  inconvenience  the  bookseller  more 
than  their  delay,  we  should  exclude  all  lists  that 
would  interfere  by  their  lateiieis  with  the  prompt 
publication  uf  the  An'm;al.  It  is  necessary,  how- 
ever, to  be  more  prompt  in  future,  for  only  by 
such  promptness  can  we  be  In  lime  also  with  our 
Fall  and  Christmas  publications.  And  we  are 
determined  this  year  to  be  ahead  of  any  previous 
season  ;  for  ii  has  been  fully  demonstrated  to 
all  concerned,  that  every  day's  gain  in  the  issue 
of  our  catalogues  makes  the  investment  more 
profitable  to  the  booksellers  Hence  publishers. 
in  their  own  interests,  should  help  us  to  help 

Publishers  that  have  not  yet  given  their  in- 
struclions  concerning  the  supply  of  their  lists  for 
the  Trade  List  Anxual,  should  do  so  before 
leaving  town.  Our  circular  slating  the  particulars 
should  be  placed  into  the  hands  of  those  who- 
are  intrusted  with  the  despatch.  A  duplicate 
copy  will  be  sent  should  our  first  have  been  mis- 
laid or  not  received.  All  the  lists  should  be  de- 
livered by  August  I. 

The  Edl'catlonal  CATAtOouE  is  so  far  ad- 
vanced that  any  further  additior 
must  be  sent  at  once  ;  also  adve 
publishers  are  again  respectfully  reminded  that 
if  the  usefulness  of  the  Eiilcational  Cata- 
LOcuE  is  recognized  by  the  trade,  it  should  meet 
with  such  recognition  on  their  part  as  alone. 
enables  us  to  continue  Ibe  publication. 

And  that  the  usefulness  of  Ihe  Educational 
Cataloui')^  is  reL-ogniied  is  shown  by  our  ad- 
vance orders,  which  to-day  have  reached  nearly 
20,ooocopies — the  largest  number  we  have  bad 
since  the  educational  publishers  have  adopted 
their  new  methods.  This  also  shows  that  Ibe  live 
booksellers  have  rallied  and  are  determined  not 
to  be  ousted  again  from  turning  an  honesl  penny 
in  leeitimaie  business.  Booksellers  that  have  not 
yet  sent  their  orders  should  make  immediate  ap- 
plication to  secure  the  benefit  of  thefirti  edition. 
The  paper  on  hand  for  the  imprint  edition  is 
limited  10  25.000  copies.  Should  the  orders  con- 
tinue in  the  ratio  of  Ine  early  responses,  we:

shall  be  glad  to  do  so  :  but  there  will  necessa- 
rily be  some  delay  in  the  filling  vE  sucfa^orders.

POSTAL  MATTERS. 
TIfE  NEW  HONEY- OKDER  SYSTEM. 
Dr.  McDonald,  Superincendeni  of  the  Money- 
Order  System  of  cbe  Post-OIBce  Department,  we 
learn  from  the  CItviland  Httald,  has  given  it  out 
that  the  lair  providing  for  "  postal  rates"  will  go 
into  effect  on  Ihc  3d  of  September.  The  reduc- 
tion in  fees  to  be  charged  the  public  for  domes- 
tic money-orders"  will  go  into  effect  on  the  2d 
of  July,  and  after  the  same  date  a  single  do- 
mestic money-order  may  be  issued  for  any 
amount  from  i  cent  to  $100,  instead  of  tsOj  '^ 
(be  limit  has  been  hitherto.  For  orders  not  ex- 
ceeding $10  a  fee  of  a  cents  will  be  charged. 
This  lee  is  I  or  3  cents  less  than  the  actual  cost 
10  the  Governmiint,  but  il  makes  up  the  loss 
from  the  increased  fees  charged  for  transmitting 
larger  sums  of  money.  As  a  matter  of  fact,  it 
costs  the  govcrnmcnc  the  same  lo  transmit  an 
order  for  $ioas  it  does  one  for  f  100,  the  clerical 
work  b«ing  the  same,  but  by  the  larger  fee  on 
larger  orders  the  loss  on  the  smaller  ones  is 
made  up.  the  fee  on  an  order  for  8100  being  45 
cents.  The  fees  upon  inlemalionat  money-or- 
ders remain  unchanged.  The  question  o(  in- 
creasing the  maximum  limit  of  a  single  inter- 
national money-order  tofioo  is  to  be  considered 
by  the  post-office  officials,  though  additional 
legislation  on  the  part  of  Congress  will  be  nec- 
essary to  carry  out  this  object.  The  money- 
order  system  is  developing  rapidly  and  growing 
in  public  esteem,  as  a  safe  means  for  the  trans- 
mission of  money.  Last  year  the  average  loss 
was  one  oOt  of  175,000  orders  issued.  The  loss. 
however,  does  not  fall  upon  the  public  unless 
the  payer  or  payee  is  at  fault,  but  is  collected  by 
the  Government  from  the  official  who  is  lo 
blam«.  and  made  good  to  the  loser.  The  inter- 
national  order  system  is  rapidly  extending 
throughout  the  civilized  world.  It  now  embraces 
the  United  Sutes,  British  North  America,  Great 
Britain  and  Ireland,  Germany,  Switzerland, 
luly,  France,  Algeria,  Jamaica,  New  South 
Wales,  Victoria.  New  Zealand.  Belgium,  Tas- 
mania, British  India,  Denmark,  Norway,  Swe- 
den, the  Netherlands,  and  Austria.  On  and  after 
July  %,  Portugal  exchanges  with  the  United 
States,  and  negotiations  are  in  progress  with  the 
governments  of  the  Hawaiian  Kingdom  and 
that  of  the  Cape  of  Good  Hope,  and  it  is  thought 
they  will  come  into  the  circle  by  the  ist  of  Oc- 
tober, and  Japan  by  the  ist  of  January  next. 

BEDFORD  THE  BINDER. 

Mr.  G.  W.  Smai-lev,  in  the  Tnbam,  July  i, 
pays  the  following  tribute  to  this  famous  binder : 

"Mr.  Bedford,  the  bookbinder,  is  dead.  His 
name  has  been  known  for  a  generation,  and  his 
reputation  during  a  long  period  stood  higher 
than  that  of  any  English  rival.  "  Bound  by  F. 
Bedford"  was  at  one  time  thought  tu  be  a  guar- 
antee of  good  workmanship,  and,  which  is  not 
less  important,  of  completeness  in  the  book  so 
slampcd.  Of  late  years,  Mr.  Bedford's  standard 
of  excellence  fell  off.  The  American  collector 
who  cares  tor  good  examples  of  his  work  will 
do  well  to  choose  bindings  executed  not  less  than 
ten  years  ago.  11!  health  had  something  to  do 
with  the  inferiority  of  the  more  recent  work, 
and  other  causes  which  one  need  not  now  dwell 
upon.  A  man  is  perhaps  entitled  to  be  judged 
by  his  best — at  any  rate  not  by  his  worst  work, 
and  by  the  standard  of  his  country.    The  best 


things  Bedford  ever  did  never  approached  in 
merit  the  half  dozen  second  best  French  binders, 
while  to  Trautz,  incomparably  the  first  artist  of 
the  century,  nobody  would  dream  of  comparing 
Bedford.  He  himself  cheerfnlly  admitted  the 
superiority  of  the  French.  I  used  to  go  to  his 
place  in  York  Street.  He  was  always  ready  lo 
discuss  bindings,  and  I  have  heard  him  say  more 
than  once  that  all  the  good  he  knew  he  had 
learned  while  working  with  Cap6  (a  Frenchman 
at  one  lime  in  repute),  while  ail  the  bad  things 
he  did  he  had  been  laughl  in  England.  He 
never  pretended  10  originality.  His  standard  of 
perfection  was  a  mechanical  standard.  So  far 
as  design  was  concerned,  he  was  content  to 
copy — to  reproduce  a  pattern  in  mosaic  from  a 
volume  that  had  belonged  to  Grolier,  or  to  imi- 
tate, which  he  did  with  exactness,  the  monoto- 
nous Iteration  of  Roger  Payne,  perhaps  the  one 
Englishman  who  was  capable  of  binding  belter 
than  he  knew." 

PUBLISHERS'  PROFITS.* 
To  tht  Editor  ef  The  Nation  ; 

Sir  :  Your  correspondent,  Mr.  Franklin,  in 
^K  Nation  of  May  31,  clearly  points  out  Ibe 
folly  of  attempting  to  determine  the  profits  of 
author  and  publisher  from  a  comparison  of  the 
gross  receipts  in  each  case.  His  staiement, 
however,  that  "it  is  quite  possible  that,  from 
the  point  of  view  of  theoretical  justice,  not  only 
five  to  one,  but  fifty  to  one,  is  an  understate- 
ment of  the  difference  between  profits  "  in  favor 
of  the  publisher,  merely  echoes  the  papular 
opinion  that  authors  as  a  class  have  a  genuine 
grievance  against  publishers  on  the  score  of  an 
unfair  division  of  the  net  avails  arising  from  the 
publication  of  books.  I  believe  this  opinion  is 
erroneous,  and  therefore  does  publishers  an  in- 
justice. No  doubt  il  owes  its  existence  in  large 
measure  to  the  ignorance  of  ibose  who  hold  it 
as  to  the  cost  atlendingthe  publication  of  boolcs. 
Many  individual  cases  of  gross  injustice  upon 
the  part  of  publisher  toward  author  have  oc- 
curred, and  many  others  are  likely  to  occur  in 
the  future,  but  with  these  1  am  not  at  present 
concerned.  Are  publishers  as  a  class  unjust  to 
authors  ?     It  is  so  alleged. 

It  is  clear  that  the  author  whose  reputation  Is 
established  can  have  no  ground  for  complaint, 
as  he  holds  "  the  balance  of  power,"  and  is  in  a 
position  to  dictate  fair  and  honorable  terms. 
Publishing  houses  are  eager  for  books  by  the 
writer,  the  merit  and  popularity  of  whose  pub- 
lished works  are  a  sufficient  guarantee  10  insure 
the  success  of  any  new  production  from  bis  pen. 
Neither  can  the  author  grieve  over  an  unjtiit 
division  who  sells  his  manuscripts  outright  for 
an  agreed  sum  ;  nor  he  who  chooses  lo  become 
his  own  publisher,  and  thus  obtains  whatever 
profit  his  venture  realizes.  The  grounds  for  a 
teat  grievance  can  therefore  exist,  if  at  all,  only 
with  those  writers  who  publish  on  the  royalty 
plan,  agreeing  with  their  publishers  to  receive 
as  their  share  of  the  profits  a  percentage  of  the 
retail  price  of  their  books.  Il  is  safe  to  say  thai 
the  average  sale  of  all  books  brought  before  the 
public  on  this  plan  will  not  exceed  five  thousand 
copies  for  any  one  publication  ;  or,  in  other 
words,  of  books  so  published,  those  whole  sale  is 
less  than  five  thousand  exceed  the  number  whose 
sale  is  greater.     Now,  any  reliable  publisher.  I 


•S«Pi 


■'  WaiKLv.  Jun 


The  Publuktrs'  Weekly. 


\N0.  598]  July  7,  '83. 


ibink.  <T[tt  verify  the  •uieroeni  that  uiilesi  the  | 
sale  exceed  Ave  thousand  copies,  the  author, 
nine  times  in  tea,  receives  a  larger  profit  than 
his  publither.  For  example,  from  a  book  irhose 
retail  price  is  fixed  at  $1.50,  and  which  reuhet  1 
an  ultimate  sale  of  five  thousand  copies,  the  ' 
author,  at  a  rooaltj  of  fifteen  cents  for  each 
copy  sold,  will  invariably  derive  a  profit  of  $750, 
while  the  publisher's  profit  will  oftener  be  lets 
than  more  thau  that  sum.  Were  seventy-five 
cents,  as  erroneously  stated  in  the  Natinn  of  ' 
Mbv  17,  his  true  profit,  he  mould  realize  a  gain 
of  t3750.  But  before  he  can  ascertain  his  profit 
he  must  subtract  from  this  amount  the  cost  of 
the  electrotype  plates,  the  cost  of  the  manufact-  '• 
ure  of  the  five  thousand  copies,  the  expense  ot  1 
advertising,  shipping,  collecting,  bis  losses  from 
bad  accounts,  the  cost  of  'unsold  copies  and  of 
tbuse  given  for  review,  and  any  other  outlay 
Dot  here  specified. 

However  much  "  theoretical  justice"  might 
be  made  to   yield   the  author,   practical  justice 


.,  O.,  Jut

COMMUNICATIONS. 

COVERNMKNT  CONTRACTS. 
Ediler  PubHskers-  Wetify  : 

iNyour  issue  of  June  30th,  you  state  that  "Wm. 
A.  Davis  has  t>een  awarded  the  contract  (or 
supplying  niucilage  and  ink  10  the  ist  and  3d 
class  post-offices.  War  and  Treasury  Depart- 
ments of  the  entire  U.  S."  This  statement  is 
wide   of  the   mark  and   calculated  to  do  other 


A.  Davis  gets  the  award  for  50  gi 
J.  D.  Free  gets  the  award  on  ao  doz.  pints  of 
Mr.  Davis's  crimson.  The  balance  of  the  ink  is 
awarded  to  other  parties.  So  also  with  mttcilage, 
Mr.  Davis  gets  only  a  portion. 

The  War  Department  calls  for  thirteen  kinds 
of  ink.  and  Mr.  Davis  get*  none  of  them,  so 
also  in  the  post-office  he  gets  no  ink,  and  only  a 
part  of  the  mucilage. 

I  have  no  desire  to  detract  from  the  honor  ac- 
corded Mr,  Davis,  but  it  ought  not  be  sought  at 
the  expense  of  truth.  The  fact  is  these  contracts, 
are  pretty  well  divided,  and  no  one  person  con- 
trols them  very  largely..  It  is  reduced  to  a  ques- 
tion of  how  much  you  are  witling  to  lose  or  how 
small  a  margin  you  can  get  clear  with.  The 
government  get  their  supplies  at  bottom  prices, 
and  nobody  will  ever  get  rich  on  the  profits. 

Yours  very  truly        Wm.  Bali.antyne. 

Washi.vuton,  D,  C.  June  30, 1883. 

JOURNALISTIC   NOTES. 

The  Chrislian  Liadtr,  the  organ  of  the  Uaiver 
salist  denomination,  has  been  enlarged  from  48 
to  56  coltimns  and  3l  inches  added  to  the  length 
of  the  columns.  The  Rev.  Dr.  A.  Gunnison  will 
contribute  a  very  interesting  series  of  "  Letters 
on  California  "  to  its  columns. 

The  sutcraent  that  Mr.  George  W,  Chllds,  of 
the  Philadelphia  Public  Uiger.  had  purchased 
the  Germantown  Tettgraph  from  its  veteran  ed- 
itor. Major  Philip  R.  Freas,  is  incorrect.  Mr. 
Henry  W.  Raymond,  for  many  years  connected 
with  the  Chicago  Tribune  as  its  literary  editor, 
has  purchased  the  Telegraph,  and  Mr.  Chitdshas 
guaranteed  the  sale. 


LITERARY  AND- TRADE  NOTES. 

D.  &  J.  Sadlier  &  Co.  have  issued  a  cheap 
edition  of  Moore's  "Irish  Melodies,"  with  a 
memoir  and  notes  by  Mr.  John  Savage. 

W.  R.  Jenkins  will  issue  immediately  "  Le 
Gentilhomme  Pauvre."  by  Dumanoir  and  Le- 
farque — being  the  luurth  number  of  the  Th6iitre 
Con  temporal  n. 

Funk  &  Wacnalls  have  in  preparation, 
"Historical  and  Other  Studies,"  by  J.  A. 
Froude  ;  "  Artisan  Life  In  the  Time  of  Jesus," 
by  Prof.  Frani  Delitzsch  ;  and  "By-ways  of 
Literature,"  by  Dr.  D,  H.  Wheeler. 

Tke  Old  Testament  company  of  revisers  have 
now  completed  their  last  revision  and  are  mak- 
ing up  the  appendix  containing  tbe  unadjusted 
differences  between  the  American  and  English 
revisers.  The  revision  will  be  completed  prob- 
ably toward  the  close  of  the  vefar,  and  nill  be 
brought  out  next  year  by  the  Oxford  and  Cam- 
bridge Presses.

on  "  French  and  German  Socialism  in  Modem

"  Life  of  James  Buchanan."

The  catalogue  this  year  follows  the  alphabetical  system  pronounced  by  the  experience  of  the 
trade  the  most  latisfaclory  for  practical  use.  It  Includes  a  price-list  arranged  alphabetically  by 
author's  or  editor's  name,  and  a  detailed  subject-index,  referring  from  each  specitic  subject  lo 
authors  of  books  on  that  subject,  so  that  the  advantages  of  both  a  finding  Hit  for  tht  tradi  and 
a  tlass  calalogut  for  tht  use  0/  scheeh  are  combined.

Publishers  Trade -List  Annual 

For  1883  {Eleventh  Year). 

Large  8vo,  over  2000  pages.     (Ready  in  September.) 


The  improvements  in  the  Trade-List  Annual  last  year  have  given  such  general  aatisbction 
that  there  will  be  no  change  in  the  forthcoming  volume  {fltvmth  }reiir)  as  to  the  essential  features, 
which  are : 

I.  The  latest  Catalogues  of  American  Publisher!,  contributed  hj  themselves  and  arranged  alpha- 
beiically  bj  the  names  of  the  publishers. 

3.  A  complete  reprint  of  the  "  Publishers' Weekly  "/><'<"■''/(  Records  of  Books  published  (inclu- 
sive of  9II  the  descriptive  notes)  from  Julj  S,  iSSa,  to  June  30,  i8S3.  In  order  10  facilitate 
reference,  it  will  be  accompanied  by  a  full  index,  by  which  every  book  on  record  can  be 
found,  whether  it  is  looked  for  under  HMthtir,  liili,  at  suijtct. 

3.  The  "  Educational  Catalogue  "  for  1883,  which  is  used  by  the  entire  trade  and  educational 
interests  as  the  most  representative  reference-list  of  School-books. 

The  above  Lists,  all  bound  in  one  volume,  present  in  their  combination  so  convenient  and 
time-saving  a  working-tool  as  to  make  it  indispensable  to  every  one  who  has  any  interest  in  the 
purchase  sud  sale  of  books. 

The  price  will  be  OtM  Dollar  per  copy  ntl,  bound  In  cloth,  or  can  he  Kal  postpaid  for 
One  DoUar  and  a  half  mt,  payable  In  advance.  The  price  of  the  volume  will  be  raised 
a/ltr  day  ef  ftiiHtatien. 

Dtlajftd  or  unpaid  aubacrtpHons  are  not  entitled  to  the  subaeHption 
price  ($1). 

Remittances  should  be  made  by  check,  money-order  on  New  York,  or  registered  letter,  as 
«re  cannot  be  responsible  for  any  losses.     Receipt  for  remittance  will  be  sent  by  return  mail.

by  express  unpaid.

THE  TRIBUNE  vi.  APPLETON. 

**  ]p  apcnoB  whapr7«iHt«lh«cbAfb:tari]faihtriwilhBa 
doisn  bui  iB4ucaTn  Iheir  (aulli,  ud  to  publish  ihen  Is 
ibc  mrU.  dotrrc*  the  tilk  of  ■  iludcnr  of  npuuiiau  of 
BCD,  whjr  gjwuld  am  b  criiie,  who  rcHb  with  tb*  umt  nu- 
Icf otm  nn,  ba  pnfolf  Hi^ed  tba  iliaina  of  tb>  npu' 
Ulieuofbonkil"— FiuDiKC 

Then  what  shall  a  critic  be  styled,  who,  with 
malcTolent  view,  sUuiden  the  repuiatlon  of 
book!  with  DO  other  design  than  to  slander  the 
reputation  of  men  ?  It  is  with  such  a  critic  that  we 
have  to  deal,  asd,  after  previous  warnings,  our 
readers  will  not  be  surprised  to  learn  that  the  said 
critic  is  in  tbeseTriceof  the  N.  Y,  Tribmu.  Time 
was  when  the  Tribmut  was  the  pride  of  American 
jonmalism  :  before  Horace  Greeley  aspired  to 
political  honors.  Time  was  when  the  Tribuni 
was  " disiiognished  as  a  tribunal  of  letters;" 
before  men  of  letters  wen  called  to  motirn  the 
<leath  of  George  Riplej.  We  shall  draw  no  gen- 
eral cDmpariaoni,  bat  con&ne  ourselves  to  a  dis- 
Uocl  case  in  asserting  that  George  Ripley  would 
hare  laid  down  his  pen  forever,  before  be  would 
bav«  allowed  literary  criticism  to  be  dragged 
down  to  the  level  of  the  TrUunt  notice  of  He- 
Master's  "  History  of  the  People  of  the  United 
States."  To  trace  the  malevolent  intent  of  the 
review   We   mnst   first  glance  at  the  attending 


It  is  a  public  secret  that  the  issues  of  the 
houae  of  D.  Applet«a  &  Co.  are  not  reviewed 
by  the  N.  Y.  Tribtint.  From  this  fact  it  may  be 
concluded  either  thai  the  Aiqilelops  do  not  tend 
press  copies  to  the  Trituiu,  or  that  the  Trituiu 
makes  it  a  point  not  to  review  their  books. 
Saddenly  appears  an  elaborate  review  of  an 
Appletou  book,  and  the  question  is  lutarally 
arted.  Why  doea  the  TntmHt  review  this  book 


and  mx  others?  Why  not  others  if  the  pub- 
lishers send  press  copies  of  all,  or  why  just 
this  one  if  the  Triittnt  itself  procured  a  copy 
for  review  ?  Any  journal  that  baa  a  "  literary 
column,"  cannot,  conscientiously,  ignore  all  the 
books  of  a  great  publishing  house.  We  cannot 
think  the  TWJwnf  would  be  guilty  of  the  folly  of 
asserting  that  tor  years  the  Appletons  have  pub- 
lished no  books  worth  reviewing,  whether  good 
or  bad,  except  McMaslet's  "  History."  The 
spirit  of  the  review  inclines  us  to  the  idea  that 
the  Tribuiu  was  quite  ready  to  pay  for  its 
copy  ;  of  this,  however,  the  form  of  the  review 
gives  no  indication,  and  the  general  reader  gets 
the  Impression  that  thn  Tribunt  is  criticising  a 
book  sent  for  review.  The  choice  of  form  is, 
however,  of  no  consequence,  but  the  choice  of 
matter  and  its  use  plainly  show  that  this  review 
was  written  to  compass  an  end.  Let  it  be 
understood  that  the  Publishers'  Wkeklv  does 
not  presume  to  pose  as  a  critic  on  literary 
questions.  The  JWJwfwmay  be  right  or  wrong 
in  its  criticism  of  this  book.  It  has  certainly  the 
right  to  criticise  adversely,  provided  Its  opinion  is 
honest.  But  it  is  not  here  a  question  of  opinion. 

HcMaster's  "  History"  appeared  in  J/anri. 
The  chief  charges  the  Trihum  makes  againstihe 
book  are  :  i.  Imitation  of  Macaulay's  style  \  i, 
"Something  more  than  imitation,"  "actual 
adaptation,"  "a  crime  not  easily  distinguishable 
from  theft ;"  3.  "  Frequent  offences  against  lit- 
erary propriety,  not  to  say  English  grammar." 
This  review  appeared  in  the  Tribune  of  July  i. 
Those  who  have  watched  the  expressions  of  the 
American  press  on  this  important  work  since 
last  March  will  draw  their  own  conclusions. 
The  originality  of  the  criticism  of  the  Tribune, 
particularly  of  Ibe  Macatllay  discovery,  is  placed 
in  Its  proper  light  by  a  passage  below,  from  the 
Critic  of  June  30,  referring  to  a  controversy  that 
had  arisen  between  some  of  our  leading  literary 
journals. 

' '  Because  the  Natian  lailed  to  see,  until  some 
months  after  its  publication,  that  Mr.  John  Bach 
McMastcr's  '  History  of  the  People  of  the 
United  States'  was  written  very  much  in  ihe 
style  of  Macaulay  s  History  of  England,  it  would 
bare  the  world  believe  that  most,  if  not  all  of  the 
reviewers,  were  as  slow  to  make  Ihe  discovery. 
On  June  31,  having  just  awakened  to  this  simi- 
larity, the  A'a/tffMsaid  :

Whether  the  Tribuni,  of  July  i,  was  ignorant 
of  these  discoveries  ;  whether  it  has  any  claim 
10  originality  ;  or  whether  this  review  is  "  more 
than  tmitalion,"  we  shall  not  venture  to  decide^ 
That  a  work  of  such  importance,  which  appeared 
\a  March  a.nA  which  "  was  immediately  noticed 
by  all  the  daily  papcn,  and  all  the  weekly  and

readers  of  the  Tribune  as  tate  as  July  1,  would 
seem  to  be,  to  say  the  least,  poor  journalism. 
It. is  not  only  poar  journalism  ;  it  is  also  vicious 
journalistn.  There  was  method  in  the  Tribuni'i 
madness  [this  is  "  actual  adaptation  "],  for  this 
delay  on  the  part  of  a  neaitpafer  was  so  extraordi-

portunity. Every  one  had  spoken  ;  the  first  en- 
thusiasm was  followed  by  a  dissecting  analysis 
oi  the  most  minute  shortcomings  of  the  author. 
The  Triiiiiu,  then,  had  its  opportunity.  And  this 
is  the  summing  up  of  its  verdict — the  climax 
that  is  placed  at  the  «nd  of  its  elaborate  review, 
like  the  tableau  at  the  end  of  our  sensational 

"  Had  Mr.  McMaster  carried  his  MS.  to  a  pub- 
lishing-house of  greater  reputation  for  judgment 
and  scholarship,  his  imitations  and  adaptaliooi, 
whether  voluntary  or  involuntary,  would  have 
been  detected  and  his  blunders  corrected.  It  is 
safe  to  say  that  no  other  firm  in  the  country  as- 
suming 10  issue  good  literature  would  have  shown 
equal  ignorance,  at  least  of  s  history  so  famous 
as  Macaulay'a.  It  would  be  pleasant  to  stop  here, 
but  we  must  express  our  surprise  and  disappoint- 
ment at  the  frequent  ofiences  against  literary 
propriety,  not  to  say  English  grammar,  into 
which  the  author  has  allowed  himself  to  be  be- 
trayed by  headlong  haste.  ...  It  is  possible 
that  he  may  share  the  shame  of  these  slovenly 
errors  in  an  ambitious  history  with  his  publisher, 
for  a  printing-house  which  is  illiterate  enough 
to  pass  such  glaring  blunders  is  surely  illiterate 
enough  to  make  them." 


This  onslaught  la  so  savage,  the  perversion  ot 
literary  criticism  so  vindictive,  that  onr  only 
comment  is  a  reference  to  our  inlrodnetory. 
It  cannot  even  be  said,  in  mitigaUon,  that  tbe 
TrihuM  has  allowed  itself  "  to  be  betrayed  by 
headlong  haste." 

But  to  show  still  more  the  "  malevolent  view" 
of  tbe  Triiuiu,  we  quote  one  ol  the  few  conces- 
sions Ihat,  in  the  face  of  public  opinion,  perhaps, 
it  thought  fit  to  make  : 

"  His  method  of  treatment  is  different  from 
that  of  his  predecessors.  Considering  the  re- 
organization as  a  purely  popular  achievement, 
he  describes  at  full  length  the  meetings,  discus- 
sions, newspaper  and  pamphlet  controversies, 
propositions  both  wild  and  rational,  opposition 
both  factious  and  uincere,  which  led  at  last  to  the 
convention  and  its  remarkable  work.  It  will 
.probably  be  thought  that  he  undcrestim 


differ  on  this  point,  there  can  be  no  question 
that  he  has  presented  an  able  and  instructive 
sketch  of  the  progress  of  public  opinion  and 
political  enlightenment  at  a  most  critical  epoch 
in  the  experiment  of  popular  government." 

We  shall  not  discuss  the  aatbor'a  right  to  forrn 
his  style  after  any  model  that  suits  his  subject ; 
nor  his  right  to  usr.  without  quoting,  urns  of 
phrase  or  similes  in  common  use ;  nor  bis 
right  to  "  adapt "  or  Elate  facts  that  require  tbe 
use  of  similar  language  ;  but  we  shall  question 
the  new  canon  of  criticism  laid  down  by  ibe 
Tribune :  That  the  authorshould  take  his  literary 
instruction  from  the  publisher ;  or  that  the  pub- 
lisher be  responsible  for  the  literary  style,  tbe 
"imitations  and  adaptations,"  tbe  peculiarities 
of  language,  etc.,  of  an  author.  In  this  c«se  it 
chances  to  be  an  author  "  whose  method  of 
treatment  is  different  from  that  of  hia  predeces- 
sors ;"  who  "  haa  presented  an  able  and  instruc- 
tive sketch  of  the  progress  of  public  opinion  and 
political  enlightenment  ata  most  critical  epoch  In 
the  experiment  of  papular  government."  Inas- 
much as  the  Applelons  have  shown  quite  as  much 
"judgment  and  scholarship"  in  discovering 
the  merits  of  the  work  as  the  Tribune  critic  hte 
displayed  in  discovering  the  demerits,  it  is  not  tbe 
"reputation"  of  the  publisher  that  suffers  from 
the  comparison.  Butwhen,  even  by  the  TW^ar'i 
own  showing,  the  work  possesses  so  much  merit 
as  to  have  a  "  claim  to  our  careful  study  and 
thanks,"  why  should  Ihe  publisher,  who  is 
censured  for  all  Ihe  faults  of  the  author,  have  no 
share  of  the  "  thanks  "  ?

July  I,  we  give  below  the  impressions  of  Char- 
les Dudley  Warner,  a  critic  who — even  tbe 
Tribune  will  admit—has  no  mean  "  reputation 
for   judgment  and  scholarship."     It  does  one

McMASTER'S  HISTORY  OF  THE  PEOPLE 
OF  THE  UNITED  STATES. 

CkmrUt  t>*Mty  WmTrnwr,  in  Ik,  Harlfiri  Ctmrmmt. 

WcTHOCT  a  note  of  warning  or  preparation, 
with  no  blowing  of  trumpets,  without  even  a  scrap 
of  preface,  suddenly  appears  one  of  the  most  im- 
portant,  one  ol  the  most  necessary,  certainly  one 
of  the  most  fascinating  and  entertaining  volumes 
that  the  literature  of  this  country  is  enriched 
with.  This  is  "  A  History  of  the  People  of  the 
United  States,  from  the  Revolulion  to  the  Civil 
War."  by  John  Bach  McMasUr.  The  first  of  the 
five  projected  volumes  is  a  royal  octavo  of  6a3 
pages,  with  a  good  index.  Wc  have  not  yet  pro- 
ceeded far  enough  in  it  to  pronounce  upon  the 
author's  theory  and  philosophy,  and  of  course 
nut  Ear  enough  to  risk  any  judgment  upon  the 
work  as  a  whole,  but  quite  lar  enough  to  speak 
poutively  about  the  excellence  of  its  method,  its 
oppormneness,  and  its  unfailing  charm  asabirak 

The  reader  will  soon  perceive  that  this  is 
Hacanlay's  method  of  writing  history,  that  is, 
ibe  ntodem  method  ;  and  that  we  have  here  what 
is  most  important  to  be  known  in  the  develop- 
ment of  a  people,  namely,  the  life  of  the  people 
themselves.  The  reader  will  inevitably  be  re- 
minded of  Macaulay's  great  chapter  in  which  he 
oude  a  r/suvti  of  the  condition  of  England  at  the 
period  he  began  his  history,  but  he  will  make  the 
comparison  in  a  apirit  of  national  pride  that  a 
writer  has  arisen  capable  of  weaving  our  common 
Hie  into  as  brilliant  and  vivacious  a  narrative  as 
that  which  entranced  the  world  when  Macaulay 
look  up  bis  pen. 

This  new  narrative  judiciously  mingles  social 
with  the  political  history.  It  deals,  of  course, 
with  wars  and  conspiracies,  with  treaties,  with 
Prestdenii  and  politicians,  with  ambitions  and 
■he  rise  of  K)^at  parties ;  but  the  history  of  the 
people  is  the  chief  tbrtne.  "  At  every  stage  of 
Ihe  splendid  progress  which  separates  the  Ameri- 
ca ol  Washington  and  Adams  from  the  America 
in  which  we  live,"  says  the  author.  "  it  shall  be 
my  porpooe  to  describe  the  dress,  the  occupa- 
tions, the  amusements,  Ihe  literary  canons  of  the 
limes  \  to  note  the  changes  of  manners  and 
morals ;  to  trace  the  growth  of  that  humane 
spirit  which  abolished  punlsbmenl  for  debt, 
which  reformed  the  discipline  of  prisons  and  of 
jails,  and  wbich  has,  in  our  own  time,  destroyed 
slavery  and  lessened  the  misery  of  dumb  brutes. 
Nor  shall  it  be  less  my  aim  to  recount  the  mani- 
loU  improvements  which,  in  a  thousand  ways, 
bare  multiplied  the  conveniences  of  life,  and 
ministered  to  the  happiness  of  our  race  ;  to  de- 
scribe the  rise  and  progress  of  that  long  saiies  of 
merhinlcal  inventions  and  discoveries  which  is 


now  the  admiration  of  the  world,  and  oar  just 
pride  and  boast ;  to  tell  how,  under  the  benign 
influence  of  liberty  and  peace,  these  sprang  up, 
in  the  course  of  a  single  century,  a  prosperity 
unparalleled  in  the  annals  of  huroon  affairs  ;  how, 
from  a  state  of  great  poverty  and  feebleness, 
OUT  country  grew  rapidly  to  one  of  opulence  and 
power  ;  how  her  sericulture  and  her  manufactures 
flourished  together  ;  how,  by  a  wise  system  of 
free  education  and  a  free  press,  knowledge  was 
disseminated,  and  the  arts  and  sciences  ad- 
vanced ;  how  the  ingenuity  of  her  people  be- 
came fruitful  of  wonders  far  more  astonishing 
than  any  of  which  the  alchemists  bad  ever 
dreamed." 

This  splendid  programme  is  no  empty  boast ; 
it  is  carried  out  with  a  vrrvt,  in  brilliant  gronp- 
ing  of  details  patiently  and  laboriously  gathered, 
an  admirable  sense  of  proportion,  a  power  of 
generalization  and  a  literary  style  concise,  vigor- 
ous, picturesque,  and  eminently  readable,  which> 
Slace  this  volume,  at  a  bound,  among  the  first' 
Istortes  in  our  language.  We  turn  page  after 
page  in  spell-bound  attention.  Wc  see  pass  ini 
review  the  social  condition  of  our  past  Revolu-' 
tionary  period,  and  the  distracted  reviewer,  whi>> 
marks  passage  after  passage,  ends  in  despair  of 
choosing  anything,  and  wants  to  lay  it  all  beforr 
his  readers.  This  may  seem  extravagant,  yet 
we  are  willing  to  rest  our  opinion  on  the  popular 
verdict  of  a  book  that  everybody  who  reads  will 
soon  be  perusing.  Never  before  has  t>een  nssde 
so  graphic  and  interesting  a  pictufe  *A  our  social 
life,  in  all  its  homely  details,  a  century  ago.

American  Educational  Catalogue 

For  1883  [Fourteenth  Year]. 
COMPILED    BY    G.    D.    T.    ROUSE. 


The  Educational  Catalc^ue  has  been  found  so  valuable  to  ibe  Trade,  not  only  as  a 
finding  liar,  but  for  circulation  among  educalional  cnstomeis,  for  the  purpose  of  aalicliing  orders 
and  saving  at  the  same  time  much  troubletome  correspondence,  that  Its  petmaneot  coniiaaaace 
is  Tceogniied  as  a  necessitj. 

The  cati,1ogue  tbis  jnar  follows  the  alphabetical  STStem  pronounced  b^  the  eiperieoce  of  the 
trade  the  most  satisfactoij  for  practical  use.  It  include*  a  price-list  arranged  alphabetically  bj  ■ 
author's  or  editor's  name,  and  a  detailed  subject-index,  referring  from  each  specific  subject  to 
authors  of  books  on  that  subject,  so  that  the  kdvantagei  .of  both  afindatg  lUtfor  ike  tradt  and 
a  elatt  tatalogut  for  tht  uit  tf  Kkothvn  combined. 

He  other  liit  In  Iha  Buukcl  liTci  ihii  pnclic*!  sUBbinjaisn.     Awl  d«  a>h<r  catalofut  h«  nm  been  Bade  appreii. 

•iibu  of  iIm  otbn  lira  that  wan  oScnd  la  lbs  tnde. 

The  list  has  been  entirely  reset,  to  include  the  new  books  In  thetr  proper  alphabetical  order, 
and  also  to  embody  all  the  corrections  of  prices,  oroissioos,  or  other  errors  in  last  year's  cata- 

Publishers'  prices  are  in  all  cases  given— rffni/,  if  reuU  (in  first  column) ;  "  ml"  If  "  net " 
(in  second  column);  where  a  "mailmg"  percentage  is  specified  by  the  publisher,  the  "  mailing 
price"  is  given  in  the  retail  column  marked  with  an  asterisk.  A  carefully  drawn  eiplanatioD 
In  the  heading,  to  private  buyers,  adapts  (he  catalogue  to  the  use  of  the  retail  trade. 

It  ihouM  b*  uadenlood  thai,  of  the  rtgulic  retail-price  publlihen,  vAf  Ih*  letaU  price  )•  fiTen,  ead  Ike  net  prices 
vAj  of  these  publiihen  ^h  maW  it  ■  practice  to  meii  net-priee  liitf  te  KhBob— in  feet,  nuke  their  net  peieee  their

THE  LARGEST  OLD  BDDK-STDRE  IN  AMEBICA. 

School-Book  Department. 

We  are,  without  exception,  the  largest  retail  dealers 
in  Old  School-Books  in  the  United  States.  We  buy 
large  quantities,  and  our  buyers,  in  order  to  get  the 
books  we  want,  very  often  have  to  take  a  great  many 
others  in  the  lot  that  are  not  salable  here.  These  we 
will  sell  at  low  prices,  as  they  are  of  no  use  to  us,  and, 
while  we  -do  not  say  we  can  supply  all  you  want,  we 
think  it  would  pay  you  to  send  us  a  list,  and  if  we 
have  them  they  will  be  sold  at  a  Bargain.  No  stamp 
is  required  for  an  answer.  We  answer  all  letters 
received.

Applet ons'  Readers 

Were  the  first  school  publicalions  ihat  Tullj'  Tecognized  the  advanced  position  and  new  demands 
of  the  teacher's  piofession,  and  that  embodied  in  a  perfected  Tonn  the 

Modem  Educational  Ideas 

which,  in  the  intellectual  activities  of  the  age.  were  demanding  higher  and  better  expTsssions,  in 
harmonj  with  the  prevailing  spirit  of  progress  and  improvement. 

The  fact  that  in  all  the  series  oi  Readers  that  liave  been  issued  since  Ihe  publication  of  Apple- 
tons'— and  ihe;  are  numerous  in  consequence — ihe  distinctive  features  which  have  made  the 
Appleton  series  the  standard  and 

Preferred  Text-Books  of  Reading  in  this  Country 

have  been  imitated  Is  the  strongest  evidence  of  their  acknowledged  superioritr  and  merit. 

Applitons'  Rsaders  are  not  machloe-made  books,  but  are  the  carefull;  shaped  results  of 
long  and  intelligent!}'  conducted  work  in  the  school-loom  by  the

Library   Journal. 


ejUciat  Ortan  a/  Ik 


The  Librakv  Journal  wa«  esiablifhed  in 
1876  bf  Ihe  co-operative  eflorls  of  the  leading 
librarians  on  both  sides  of  the  Atlantic.  Its 
■chief  object  is  to  be  a  practical  help  lo  the 
every-dayadminislration  of  both  large  and  small 
tibiaries,  and  ro  effect  a  saving  by  enabling 
'library  work  to  be  done  in  the  best  way,  it  the 
lowest  cost.  Tbe  Journal  especially  meets  the 
the  needs  of  the  smaller  libraries.  oBering  them 
ihe  costly  experience  and  practical  advice  of 
'the  largest.  In  refraining  from  doing  Imper- 
fectly what  is  done  so  well  by  the  several  jour- 
nals specially  devoted  10  antiquarian  or  purely 
liisto[iciI  interests,  the  Library  Journal  Is 
-enabled  to  give  iti  chief  attention  to  modern 
biblit^raphy  and  current  literature,  as  repre- 
sented  particularly  in  its  department*  of  "  Bib- 
liography "  (proper),  "  Library  Economv  and 
History,"  "  Anonyms  and  Pseudonyms,  and 
the  "Library  Puichase-Liat." 

Two  important  new  features,  that  will  render 
the  Library  Journal  more  generally  useful, 
have  been  introduced  during  1BS3:

also  prepared  to  buy  both  new,  shelf-worn,  and  second-hand  School- 
Books  for  which  dealers  and  teachers  have  no  further  use.  Quo- 
tations given  on  application.

iniroiluction.  Teachers  purposing  changes  in  their  books  will  find  it  to  their  inieresl  to  com- 
municate with  us  in  advance. 

Roffct's  Thesaurus  of  English  Words  and  Phrases.    A  work  whose  value 

has  been  tested  \ty  nearly  ihiily  years  of  use  in  the  lilerary  world.  For  writers  of  all  kinds, 
even  if  nothing  more  than  lellet-wrilerB,  it  is  second  only  lo  the  dictionary  in  importance.  Its 
arrangement  of  wotdi  i«  at  once  simple  and  systematic,  no  lime  must  be  lost  in  studying  it, 
and  a  full  index  enables  one  lo  find  what  he  wants  ai  a  glance.  Over  aoo  pages  and  30.000

R.  Worthington's  New  Juvenile  Books. 

m.  WorthingUm  begs  to  inform  the  trade  thai  he 
has  now  completed  his  arrangements  {or  the  coming 
season,  and  takes  pleasure  in  directing  their  attention 
to  his  large  and  unrivalled  series  of  holiday  juvenile 
Books.  SIhe  great  feature  of  his  boohs,  and  the  one 
•which  has  made  his  juvenile  publications  so  very  pop- 
ular, is  that  they  are  prepared  with  a  perfect  under- 
standing of  American  children  s  wants.  Sfhey  com- 
bine entertainment  and  instruction,  the  text  is  written 
by  eminent  authors,  and  the  illlustrations  are  selected 
not  only  for  the  richness  and  variety  of  the  designs, 
but  also  for  their  educational  value.  All  his  publica- 
tions are  tastefully  bound,  and  the  board  books  are  put 
up  in  handsome  chromo-lithographic  ccrvers  that  will 
attract  attention  and  apprcrval. 

m.  Worthington  also  begs  to  say  that  he  has  made 
arrangements  with  several  eminent  §nglish  houses 
for  the  sale  of  their  juvenile  publications  in  this  mar- 
ket, and  that  he  can  offer  them  on  as  liberal  terms 
as  his  own. 

Our  new  Catalogue  is  in  the  press  and  will  soon  be  issued.

GiNN,  Heath  &  Co.  have  just  publishe'd 
"  Modem  Spanish  Readings."  by  Prof.  Wm.  J.  . 
Knapp.  The  volume  comprises  besides  the  teat, 
notes,  an  etymological  vocabulary  preceded  by 
an  hi storico- linguistic  introduclioa,  a  bibliog- 
raphy of  early  grammars  and  dictionaries,  and  a 
complete  philological  apparatus.  It  contains 
alio  a  translation  of  a  "  German  Plan  on  Class 
Scientific  Training." 

John  Allyn  has  just  ready  "  A  First  Latin 
Book,"  by  D.  Y.  Comstock.  of  Phillip's  Acad-  : 
emy,  Andover.  The  book  is  designed  to  assist 
in  a  thorough  preparation  for  the  study  of  Casar. 
A  complete  Latin-English  and  English-Latin 
vocabulary  Is  added  conuining  every  nord  used 
in  the  lessons.  Twu  editions  are  published,  one 
with  an  appendix  containing  an  outline  of  ail  the 
esiential  forms  of  infleclion,  desired  by  many 
instructors,  and  the  other  without  the  appendix. 

D.  LoTHKOP  &  Co.  publish  this  week  a  volume 
— the  last — by  the  late  Rev.  Dr.  J.  M.  Manning 
of  the  "  Old  South,"  entitled  "  Not  of  Man,  but 
of  God."  The  volume  is  said  to  comprise  the 
tipest  and  best  fruitage  ot  the  earnest  thought  of 
this  beloved  man  of  God.  They  have  also  just 
ready  "  Side  by  Side,"  a  new  story  by  Pansy 
which  embodies  the  Intemalional  Sunday-school 
lessons  for  the  year  :  and  new  editions  of  "The 
Old  Oaken  Bucket, '  "  Ninety  and  Nine,"  and 
"  Jesus,  Lover  of  my  Soul,"  which  may  now  be 
had  both  in  cloth  and  fringed  bindings. 

Roberts  Bros,  have  just  ready  the  first  of  a 
collection  of  old  favorites  which  they  will  iMtie 
under  the  general  title  o(  Tit  Clatiic  series. 
The  volumes  ready  are  :  "  Lady  of  the  Lake,"

the  three  in  one  volume  ;  "  The  Vicar  of  Wake- 
Geld,"  with  illustrations  by  Mulready  :  "  Robin- 
son Crusoe,"  with  Ulastrations  by  Siothard  ; 

Paul  and   Virginia,"  illusiraied  by  Lalauie  ; 


with  maps  and  portraits  ;  Maria  Edgeworth's 
"Classic  Tales,"  with  a  biographical  slietcb  by 
Grace  A.  Oliver ;  Macaulay's  "  Lays  of  Ancient 
Rome,"  with  a  biographical  sketch  and  illustra^ 
tions :  "  Pilgiiin's  Progress,"  with  all  of  the 
original  Illustrations  in  facsimile  ;  "  Classic 
Heroic  Ballads  ;"  edited  by  the  editor  of  "  Quiet 
Hours;"  "  Classic  Tales,  by  Anna  LeiiiiaBar* 
bauld,  with  a  biographical  sketch  by  Grace  A. 
Oliver!  and  "Classic  Tales,"  by  Ann  and 
Jane  Taylor,  also  with  a  biographical  sketch  by 
Grace  A.  Oliver.  Other  volumes  are  in  prepa- 
ration and  will  be  announced  later  on.  Great 
care  has  been  taken  to  insure  correct  text,  and 
no  pains  have  been  spared  in  the  printing 
(which  is  from  new  plates,)  and  binding.  The 
price  of  each  volume  has  been  fixed  at  %i  retail. 
They  will  issue  early  in  August  an  anonymous 
novel  entitled  "A  Newport  Aquarelle,"  which 
will  describe  life  at  Newport  and  give  sketches 
of  some  of  the  prominent  frequenters  of  that  and 
other  fashionable  summer  lesorti.  They  will 
also  issne  at  the  same  time,  in  the  Famtut 
Wtmtn  series,   "Mary   Lamb,"  by  Adr^^@^

membered as  one  of  the  early  discoverers  of

'■  The  remarkable  success  of  the  iaie  John

aitd  a  pupil  of  Canon  Slubbs.  the  most  paini- 
laking  of  English  historians,  while  Macaulay

INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT. 

At  a  meeting  of  the  Committee  of  the  Interna- 
tional Literary  Association  held  July  17,  over 
which  Blanchard  Jerrold  presided,  the  draft  of 
an  International  Copyright  Convention,  which 
is  to  be  presented  at  the  conference  to  be  held  in 
Berne  in  September  next,  was  read. 

Science  proposes  [hat  the  American  Associa- 
tion for  the  Advancement  of  Science  appoint  a 
committee,  at  its  neit  meeting,  to  draw  up  a 
definite  list  of  such  foreign  technical  journals  of 
mathematics,  physics,  and  the  like  as  do  not 
compete  with  any  enterprise  of  any  publishing 
firm  in  the  United  States,  and  then  urge  upon 
Congress  the  passage  of  a  special  act  putting 
these  journals  on  the  free  list. 

Rf.kerrinu  10  the  attempt  about  to  be  made 
to  obtain  internalional  rupyrighl  between  this 
country  and  Great  Britain,  the  London  ,.4 M/iii/ifm 
remarks:  "The  failure  of  previous  efforts,  is 
largely  due  to  publishers  on  both  sides  of  the 
Atlantic  taking  an  active  pan  in  a  question 
which  is  essentially  one  concerning  the  author, 
and  to  their  thinking  more  of  the  interests  of 
trade  in  books  than  of  the  encouragement  of 
literary   production.     We  understand   that   the 


TAf  Publiifun'  Weekly. 


\No.  6oi]  /ufy  a8.  '83. 


prindpal  autbon  In  the  Uaited  Sutea  have  re-  ! 
■olved  to  act  for  tbeniMlirca,  iadependently  of  i 
publishers,  and  appeal  to  Congress  and  the 
people  to  do  justice  to  authors  as  a  body  irre- 
•pective  of  nationality.  Much  time  and  labor  | 
have  been  expended  in  maluripg  chc  new 
scheme,  of  which  the  programme  wjll  soon  be 
made  public.  An  element  in  favor  of  iis  suc- 
cess is  that  the  most  influential  United  States 
journals  irrespective  of  politici.  are  prepared  to 
advocate  the  granting  of  international  copyright 
in' the  interests  of  authors." 

The  literary  coQTenlion  belveen  France  and 
Germany  which  has  just  been  signed,  consists  oi 
eighteen  clauses.  The  literary  extracts  inserted 
in  school  boolis  and  ordinary  newspaper  articles 
are  not  subject  to  authors'  rights.  t>ui  the  novels 
publish^  in  a  serial  form  and  scientific  articles 
are  not  to  be  reproduced  without  the  special 
permission  of  the  author  or  the  editor,  as  the  case 
may  be.  The  two  coniraccing  governmenls  un- 
dertake to  prevent  as  far  as  possible  the  publi- 
cation and  sale  of  cheap  imitations  of  standard 
works,  and  in  the  event  □(  one  of  them  making 
in  the  future  a  literary  convention  with  a  third 
Government  any  clauses  which  it  may  contain 
more  tavorable  than  those  now  stipulated  shall 
be  applicable  to  the  other.  The  convention  has 
been  signed  for  a  period  of  six  years,  with  the 
reserve  that  either  party  may  give  a  twelve- 
month's notice  of  withdrawal,  failing  which  it 
will  continue  in  force  for  another  six  years.  The 
convention  will  have  a  retrospective  effect  as 
regards  the  works  published  and  referred  to  in 
its  clauses. 

By  article  10  the  authors  of  the  two  countries 
are  spared  all  fortnalities  of  registration  ;  (he 
appearance  of  the  writer's  name  on  the  title-page 
is  to  be  considered  sufficient  proof  of  his  rights 
unless  the  contrary  is  proved.  In  the  case  of 
anonymous  or  pseudonymous  works  the  pub- 
lisher will  be  regarded  as  the  author's  represent- 
ative. The  knotty  point  of  the  right  of  transla- 
tion has  been  solved  by  a  compromise.  The 
necessity  to  print  a  reserve  of  the  right  of  trans- 
lation on  the  book  is  abolished,  so  is  the  regis- 
tration of  translations.  The  author  is  to  retain 
his  right  of  translalions  for  ten  years  instead  of 
the  five  hitherto  allowed.  When  a  work  is 
issued  in  pans  the  ten  years  are  to  be  counted 
from  the  issue  of  the  last  part.  Books  and  act- 
ing plays  are  put  on  the  same  footing  :  and  the 
treaty  will  apply  to  works  already  published. 


THE  MUNRO  FIRE. 
A  fire  occurred  in  the  Munro  Building,  in  Van- 
dewaterSt.,  New  York  City,  on  Sunday  morning, 
July  21,  resulting  in  loss  of  property  to  the 
amount  of  (163,200.  The  Munio  Building,  in 
which  George  Munro  publishes  the  Firisidi 
Comfaman  and  the  Statidr  Libmty,  is  com- 
posed of  three  structures,  running  through 
from  Vandewiler  to  Rose  Street.  In  Vandc- 
waler  Street  the  building  is  of  brick,  eight  stor- 
ies in  height,  and  extends  from  No,  17  to  No.  a;. 
An  extension  connects  this  building  with  the 
large  structure  in  Rose  Street,  which  is  nim 
stories  high,  runs  from  No.  43  to  No,  51  Ros< 
Street,  and  Is  entirely  Rre-proof,  being  furntshei 
with  iron  and  brick  floors,  iron  stairways,  and 
Iron  doors.  The  Vandewater  Street  building  is 
lire-proof,  with  the  exception  of  the  floors,  which 


are  of  wood,  and  it  is  provided  with  iron  shat- 
ters to  the  windows. 

The  Vandewater  Street  side  of  the  Msoro 
Building  is  occupied  on  tbe  eighth  floor  by  the 
Hub  Publishing  Company  of  New  York  and  Mr. 
Munro 's  press-room,  on  the  seventh  floor  by  the 
Finside  Companien  and  the  Seajidi  Idbrary,  OD 
the  sixth  floor  by  Lovejoy.  Son  &  Co.,  electro- 
typers  and  stercoiypers  :  on  the  fifth  Bow  by 
the  Vulcanite  Jewelry  Company  and  A.  Ander- 
Vm.  printer ;  on  tbe  fourth  by  John  Medole  & 
Son.  book  and  job  printers  :  on  the  third  by  E. 
E.  Tomlinson,  pamphlet  binder ;  on  the  second 
by  Henry  Dawson,  printer,  and  on  the  first  by 
tbe  Schuyler  Electric  Light  Company,  tbe  of- 
fices of  [the  New  York  Hilntii, and  the  offices 
factory  of  tbe  New  York  Screw-top  Works. 
On  the  Rose  Street  side  of  tbe  building  Ibe 
ninth  floor  is  used  by  Mr.  Munro  as  a  press- 
room ;  the  eighth  floor  is  occupied  by  Mr.  Mun- 
ro, Sackett,  Wilhelm  &  Betsig.  lithographers, 
who  employ  150  men  and  run  13  presses.  The 
seventh  floor  is  used  as  a  storage-room  by  Mr. 
Munro  ;  the  sixth  is  empty  :  the  fifth  is  occu- 
pied by  Lovejoy  iSr  Son  and  the  Universal  Rub- 
ber Company  ;  Ibe  fourth  by  David  H.  Gilder- 
sleeve  as  a  composing-Toom  and  bindery ; 
the  third  by  Handy  k  Walters,  printers  :  the 
second  by  Stringam  &  Combers,  label  cutters  : 
and  the  first  by  Charles  Eifti  Johnson  &  Co.. 
and  Bingham,  Daley  &  O'Hara,  manufacturers 
of  printers'  composition  rollers. 


THE  ORANGE  JUDD  PUBLISHING  CO. 

The  Orange  Jvdii  Publishing  Co.  wishes 
it  to  be  understood  that  Mr.  Orange  Judd,  wbo 
makes  an  assignment,  has  not.  for  some  time. 
been  connected  with  the  Publishing  Company 
which  bears  his  name,  and  tbat  his  relations  10 
it  have  been,  for  years,  only  nominal,  la  137S 
the  control  and  management  of  the  Company 
passed  into  the  hands  of  his  brother  and  other 
parties.  'The  Rochester  Z>r«nrmf  of  July  zuch. 
contains  the  following:  "  The  failure  last  week 
of  Orange  Judd  in  no  way  aflects  the  credit  or 
standing  of  the  Ameiicaii  Agrieullurisi,  or  of 
tbe  Company  by  which  that  journal  is  published. 
David  W.  Judd,  the  President  of  the  Company, 
writes  a  letter  to  a  friend  in  this  city,  frcm 
which  we  make  the  following  extract :  '  So  far 
from  being  affected  in  tbe  slightest  degree  by 
Orange  Judd's  personal  failure,  this  Company 
to-day  has  little  or  no  liabilities  beyond  curreni 
running  expenses,  has  a  splendid  surplus,  has  in 
two  years'  lime  bought  in  and  wiped  out  one 
fifth  o(  its  entire  capital  slock,  and  is  to-day 
doing  a  larger  and  more  prosperous  business  and 
is  preparing  to  issue  more  books  than  at  any  time 
during  its  long  career  as  a  publishing  house.'  " 

Bradstreet's  Mercantile  Agency,  on  the  16th 
insl.,  sent  out  the  following  notice  to  its  patrons  . 
"The  failure  of  Mr.  Orange  Judd  dues  not.  it 
is  said,  in  anyway  affect  the  standing  of  this 
Company,  as  his  interest  herein  has  beeii  nomi- 
nal only,  for  some  time  past,  and  his  troubles 
are  reported  to  be  the  result  of  entanglements 
arising  from  outside  operations.  The  indebteii- 
ness  of  the  Company,  it  is  claimed,  is  only  for 
running  expenses  more  than  fidly  offset  by  good 
receivables,  and  cash  tti  bank,  and  it  is  said  they 
have  retired  over  one  hundred  thousand  dollars 
of  Che  capital  stock.  Are  still  quoted,  in  high 
credit."  . , , '^lOU'^vIL

THE     PRACTICAL    ADVANTAGES    OF 
TRADE  JOURNALS. 

Fr^m  tkt  BrilUk  ■«/  CoUnial  Prlnlir  tnj  SlalUnir. 

What  commerce  doea  10  stimulate  the  industries 
of  tbe  world,  the  [r>de  journal  doH  for  (he 
trade  it  represents.  Ic  would  be  difGcult  to  name 
a  Irade  of  »aj  importance  which  is  not  represent- 
ed by  its  journal.  The  reason  of  (his  is  clear 
enough  to  those  who  know  how  much  absorbed 
good  workmen  arc  In  their  calling,  and  howanx- 
iuus  the;  are  to  know  what  is  going  on  in  their 
own  immediate  world — what  improvemrnts  and 
developments  are  taking  place  ;  what  is  new, 
and  what  becoming  obsolete.  As  the  daily 
paper  presents  to  the  general  public  reports  of 
noubic  events  in  the  social,  political,  and  com- 
mercial world,  so  (he  trade  journal  culls  all  facts 
bearing  on  the  interests  it  represents,  and  fur- 
nishes ibem  to  its  readers. 

The  importance  of  trade  journalsislndlcated  by  ' 
the  fan  that  they  are  whollj  supported  by  the  | 
trades  that  each  represents,  and  cannot  be  said,  I 
to  any  appreciable  degree,  10  be  read  by  the  out- 
side public.  And  this  condition  of  things  is  quite  , 
natural,  because  every  one  takes  the  greatest  in-  j 
lerest  in  what  most  intimately  concerns  himself, 
and  the  journal  of  greatest  value  to  the  tradesman  . 
is  (bat  which  has  something  instructive  and  en-  j 
cuuraging  (o  sajr  about  what  he  is  doing,  and 
about  what  othen'in  (he  same  (rade  are  doing, 

No(hwith*undtng  tbe  cordial  suppor(  given  to 
Irade  journalism  in  (his  country,  it  may  be  said 
that  it  Is  not  whully  understood  or  appreciated  ! 
by  those  most  interested.  Much  more  could  be 
made  of  it  if  its  readers  were  only  to  consider 
tbcmselves  aa  contributors  and  correspondents. 
There  is  probably  no  member  of  a  trade  or  pro- 
fession who  does  not  wish  (o  know  something  or 
have  some  doubt  removed.  There  are  probably  ' 
few,  whether  employers  or  employed,  who  have 
not  discovered  something  in  their  special  calling  j 
which,  if  known,  would  be  of  practical  value  to  , 
those  of  bis  own  (rade.  Many  of  the  best  work'  | 
men  hesitate  to  take  up  the  pen  10  enlighten  j 
Dtbcn,  though  they  are  willing  to  do  so,  and 
know  that  they  have  ideas  which  would  be  help-  : 
ful  to  their  fellows,  simply  because  they  cannot  < 
tnm  out  their  sentences  in  a  style  which  they  1 
consider  proper  for  a  widely-read  paper.  This  j 
is  largely  due  to  false  modesty,  and  perhaps  : 
through  indulging  it  many  a  brilliant  idea,  and  1 
often  a  valuable  invention  or  suggestion  of  real  ' 
practical  utility,  i*  buried  in  a  man's  bosom,  and  1 
dies  with  bim.  It  is  the  idea,  and  not  the  Ian-  | 
guage  which  clothes  it.  that  is  important.  It  is  . 
the  diamond,  with  its  bright  sparkle,  and  not  the  1 
setting,  however  ricli,  that  attracts  the  eye.  ' 

So  the  tradesman  who  has  developedanything 
new  in  thought  or  in  actual  experiment,  is  in 
duty  bound  to  give  currency  to  it  for  the  benetil 
of  his  fellow- workmen,  where  this  can  be  done  1 
without  too  great  pecuniary  sacrifice.  The  gos- 
pel parable  reproves  him  most  severely  who  | 
wrapped  his  talent  in  a  napkin  and  buried  it. 

If  all  artisans  and  mechanics  were  to  consider  j 
their  own  trade  journals  as  the  storehouse  for  , 
(heir  ideas,  and  a  free  exchange  (or  all  communi-  | 
ca(ions  affecting  (heir  calling  and  interest,  every  . 
journal  of  this  class  would  grow  in  importance 
land  general  interest.  It  would  be  a  means  of 
improving  iheir  condition,  enhancing  their  skill,  j 
stimulating  original  thought,  awakening  a  more  | 
friendly  feeling  among  mechanics,  and  possibly  1 
effecting  a  better  uadersianding  between  em-  1 
ployers  and  employed.  I 


OBITUARY, 

A.  H.  HISKLE. 
Mr.  a,  H.  HtNKLE  died  at  Andover.  Mass., 
July  16th.  At  (he  time  of  his  death,  Mr.  Hinkle 
had  just  completed  a  circuit  of  visits  to  each 
member  of  his  family,  and  was  preparing  to  re- 
turn to  his  home  at  Mt  Auburn,  when  he  was 
stricken  suddenly  with  an  affection  of  (he  heart. 
His  remains  were  brought  la  his  home  and  bur- 
ied July  3lEt  Mr.  Hinkle  was  born  at  Doyles- 
ton.  Pa,.  March  iglh.  1B15,  and  was  therefore 
sixty-eight  at  the  time  of  his  death.  There  are 
few  names  belter  known  among  publishers  than 
the  one  which,  for  several  years,  has  been  with- 
drawn from  the  firm  with  which  it  was  identified. 
For  many  years  Mr.  Hinkle  was  connected  with 
the  large  house  of  W.  B.bmith  &  Co.,  which  has 
changed  its  s(yle  from  time  to  time  to  Sargent, 
Wilson  &  Hinkle,  Wilson,  Hinkle  &  Co,,  and  is 
now  Van  Antwerp,  Bragg  &  Co.,  of  which  Mr. 
Hinkle's  son  is  a  partner.  In  latter  years  Mt. 
Hinkle  has  devoted  himself  to  the  care  of  his 
fortune  and  to  the  work  of  managing  various 
benevolent  institutions  which  owed  their  exist- 
ence to  his  generosity.  The  Lane  Seminary  and 
the  Old  Man's  Home  were  his  favorite  objects  of 
charity,  but  he  gave  time  and  money  liberally  to 
the  many  objects  in  which  bis  church  was  inter- 
ested. Mr.  Hinkle's  face  showed  his  warm  and 
social  nature,  and  no  man  ever  left  a  larger  circle 
of  inUmate  friends.  Mr,  Hinkle  will  be  missed 
in  his  church,  in  his  charities,  and  in  the  cnm- 
munity  where  he  had  made  bis  home.  He  was 
warmhearted  and  generous  in  all  his  dealing* 
but  he  was  also  an  energetic,  practical  business 
man,  and  bis  fellow-citizens  tettilied  to  their  ap- 
preciation of  his  stern  integrity  and  remarkable 
business  capacfty  by  electing  him,  upon  many 
occasions,    to   positions  of    important  financial

Bros.  For  twenty  years  Mr.  Hoskins  has  given

to  the  merit  of  the  wood-engraving  of  Harfrrs 
Menihly.  and  lie  now  reiires  to  his  home  in 
Crawford,  N.  J.,  where  he  has  fitted  up  a  studio 
and  will  in  future  devote  hi'msell  entirely  to  the 
perfecting  of  the  art  of  wood-engraving.

tlon  hitherto  published.  Mr.  Stoddard  writes  an

prepared  by  both  of  the  editors.  The  work  will

a  number  of  the  Examinrr  goes  forth  with  no 
tMyok  reviews  in  iu  literary  columns.  This  is 
not  because  of  tack  of  diligence  on  the  part  of 
any  member  of  the  staff,  but  because  the  snm- 
ner  season  this  year  is  so  unprecedcncly  dull." 

J.  B,  LiPPi.NcoTT  ft  Co.  have  just  issued  a 
ae*  novel   by    Frank    Lee    Benedict,    eniitleU 


I  "The  Price  She  Paid,"  which  is  pronounced

;  Book-Lover's  Enchiridion. " 
I  Hekrv  Holt  ft  Co.  have  just  published  in  the 
Ltisurt  Hour  and  Lrisurt  Montmt  series,  Jean 
Paul  Richier's  "  The  Invisible  Lodge."  one  of 
his  earliest  romances,  and  one  that  helped  largely 
'  in  making  his  reputation.  It  is  peculiarly  interest- 
;  ing  as  containing  a  good  deal  of  Richier's  own 
'  biography.  Translated  from  the  German  by  the 
!  late  Charles  T,  Brooks. 

I  HoL'UHTOM,  Mifflin  &  Co.  announce  that 
;  "The  Autobiography  of  Thurlow  Weed"  will 
\  be  ready  for  subscribers  this  week.  It  makes  a 
I  handsome  octavo  volume  ol  seven  hundred 
pages,  with  steel  portraits.  Over  one  bundled 
;  pages  are  given  to  a  full  account  of  ihe  siory  of 
I  William  Morgan's  abduction  ;  and  a  portrait  on 
'  steel  of  Morgan,  for  the  first  lime  made  public, 
I  will  appear  in  ihe  book. 

James  R.  Osgood  &  Co.  are  to  be  the  Ameri- 
can publishers  of  Mr.  Gosse's  new  volume — 
■  "  Sevcnteenih  Century  Studies''  —  which  has 
I  been  prepared  on  the  novel  plan  of  setecling  a 
!  typical  figure  for  each  decade.  The  ten  worthies 
:  he  has  chosen  to  treat  arc,  in  chronological 
I  order  :  Lodge,  Webster,  Rowlands.  Randolph, 
t  Hcrrick,  Crashaw,  Cowley,  Mrs.  Phillips  (the 
{  "  Matchless  Orinda"),  Elberedge,  and  Otway. 
L  De  Wolfe,  Fiske  &  Ci>,,  Boston,  have  ready 
"  The  New  Excelsior  Dictionary  of  the  English 
I  Language,"  a  handsome  i6mo,  containing  in  ad- 
I  dicion  to  nearly  35,000  words  and  definitions  a 
copious  supplement  of  new  words  and  meanings, 
and  a  large  number  of  very  valuable  tables,  lists. 
etc.  They  will  also  issue  "  Craig's  Dictionary," 
an  elegant  lamo  volume,  containing  ihe  same 
matter  with  a  large  number  of  extra  itlustralions 
and  printed  with  red  lines. 

Ci'FPLEs,  Ufham  &  Co.  announce  as  almost 
ready  ''The  Pilgrim  Republic."  an  historical 
review  of  the  colony  of  New  Plymouth,  by  Hon. 

{ohn  A.  Goodwin,  the  editor  of  Vox  Populi. 
.owell.  It  is  said  to  be  a  complete  history  of 
Ihe  Pilgrims  In  their  English  homes  and  their 
Dutch  I  topping  place,  and  then  in  the  perma- 
nent community  of  New  Plymouth.  Of  this

meet  exiremcly  well  a  popular  need."  The  vol- 
ume will  be  printed  on  heavy  tinted  paper,  in 
old  style  type,  and  will  contain  upward  of  500 
pages. 

JoH.N  WlLBV  ft  Sons  announce  for  immediate

ous steel-plate*  :  "  Electricity  and  Resistance  of

engineering:  Wood's  "Mechanics  of  Fluids" 
and  "  Analytical  Mechanics ;"  and  Ruskin's 
"  Fors  Clavigera,"  containing  "  Lost  Jewels." 
They  have  just  issued  "  The  Art  of  England,"  by

Cruelly ;"  Drinker's  "  Explosive  Compounds 
and  Rock  Drills-,"  and  Do  Bois'  ''Strains In 
Framed  Structures."

VIDENT ASSOCTATION  OF  THE  U.  S. 

A  RBGULAK  montliljr  meeting  of  the  Board  of 
Tnuiees  was  beld  on  Wedoeidaj,  Julj  25,  at  S 
F.M.,  at  Cassell  &  Co.'s,  Pres.  Dillingham  in 
the  chair.  It  was  decided  to  postpone  the  elec- 
tion of  the  Assistant  Vice- Pres  [dents  for  cities 
outside  of  New  York,  and  of  a  Trustee  to  fill  a 
vacancy,  until  the  nejrt  meetlag.  The  Trustees 
are  most  aoxlous  to  irfect  efficient  Assistant 
Vice-Presidents,  men  who  wUl  show  an  active 
interest  in  the  Association  by  noiliinK  to  increase 
its  laemberthlp.  A  list  of  sach  ts  being  made 
up. 

The  Secretary  read  a  petition  from  twenty  of 
the  San  Franciico  trade  to  become  members. 
Circulars  and  application^blanlcs  were  dtiiy  for- 

Oit  motion,  the  following  twenty-five  appti- 
csnis  were  elected  members ; 

C.  W.  auan.  J.  J.  Coonm. 

F.  W.  Arwia.  a  L.  Cmiquti. 

T.  H.  ToppiBi.  S.  S.  Sboaidi. 

H.  C.  Jukint.  J.  W.  UuJuj. 

R.  W.  D«ycr.  D.  MdCtana. 

Edmnd  K*uli.  f.  L.  Hcyla.

Cdnrard  Lanli,  H.  Rotbcnuui. 

Isha  W.  Lffnll.  A.  K.  Adui.

Katie  C  Humy.  J.  H.  RichudKo. 

M.  F.  Lyockn.  J.  w.  H««. 

Chu.  Baiidi. 
This  wilt  make  ihe;grand  total  of  certificates 
issued  513,  giving  thj  Association  a  net  mem- 
bership of  nearly  500.  At  the  present  rale  of 
increase  (sixty-five  haring  been  enrolled  at  two 
meetings),  the  maximum,  1000,  will  be  reached 
possibly  within  a  year,  after  which  no  more 
names  can  bej^added.  The  Trustees  therefore 
urge   upon  Ihc  <tcade  'MUside  of  New  Vork  the 


claims  of  the  Association  and  ask  for  help  in  the 
good  work.  The  organization  is  for  the  entire 
country,  and  being  national  [n  its  character,  they 
want  the  increase  to  represent  other  sections 
than  New  York,  where  recruiting  is  carried  on 
with  great  efficiency.  The  Secretary  will  be 
glad  to  supply  blanks  on  application  by  address- 
ing him  in  care  of  Thomas  Whittaker,  Bible 
House,  New  York. 

C.  T.  Dillingham,  President. 
J.  A.  HoLDEN,  Secretary. 

The  Lileroiy  World.  July  a8,  has  kindly  re- 
ferred to  our  article  on  the  Triituu'i  review  of 
McMaster's  History,  as  follows : 

"The  Publishers"  Weekly  of  July  14  has  a 
very  just  rebuke  of  the  N.  Y.  Tniunit  gratui- 
tious  attempt  to  write  down  Mr.  McMaster's 
■  History  ot  the  People  of  the  United  Sutes,'  on 
the  ground  of  being  an  imitation  of  Macaulay. " 

We  cannot  quietly  accept  this  undeserved 
compliment.  We  took  special  pains  to  guard 
against  any  such  interpretation.  The  Weekly 
did  not  pretend  to  deal  with  the  Tribunis  criti- 
cism of  the  author.  It  meant  [o  deal  only  with 
its  slander  of  the  publisher ;  with  its  doieo 
lines  to  which  the  columns  were  the  introduc- 
tion ;  with  its  personal,  not  its  literary  malice. 

The  Amtrican  hoeksellir  has  promptly  acted 
upon  our  advice  and  has  produced  its  own  Ameri- 
can Educational  Catalogue.  It  does  not  say  it  fol- 
lows ouradvice.  nor  does  it  even  give  the  Weekly 
any  credit  for  certain  appropriations.  Nor  Is  it 
necessary— its  features  bear  such  a  family  like- 
ness that  Ihe  relationship  to  the  Weekly  nil!  be 
recognized  at  a  glance  by  any  of  our  readers. 
But  it  would  seem  to  us  that  a  concern  that  can 
afford,  in  the  service  of  its  own  distributing  in- 
terests, to  give  away  such  a  list,  even  without  Ihe 
support  of  the  publishers,  might,  with  that  sup- 
port, well  aflord  to  pay  for  original  work  instead 
of  borrowing  without  even  saying  "by  your 
leave."  It  is  evidently  easy  and  profitable  to 
do  business  to  Ihe  air  (though  we  have  not  yet 
caught  it):  "  Oh,  why  should  thr  spirit  of  mortal 
be  proud  ?"

day book.     It  will  be  published  this  autumn.

for  a  History  of  China.

under  the  title  of  '■  My  Musical  Life,"  similar 
lo  "  Music  and  Morals."

Queen  Victoria  (or  the  use  of  AiBcrkan  ohit- 
dren.  '

The  LittTary  World.  July  14,  nublishes  a  letter 
from  Miss  Marie  A.  Brown,  01  Stockholm,  ex- 
pressing her  intention  of  presenting  to  American 
readers  in  authoriied  translations  several  Swedish 
novels.  coUections  of  tales,  and  an  illustrated 
Swedish  anthology. 

Prof.  W.  D.  Whitnev,  who,  u  previously 
stated,  ha*  undeitaken  the  chief  editorship  of  the 
American  edition  uf  the  "  Imperial  Dictionary," 
asks  help  from  all  interested  in  the  work.  Prof. 
A.  S.  BoUes  will  have  the  oversight  of  words  in 
commercial  and  financial  usage.  Prof.  Whitney 
desires  "  studenla  of  English,  and  those  familiar 
with  the  vocabularies  of  the  different  sciences  and 
trades,  to  send  us  any  special  critleiim,  any  rare 
or  Dew  word,  or  new  use  of  an  old  word ,  any  pe- 
culiar phrase  or  local  idiom,  any  rare  citation  or 
any  ea|>eciBlly  appropiiaie  citation  illustrating  a 
bmiliarword  which  mayhave  attracted  their  al- 
teotion,  and  which  they  may  be  willing  to  pCTtnll 
the  editor  of  the  dictionary  to  use.  We  especially 
request  that  when  practicable  the  word*  be 
accompanied  by  some  quotation  and  Ihe  name  and 
page  of  the  book  in  which  it  Is  found." 

Mr.  W.  H.  H.  MtTRRAY,  once  pastor  of  the 
Park  Street  Church,  Boston,  and  famoiu  fur  his 
Adirondack  adventure*  and  "  buck  -  board 
wagon"  enterprise,  is  about  to  bring  out  the  first 
two  of  six  volumes  u(  Adirondack  talcs.  At 
^e  same  lime  he  is  finishing  a  book  on  life  in 
Texas,  preparing  a  course  of  philosophical  lect- 
ures for  the  coining  season,  and  getting  ready  to 
begin  the  practice  of  law.  "  These  occupations," 
says  the  K.V.TVi^wr^,"  would  seem  to  be  enough 
and  more  than  rnough  to  keep  any  man  busv, 
but  he  does  not  hesitate  to  assume  them.  He 
helieves  that  no  person  ever  turned  out  more 
■natter  for  publication  In  fifteen  years  than  he 
did  in  Boston.  For  nine  years  every  line  he 
wrote  was  printed,  and  for  eight  years,  in  order 
to  work  sysiemaiically,  he  regulated  his  steep  by 
an  alarm  watch,  and  only  twice  failed  to  heed  its 
warning." 

pKOF.  A.  H.  Keane,  Prof,  of  Hindusuni  In  the 
University  College,  London,  has  prepared  an 
elaborate  work,  "  A  Classification  of  the  Races 
of  Mankind,"  which  he  proposes  to  issue  as  soon 
as  a  sufficent  number  of  subscribers  have  been 
obtained.  In  this  work  be  plans  a  discussion  of 
such  broad  questions  as  the  evolution  of  man,  the 
antiquity  and  specific  unity  of  species,  the  funda- 
mental human  type,  iheir  evolution  and  dis- 
persion, the  peopling  ofthecontinents,  theorigin 
of  articulate  speech,  and  the  morphological  orders 
and  families  of  speech-  There  will  be  an  alpha- 
betical index,  comprising  as  far  as  possible  all 
the  known  races,  tribes,  and  languages  of  each 
main  division,  to  which  is  to  be  added  full  refer- 
ences to  authoriiies,  thus  forming  a  complete 
"  Ethnological  Gaieltecr,"  valtiabte,  no  doubt, 
to  those  even  who  may  take  less  interest  in  ihe 
more  speculative  subjects  treated  in  the  other 
sections  of  the  book.    '
