Leipzig  has  been  for  a  long  time  the  centrcof  the 
publishing  trade  in  Germany  ;  but  Berlin  prtmi- 
ises  to  surpass  it  in  tills  respect.  In  the  year 
188!  more  books  were  published  in  the  imperial 
capital  than  in  Leipiig,  the  respective  numbers 
being  3464and  343a.  In  1881  Leipiig  took  the 
lead  again,  issuinK  sbaS  volumes  against  3345 of 
Berlin.  In  ihe  departments  of  jurisprudence 
and  politics  the  latter  city  published  many  more 
works.  As  10  the  change  in  orthography,  the 
omission  of  the  tetter  A  when  not  pronounced, 
this  innovation  promises  to  become  universal, 
however  awkward  it  appears  to  those  who  have 
been  accustomed  to  the  old  orthography." 


Augucl  4,  '83  [Nc.  60a]. 


Tfu  PuhlitSers'  Wtekiy.

JOURNALISTIC  NOTES. 


An  English  daily  newspaper  has  appeared  in 
Paris,  called  the  Meriting  Nfioi.  It  is  designed 
especially  tor  the  American  colony. 

Affltlons'    Literary   Bullttin,    in    its  Ust  two 

issues,  made  some  good  points  in  discussing  the 

"  Authors  and  Pubtishecs." 


Thb  August  number  of  the  National  Reviea 
cuntains  an  article  on  "  The  Scientific  Novel  and 
Gustave  Flaubert,"  by  Hugh  E.  Egertou. 


will  hereafter  be  published  by  G.  P.  Putnam*. 
Sods.  Tbe  first  iasue  under  the  new  arrange- 
ment will  appear  early  in  September. 

Dr.  Dbems  has  assumed  the  editorship  oE 
a  newly  projected  monthly,  c^Ied  Chriitian 
Thought,  the  initial  number  of  which  has  just 
beeo  published.  It  is  the  organ  of  the  Ameri- 
can Institute  of  Christian  Philosophy. 

A  NEW  review  soon  to  appear  in  Paris,  Is  La 
Revat  da  Mondt  Latin.  It  will  have  five  edi- 
tions printed,  in  Italian,  French,  Spanish,  Por~ 
tuguese.  and  Rutenic.  Its  aim  is  declared  to  be 
to  prepare  for  the  eventual  union  of  tbe  Latin 


Unitid  Stale!  SiatioHtr,  Prinltr,  and  Fancy 
Gaodi  Reperltr  is  the  self-explanatory  title  of  a 
weekly  journal,  the  first  issue  of  which  is  dated 
August  2.  It  is  published  by  W.  E.  Nickerson, 
141  8tb  Street,  New  York,  and  edited  by  P. 
Cunniam.  Though  rather  unhandy  in  size  for 
ready  reference  (tsiKiii  inches)  it  is  handsome- 
ly gotten  up  and  promises  well.  Subscription,  (i 

"Will  New  York  be  tbe  Final  World  Me- 
tropolis?" is  a  question  W.  C.  Conant  will  ask 
in  the  September  Century  and  answer  in  tbe 
affirmative  with  a  curious  array  of  facts  and  ar- 
guments. He  predicts  that  before  another  cen- 
tury passes  New  York's  population  "will  sur- 
pass that  of  London,  and  that  it  will  be  the 
unrivalled  centre  of  finance  and  commerce,  of 
luxury  and  fashion,  of  art  and  literature— the 
heart  and  brain,  in  a  word,  of  the  civilized 
world."  In  the  same  number  will  appear  an- 
other of  Elbridge  Kingsley's  engravings,  made 
directly  on  the  wood  without  previous  drawing.

phy  of  his  father,  the  late  Senator  Matt  Carpen- 
ter.

upon  a  new  story,  entitled  "  Damlano,"  tbe 
scene  of  which  is  laid  alternately  in  Europe  and 
in  Florida. 

Mk.  DonsoN  is  engaged  In  preparing  a  vol- 
ume ol  "  Selections  from  Steele,"  by  the  request 
of  tbe  Delegates  of  the  Clarendon  Press,  besides 
preparing  for  the  GelJtn  Treasury  series  a  selec-

N.  Y.,  but  for  many  years  last  past  residing  in 
Washington,  has  in  preparation  a  work  on  "The 
Authors  and  Newspapers  of  Albany"  from  the 
introduction  of  printing  to  a  recent  date.

reliable  by  literary  e^ttracts  ■■  well  as  by  anec-

illustrations    from    photographs  and  a  railroad

on  "  Obedience."

Mr,  J.  L.  Whitnkv's  paper,  "A  Modern  Pro- 
teus." disctused  a  subject  in  whkh  the  reading 
public  are  as  much  interested  as  the  librart: 
tbemsetve*  ;  the   custom,  growing  in  extent, 
republishing  old  books  under  new  titles,  with 
vi«w  of  securing  a  nen  lease  0/  life  and  of  proRi 
to  the  publisher.     The  paper  was  full  of  inti 
and  wa*  well  received. 

A  MODERN  PkOTELS  [CHANGlJiG   OF  TITLE 

"  Proletis,  the  story  goes,  could  assume  d 
shapes  to  escape  the  inlerriewer.  His  many 
sons  and  daughters,  no  doubt,  inherited  his 
piopea«ti«s  and  passed  Ihcni  on  to  succccdini. 
generations.  When  members  of  ihe  family 
first  came  to  America  is  not  clearly  ascertained — 
perhaps  with  the  great  navigator,  to  whom  they 
may  have  granted  to  be  now  Colombo,  an' 
again  Colon  or  Columbus,  and  to  be  buried  ■ 
the  same  lime  in  two  dtSerent  places,  (or  a  pei 
ptexity  to  the  archeologlst.  However  that  may 
be.  here  Ibey  have  been  found  in  increasing 
numbers  ever  since,  assuming  forms  corporeal 
and  incarpoi'eal.  as  multiform  and  elusive  as  their 
great  original. 

"  Those  of  ui  who  spend  our  days  in  ihe  pur- 
suit   of   pseudonyms  come  at  last   to   feel   that 
everyibing  is  but  a  shadow— that  nothing  c( 
in  single  shape,  and  nothing  is  what  it  appears 
upon  ibe  surface. 

"  Of  pseudonyms,  however,  although  Proteus 
may  well  have  been  the  lather  of  the  race,  it  i 
not  my  intention  here  lo  speak.  If  they  comi 
AS  shadows,  they  may  to-day,  at  least,  so  depart 
Strange  lu  say,  the  foes  which  baffle  the  librarian 
arc  olten  of  his  own  household  and  among  1  ' 
closest  friends,  even  the  books  intrusted  (o  I 
charge.  And  this  is  the  modern  Proteus 
whom  I  invite  your  attention.  Let  us  all  join 
bands  and  farm  a  ring  and  see  if  he  escape 


!  ago 


I  the 


Evening  Peil  ai  New  York,  a  letiei 
Brentano,  the  bookseiler,  in  which  he  complained 
of  the  annoyance  and  losa  to  which  he  and  olh' 
ers  bad  been  subjected,  by  publisher*  giving  ic 
a  book  more  than  one  title.  I  had  already  de- 
tected such  cases,  sad  since  that   time  have 

•  SnabeUtibioeofl^'iL 


watched  somewhat  closely  for  them.  It  may  be 
worth  the  white  10  give  here  a  few  examples 
thai  they  may  serve  10  put  librarians,  and  espe* 
iaily  those  forming  new  libraries,  on  their 
guard,  and  may  perhaps  induce  publishers  to 
give  a  more  careful  attention  to  the  subject. 

"Although  Mr.  Brentano  calls  this  practice  a 
■  modern  fraud '  of  the  publisher,  and  others 
have  denounced  it  equally  unsparingly,  it  is  to 
be  borne  in  mind  that  these  double  publications 
often  occur  throu^  thoughtlessness  rather  than 
from  any  cunningly  devised  scheme  of  wrong. 
Cases  are  frequent,  indeed,  which  would  seem 
to  all  intents  and  purposes  to  be  fraud,  as  where 
a  publisher,  obtaining  the  stereotype  plates  of  a 
book  which  has  had  its  day.  removes  the  certifi- 
cate of  copyright,  and  making  other  changes  to 
conceal  the  identity,  gives  it  a  new  title  and  a 
new  lease  of  life.  Often,  however,  it  would 
seem  to  be  Ihe  result  of  inadvertence,  the  pub- 
lisher, for  instance,  not  realizing  that  when  he 
gives  to  a~  book  a  title  to  his  mind  more 
agreeable  or  appropriate  than  the  original  one 
any  harm  can  come  of  it.  With  the  best  of  mo- 
tives, however,  he  may  well  consider  whether 
the  gain  resultant  can  compensate  for  the  loss 
and  confusion  which  are  likely  to  arise.  If  it 
be  added  that  changes  in  the  titles  of  books  are 
rarely  improvements,  the  argument  is  still 
stronger  against  them.  This  title,  it  may  be 
said,  has  in  many  cases  been  adopted  by  the 
author  himself,  perhaps  because  il  is  a  key  to  the 
character  of  the  book,  or  may  point  its  moral, 
or  be  suggested  by  some  ol  its  incidents.'  For 
good  and  sufficient  reasons  he  has  chosen  il,  and 
why  should  it  be  changed  without  cause  ? 

"  For  like  reasons.  In  translations  of  foreign 
books,  the  title  should  reproduce  the  original 
one  as  closely  as  possible.  This  is  often  not  the 
case,  and  the  title  is  generally  weakened  by  the 
change.  Apart  from  the  question  of  truthfulness, 
what  gain,  for  example,  can  there  be  in  render- 
ing Victor  Hugo's  *L'homme  qui  rit"  as  "By 
Order  of  the  King  '?  Or,  uke  the  case  of  Du 
Boisgobey's  'La  Main  Coupee,' that  is.  'The 
Severed  Hand'^so  called  from  a  sensational  in- 
cident at  the  opening  of  the  story — why  make 
the  title  read  '  The  Lost  Casket '?  By  such 
changes  the  reader  is  often  hopelessly  confused 
as  to  what  an  author  may  or  may  not  liave  writ- 
ten. To  connect,  for  example,  some  of  the 
American  translations  of  Dumas  from  their  titles, 
and,  for  thai  matter,  from  anything  to  be  found 
in  the  text,  with  their  originals,  is  a  difficult,  not 
to  say  an  impossible,  undertaking.  Sometimes 
a  change  of  title  seems  to  be  unavoidable. 
George  Macdonald,  having  given  to  a  book  of 
miscellaneous  matter  the  title  '  UtIs.'  after  it  had 
been  printed,  and  too  late  for  a  change,  was  hor- 
rified to  find  in  the  dictionary  that  one  meaning 
of  the  word  is  'worthless  leavings  or  refuse.' 
Here  one  could  hardly  blame  the  American  pub- 
lisher for  choosing  another  title,  or  even  the 
publisher  of  Charles  Dudley  Warner's  ■  Mum- 
mies and  Moslems,'  the  title  of  which,  apparently 
being  found  gloomy  and  forbidding,  gave  place 
in  a  second  edition  to  one  less  abrupt  and  start- 
ling. The  author  or  the  publisher  occasionally 
Irankly  announces  in  the  preface,  as  did  H.  A. 
Page  (Alexander  H.  Japp.  that  is)  of  his  '  Vers 
de  Societfc,"  that  the  present  issue  il  a  new  edi- 
tion of  a  book  already  published  under  another 
name,  and  some  reason  is  given  for  the  change 
as  in  this  case,  that  Ihe  new  title

The  Publithert-  Weekly.        {Ne.  605)  August  35,  '83. 


"  New  iltks  aiw  ofwo  mere  rearrange  meats  of 
Old  ond,  as  in  J.  Drew  Gay's  '  From  Pall  M&U 
to  the  Punjaub  ;  or,  Wilh  the  Prince  in  India.' 
which  when  reprinted  read  '  The  Prince  of 
Wales  in  India;  or,  From  Pall  Mall  to  the 
Punjaub.'  Here  Ihc  identity  of  tbe  two  would 
at  once  be  detected  but  for  die  tact  that  the  first 
halt  of  a  title  i>  all  that  if  uiually  found  in  pub- 
lishers' aoaouDcements  and  in  catalogues.  When 
Ibe  original  title  is  repeated  as  a  part  of  the 
new  one  (a  device,  it  is  said,  to  protect  thccopy- 
righl)i  it  i*  often  so  concealed  that  lu  detection 
is  difficult.  In  John  Habberton'i  '  Juit  Ooe 
Day,'  which  became  with  a  change  of  publisher, 
■  Mrs,  Maybum's  Twins  :  with  her  Trials  in  the 
Homing,  Afternoon,  and  EvenioR  ot  Just  One 
Day,'  or  in  Hr>.  Fetherstonhangb  s  itory,  'Kil- 
conan.'  which  is  enlarged  to  *  Lit,  Fair,  Fair, 
with  Goldea  Hair;  or,  Kilcorran'— it  ia 
much  to  ask  of  the  librarian  to  know  at  sight  that 
they  are  the  same.  His  time  is  too  valuable 
be  wasted  on  such  small  game.  Besides  he  ca 
not  allow  himself  to  look  with  suspicion  upon 
the  title  of  every  new  book,  or  sink  the  office  of 
librarian  In  that  of  a  detective.  He  must  uke 
the  advertisements  of  publishers  at  their  face 
value,  and  regard  them  as  what  they  claim  " 
be— the  announcements  of "  new  books,"  and  1 
of  mere  ritliauff.'s. 

"  Changes  of  title  are  at  times  so  transparent 
as  to  deceive  no  one,  as  in  the  case  ol  Dr.  Nich- 
ols's ■  From  Whence,  What,  Where  ?  which  be- 
came in  a  later  edition  less  mysterious  as 
•  Whence,  What,  Where  ?  A  View  of  the  Origin, 
Nature  and  Destiny  of  Man,'  and  Aldrich'a 
'Story  of  a  Bad  Boy,'  which  the  Engliih  publish- 
er's announcement  softened  by  adding  *  not  a 
VEtLV  bad  boy.' 

•■  In  all  these  cases,  innocent  or  otherwise,  with 
tbe  exception  of  the  last  mentioned,  and  in  oih' 
ers  which  might  be  added,  the  publisher  and  the 
author  are  the  only  ones  tu  reap  pecuniary  gain. 
To  the  reader,  the  bookseller,  and  the  libra- 
rian there  is  often  a  loss  of  money  as  well  as  of 
temper.  The  reader,  as  Mr,  Breniano  says,  re- 
turns bis  book  as  already  read  under  another 
title  to  the  bookseller,  who,  conscious  that  his 
customer  looks  upon  him  as  stupid  or  a  cheat, 
adds  it  to  bis  slock,  already,  it  may  t>e.  superflu- 
ous from  his  double  purchase.  The  library  and 
its  patrons  are  the  greatest  sufferers,  it  being 
impossible  to  sell  a  book  without  loss  after  it  has 
once  been  labelled  and  sent  out ;  so  it  is  neces- 
sary to  go  without  some  needed  book  which 
might  otherwise  have  been  purchased.  In  large 
public  libraries  and  in  circulating  libraries,  so 
called,  which  buy  several  copies  o(  a  new  book, 
the  loss  is  sometimes  no  slight  one. 

■' 1  ask  your  allenlion  while  I  mention  some 
of  the  books,  for  the  most  part  of  recent 
Issue,  which,  although  under  two  or  more  titles, 
are    believed    to  be   wholly    or  essentially  the 

(Mr.  Whitney  here  read  a  selection  from  some 
350  titles  of  such  books.) 

These  titles,  gathered  as  chance  has  brought 
them,  might  be  indefinitely  increased.  Enough 
has  been  given  to  serve  to  call  attention  to  a 
practice  which  is  believed  to  be  disastrous  to 
good  morals  and  to  good  letters. 

In  commenting  upon  this  paper,  Judge  Cham- 
berlain  said  that  authors  sometimes  found  great 
liif&culty  in  Ending  appropriate  names  for  their 
books.  In  fact,  they  were  not  always  allowed 
to  name  the  creation  of  their  heart  and  mind. 


The  book  was  often  named  by  the  publisher- 
Judge  Chamberlain  mentioned  an  instance  which 
came  under  his  own  personal  knowledge.  Ia 
reading  Lowell's  "Tbe  Cathedral,"  he  could 
'    '         appropriate  reason  for  the  name.     He 

.  ...ten  to  the  author  to  learn  why  tbe  name 

had  been  chosen.  In  reply  he  received  a  note 
saying  that  his  original  title  had  been  "  A  Day 
at  Chanres,"  but  his  publisher  insisted  upon  the 
change. 

Several  plans  were  suggested  to*  remedy  (he 
evil  spoken  of  in  Mr.  Whitney's  paper.  It  was 
proposed  to  draft  a  resolution  of  censure  directed 
toward  the  publishers  who  were  gnilty  of  is- 
suing books  under  more  than  one  title.  It 
was  also  thought  best  to  make  a  black  list  oi' 
those  publishers  who  have  heretofore  offended 
ia  that  particular,  the  list  in  connection  with  the 
resolution  to  be  printed  and  a  copy  sent  to  each 
of  the  publishers.  It  was  further  suggcMed  thai 
the  publishers  be  requested  in  giving  tbe  title  of 
a  translated  book  to  also  give  the  title  in  the 
original  language.  The  list  of  books  which 
have  one  or  more  titles  will  be  made  as  complete 
as  possible  and  published,  additions  to  be  made 
from  time  to  time. 

On  motion  it  was  decided  to  appoint  a  com- 
mittee to  draft  a  resolution  of  censure  as  above. 
The  President  appointed  Messrs.  Whitney  and 
Soule,  of  Boston,  and  Linderfett,  of  Milwaukee, 


An  informal  discussion  of  suggestions  toward 
the  project  of  a  co-operative  general  index  to  the 
standard  English  literature  of  criticism,  bii-^ra- 

Ehy,  history,  and  social  science,  was  opened  by 
Ir.  Poole,  of  Cbicaeo.  That  gentleman  made  a 
short  address  setting  forth  his  views  on  the  sub- 
ject. There  was  no  book  of  the  kind  in  the 
world,  he  Mid.  The  question  might  be  asked. 
How  can  one  person  do  the  work  ?  One  person 
could  not  do  the  work,  said  Mr.  Poole,  but  he 
could  do  his  share.  The  work  must  be  a  work 
u[  co-operation.  He  was  of  opinion  that  the 
members  of  tbe  American  Library  Association 
were  capable  of  carrying  the  work  on.  They 
need  not  go  into  any  expense  on  the  score  of 
publishing.  They  would  want  a  man  at  the

THE   READING   or   THE   YOISG. 

The  paper,  by  Miss  Mary  A.  Bean,  of  the 
Brookline  (Nfass.)  Public  Library,  was  a  report 
on  the  interesting  subject  of  "The  Reading  of 
the  Young."  It  consisted  mainly  of  a  compila- 
tion from  reports  from  libraries  all  over  the 
country,  concerning  their  efforts  and  success  in 
influencing  the  young  in  the  selection  of  books 
to  be  read  by  them  :  it  was  an  admirable  r^sumA 
of  many  capital  and  sensible  reflections  and  sug- 
gestions, and  these  not  from  the  larger  libraries, 
whose  reports  were  presented  last  year,  but 
from  many  Institutions  of  less  note.  The  plan 
adopted  by  Mr.  Poole,  of  Chicago,  will  serve  to 
illustrate  the  work  as  carried  on  in  one  of  the 
leading  libraries  of  the  country,  and  as  a  guide 
to  those  in  charge  of  libraries  everywhere,  how 
to  secure  the  co-operation  of  teachers  An  ap- 
pointment is  made  with  a  teacher  10  bring  bis 
class  to  the  library  on  a  Saturday  morning  at  10 
o'clock.  A  subject  for  ihe  day  is  selected,  usual- 
ly one  which  Che  class  has  been  studying  from 
text-books.  The  standard  books  and  illustrated 
works  ill  Che  library  on  that  subject  are  laid  out 

00  the  table  in  the  Director's  room,  the  teacher 
supervising  the  selection  and  preparing  himself 
to  speak  upon  ii.  and  especially  with  reference 
to  the  books  before  him,  indicating  such  as  are 
of  the  best  authority,  and  describing  Che  best 
method  of  using  them.  A  preliminary  lalk  is 
made  by  the  librarian,  who  welcomes  the  pupils, 
explains  the  purpose  for  which  they  were  in- 
vited, which  is  Ihat  they  may  become  better  ac- 
quainted with  the  library,  its  resources,  and  the 
best  way  of  using  it.  All  these  facilities  of  study 
are  theirs,  and  Ihcy  are  invited  to  make  the 
freest  use  of  them.  Hints  and  stiggesiions  are 
given  how  they  can  best  acquire  the  habiis  of 
investigating  subjects  for  themselves.  The  sub- 
ject of  the  day  is  taken  up  and  followed  out  as 
an  illustration  of  the  method  by  which  a  subject 
may  be  investigated,  the  purpose  being  to  stim- 
ulate the  pupils  to  a  scholarly  love  of  books,  10 
know  the  cools  they  are  to  use  and  how  to  use 
them.  The  teacher  then  makes  his  address, 
after  which  the  pupils  remain  and  examine  the 
books  They  are  later  taken  through  the  library 
and  shown  its  arrangement,  appliances,  and  cat- 
alogues. 

"The  results  arc  already  seen  m  Ihe  many 
pupils  who  frequent  the  reference  tables  and  have 
applied  for  cards  for  drawing  books. 

••  The  success  which  has  actendcd  this  experi- 
ment has  been  chiefly  owing  to  the  real  and  cult- 
ure which  the  teachers  have  thrown  into  il. 
Their  addresses  10  the  pupils  have  been  models 
of  gaud  lasie  and  felicitous  instruction.  If  re- 
peated CO  a  general  audience  they  would  be  a 
healthy  subscilute  for  the  superficial  talk  which 
passes  in  our  day  for  popular  lectures. "

Mr.  Mellen  Chamberlain,  Librarian  of  the 
Boston  Public  Library,  then  made  a  verba!  re- 
port on  fiction  in  public  libraries.  He  proceeded 
lo  discuss  lb«  question  of  fiction  in  public  libra- 
ries generatly.  The  deductions  made  from  his 
experience  01  the  last  five  years  at  the  Boston 
Public  Library,  which  he  considered  a  (air  sam- 
ple of  other  public  libraries,  were  that  there  was 
a  large  falling  off  in  the  circulation  of  what  arc 
known  as  popular  works  of  fiction  ;  that  part  of 
this  falling  of!  was  due  to  matters  of  local  diffi- 
culty, partly  to  the  patronage  accorded  to  cheap 
works,  but  cbicfly  lo  the  fdCt  that  there  was  a 
decided  improvement  in  the  public  taste,  shown 
by  the  growing  demand  for  works  of  a  better 
character.  His  opinion  was  that  the  public  de- 
sired to  read  better  books  and  less  in  number 
than  formerly. 


Mr.  S.  S.  Green,  of  the  Worcester  Public  Libra- 
ry, read  a  paper  showing  the  great  aid  which  the 
library  in  that  city  had  rendered  to  pupils  in 
the  public  schools,  by  means  of  books  loaned  and 
those  consulted  in  the  library  building.  He 
showed  in  a  very  concise  and  interesting  manner 
how  greatly  public  scholars  can  be  assisted  by 
and  from  public  libraries. 

Mr.  W.  F.  Poole,  of  the  Chicago  Public  Li. 
brary,  fullowed  on  the  same  subject.  His  view 
of  the  matter  was  that  public  schools  and  public 
libraries  were  supported  by  public  taxation  for 
the  same  purpose — the  education  of  Ihe  people — 
and  not  for  the  purpose  ol  enabling  them  to 
while  away  an  idle  hour. 

Mr.  W,  E.  Foster  explained  what  hod  been 
'done  in  the  co-operative  work  under  discus- 
sion at  the  Providence  Public  Library,  of  which 
he  has  charge,  and  set  forth  some  of  the  diffi- 
culties in  the  way  of  co-operalion  between  pub- 
lic libraries  and  public  schools,  such,  for  Instance, 
as  the  small  number  of  books  in  such  libraries  as 
the  Providence,  which  had  no  room  or  con- 
venience for  the  accommodation  of  students  of 
the  schools. 

Valuable  remarks  on  the  same  subject  were 
also  made  by  Mr,  C.  W,  Merrill,  of  the  Cincin- 
nati Public  Library,  and  Mr.  K.  A.  Lindcrfelt, 


of  the  Milwaukee  Public  Library.  Both  thtse 
gentlemen  related  some  curious  experiences, 
and  both  agreed  that  the  public  libraries  wne 
daily  becoming  most  important  adjuncts  to  die 
educational  forces  of  the  country.

The  principal  portion  of  the  evening  sessioa

particularly  those  of  the  lime  ol  Columbus  acv 
Cabot  He  explained  the  difficulties  which  ihe 
medieval  discoverers  experienced,  the  errors 
they  labored  under,  and  the  curious  iostrumtm 
they  used  for  ascertaining  the  latitude  and  lon- 
gitude. He  mentioned  incidentaLlly  that  a  Cim- 
bridge  professor  had  obtained  100  logs  of  the 
steamers  of  different  lines  crossing  the  Atlantic, 
and  he  had  found  them  to  vary  in  regard  to  the 
longitudes  taken,  some  being  as  much  as  nine- 
teen miles  from  the  correct  reckoning.  The 
Cunard  steamers  made  the  most  accurate  obser- 
vation, but  it  was  difficult  to  find  exact  longitude 
at  sea  within  two  or  three  miles.  He  then  de- 
scribed a  number  of  curious  maps  prepared  Jui- 
ing  the  sixteenth  century,  on  which  were  repre- 
sented what  was  then  known  or  surmised  of  ibc 
American  continent,  or,  as  it  was  then  called.  1 
part  of  the  coast  of  Asia.  He  slated  that  a  vav 
supposed  to  have  been  drawn  by  Leonardo  da 
Vinci,  now  in  the  Queen's  collection  at  Windsor 
Castle,  England,  was  the  oldest  known   piece  of 

?aper  on  which  the  word  America  is  writies. 
he  date  of  this  map  is  1503-  His  remarks  weie 
very  cleverly  illustrated  on  the  blackboard. 
Tbis  effort  of  Mr.  Winsor  was  unquestionably 
one  of  the  most  entertaining  and  iDStrudire 
features  of  the  meeting,  although  not  strictly  id 
the  line  of  bibliography.

"  That  efficiency  in  library  administration  can 
only  be  obtained  through  application  of  the  car- 
dinal  principles  of  an  enlightened  civil  service, 
c:iclusive  of  all  political  and  personal  influence, 
appointment  for, definitely  ascertained  fitness. 
promotion  tor  merit,  and  retention  during  good 
behavior,  and  that,  in  the  opinion  of  this  associa- 
tion, in  large  public  libraries  subordinate  em- 
ployes should  be  selected  by  competitive  exami- 
nation, followed  by  probationary  terms." 

The  United  Stales  Congress  was  requested  to

document,  as  soon  as  it  is  stitched,  that  Congress 
bas  ordered  to  be  printed  during  the  session." 

A    SUGGESTIVE   EXAMPLE. 

Prtm  til  Ltndtn  BwsJiuUtr,  A  ug.  4. 

A  COMPLAINT  is  made  of  the  terms  on  which 
Mr.  Lewis  Carroll  insists  that  his  books  shall  be 
supplied  to  the  trade.  Our  correspondents  are 
correct  in  their  statement  of  the  case,  thai  ten- 
pence  in  the  shilling  and  net  copies  are  the  terms 
offered.  We  take,  however,  an  entirely  opposite 
view  of  the  matter  to  that  expressed  In  the  letter 
of  "A  Finn  of  London  Booksellers,"  and  shall 
watcb  Ibe  experiment  with  much  interest.  A  few 
books  which  cannot  be  sold  ander  any  circum- 
stance whatever  at  threepence  in  the  shilling 
disconnt.  Is  just  what  is  necessary  to  check  the 
all- pre  vailing  evil ;  and  whether  it  Is  Mr.  Carroll 
or  bis  publishers  who  have  resolved  to  risk  the 
trial,  the  effort  deserves  support  instead  of  con- 
demnation. Booksellers  who  advertise  to  taVe 
threepence  fn  tbe  shilling  off  all  new  books  may 
tor  the  moment  find  themselves  in  an  awkward 
pusition.  but  we  do  not  believe  the  public  will 
hesitate  to  pay  full  price  for  a  book  they  require. 


provided  the  price  is  moderate,  and  there  is  no 
possibility  of  obtaining  It  for  less.  For  a  popu- 
lar book,  such  as  Mr.  Carroll's  forthcoming 
"  Rhyme  and  Reason"  is  certain  to  be,  to  be  sold 
throughout  the  trade  at  full  price  will  be  a  mem- 
urahle  innovation.  Most  booksellers  who  take 
the  trouble  to  think  the  matter  out  will  see  that, 
in  the  long  run.  the  infiuence  of  such  an  example 
will  be  beneficial,  and  casting  aside  the  imme- 
diate question  of  loss  or  gain  by  the  change, 
will  assist  vigorously  in  promoting  what  may 
become  a  new  departure  In  bookselling,  smaller 
nominal  and  larger  real  profits.  The  mass  of 
correspondence  which  recently  appeared  in  these 
coluronson  the  subject  of  underselling  all  tended 
to  confirm  tbe  statement  that  no  bocAcseller  can 
nowadays  get  full  price  for  new  books.  His 
prohi  is  commonly  reduced  to  the  difference  be- 
tween scrip,  at  which  he  sells  to  the  public,  and 
sale,  the  odd  book  and  the  usual  journey  order 
discount.  More  than  one  bookseller  recommend- 
ed the  reduction  bf  discounts  and  the  abolition 
of  the  odd  copy  as  the  most  promising  remedy. 
This  remedy  is  now  offered,  and  it  remains  to 
be  seen  whether  the  reuil  trade  will  allow  the 
opportunity  to  pass. 

OBITUARY. 
RSV.  WILLIAM  M.  BAKSS. 
Rev.  William  M.  Baker,  the  talented  au- 
thor as  well  as  popular  pastor  and  preacher,  died 
at  his  residence  In  South  Boston  on  August  ao. 
Mr.  Baker  was  born  at  Washington,  D.  C, 
where  his  father,  the  Rev.  Daniel  Baker.  D.D., 
was  at  that  time  the  leading  Presbyterian 
preacher,  and  also  the  author  of  several  works 
on  Baptism  and  some  volumes  o(  revival  ser- 
mons. The  son  naturally  imbibed  literary 
tastes  and  a  bent  toward  the  ministry.  He 
graduated  with  high  honors  from  Princeton  Col- 
lege in  1846,  studied  theology  for  one  year  in 
the  Presbyterian  Seminary  at  Princetun,  after 
which  he  read  with  his  father  for  two  years.  He 
was  next  ordained  pastor  of  the  Presbyterian 
church  at  Galveston,  Texas,  and  for  fifteen 
years  remained  in  that  State,  changing  his  parish 
for  one  at  Austin  during  that  time.  His  first 
published  work  was  a  biography  of  his  father 
which  appeared  in  1658,  During. tbe  Civil  War, 
by  putting  forth  great  power  of  talent  and  elo- 
quence, Mr.  Baker  maintained  his  relations  with 
the  Northern  Presbytery  unbroken,  and  did 
much  to  check  the  bitter  spirit  of  his  &ocV.  De- 
votedly attached  to  his  own  section  of  the  coun- 
try, he  nevertheless  supported  the  Union 
throughout  the  long  struggle,  and  at  this  period 
producedhisdescriptive  work  of  fiction,"  Inside  ; 
aChronicle  of  Secession,"  which  appeared  as  A 
serial  in  Narfer's  Weekly  under  the  nam  dt 
plume  of  G.  F.  Harrington.  When  the  war 
ended  his  congregation  was  so  scattered  that  Mr. 
Baker  accepted  a  call  to  the  Second  Presbyterian 
Church  at  Zanesville,  Ohio,  and  in  1873  removed 
to  Newburyport,  Mass.,  and  finally  became 
pastor  of  the  church  In  South  Boston,  from  which 
he  resigned  on  account  of  failing  health  about 
three  years  ago.  During  all  these  years  Mr. 
Baker  contributed  to  the  various  magazines  and 
religious  journals  of  the  country,  and  also  pub- 
lished some  very  successful  stories,  which 
aroused  great  curiosity  about  tbe  unknown  a 


appeared  in  the  Atlantic.     "  The  New  Timothy" 


•o6 


Tki  Puhlisktri'  Wttkly.        \No.  605I  AuguJ  25. ' 


wu  publiahed  in  1870,  and  we  att  recall  tbe 
vivid  intefcst  with  which  we  read  ihe  cxpcTicnces 
of  the  young  pasior  who  was  learning  to  be 
"kll  IhingB  to  all  men."  "Hii  Majesty  My- 
self' attracted  great  attention  in  the  No  Nawu 
series,  and  showed  remarkable  origloalUr  in  its 
discussions  of  religious  t>elief :  it  was  followed 
by  his- best  novel.  "Blessed  Saint  Certainty," 
and  shortly  by  his  last  work  of  fiction,  "  Cotoael 
Dunwoddie,  Millionaire."  Mr.  Baker  suffered 
(or  years  from  a  painful  disease,  and  the 
amount  of  work  he  accomplished  under  these 
conditions  was  a  marve!  to  all  who  had  the  ptiv- 
ilege  of  knowing  him  intimately.

Art-Books.  —  The  London  Arckitict  says : 
"Whatever  may  be  the  condition  of  art  in  this 
country  [England],  it  is  a  remarkable  lact  that 
every  year  there  are  more  books  and  publications 
relating  to  art  issued  in  the  English  language  than 
in  any  other.  Out  of  the  6B6  publications  and 
books  on  an  which  appeared  in  Europe  during 
last  year,  311  were  In  English,  369  in  German 
and  337  in  French.  The  remaining  49  publica- 
tions represent  the  efforts  of  tbe  Italians,  Swedes, 
Danes,  and  Spaniards."

NOTES  IN  SEASON. 

Jahbs  R.  Osgood  &  Co.  have  just  ready  two 
new  and  tasteful  editions  of  Owen  Mernlith's 
ever  popular  "Lucile;"  one,  to  be  known  as 
Uie  TWiriBHf  edition,  is  In  i6nio  size,  printed  with 
red  line  border  and  artistically  illustrated  ;  tbe 
other,  the  Patktt  edition,  is  in  the  shape  of  Ihe 
IaUU  Clattic  series,  contains  thirty  illustrations 
and  Is  elegantly  bound.  Both  editions  are  Irom 
new  electrotype  plates  and  are  printed  in  tbe 
best  style.  At  the  same  time  Messrs.  Osgood 
will  issue  Mr.  James'  favorite  comedy,  "Daisy 
Miller,"  in  convenient  library  style;  also  "A 
Bird's-Eye  View  of  the  Civil  War,"  based  on 
the  best  authorities  of  the  North,  South,  and 
Europe,  and  written  in  ajawinating  manner  by 
Colunel  T.  A.  Dodge,  U.  S.  A.,  whose  former 
work  on  "The  Campaign  of  Chancellorsville" 
is  a  guarantee  of  excellence  and  accuracy. 

Ckarlbs  Scribnek's  Sons  will  pubUsh  on 
the  gth  inst..  Dr.  Gerhard  Ulhom's  work  en- 
titled "  Christian  Charity  in  the  Ancient  Church." 
in  this  work  the  author  shows  that  modern 
charity  is  the  creation  of  Christianity,  by  draw- 
ing out  in  detail  the  difference  between  the  an- 
cient and  the  modern  norld.  On  the  same  day 
they  will  issue  "  The  Philosophical  Basis  of 
Theism,"  by  Prof.  S.  Harris,  of  the  Yaie  Theo- 
logical School,  which  the  author  was  induced  to 
make  public  in  order  to  satUfy  the  wish  that  the 
theologians  would  give  to  the  world  a  more 
definite  statement  of  the  principles  on  which 
they  consider  that  the  defence  of  theology  and 
Christianity  property  rests  as  against  current 
agnostic  and  materialistic  arguments.  Presi- 
dent Hopkins'  volume,  "  Scriptural  Idea  ot 
Man,"  and  a  new  edition  of  "  Best  Thoughts  of 
Plato."  may  also  be  looked  for  at  the  same  time. 
The  latter  volume  will  be  neatly  gotten  up  at  a 
very  much  reduced  price. 

G.  P.  Putnam's  Sons  will  publish  at  once  a 
timely  and  important  volume  in  "  A  History  of 
the  Northern  Pacific  Railroad,  from  the  In- 
ception of  the  Enterprise  in  1834,  to  the  OpeoinK 
of  the  Line  from  Lake  Superior  and  the  Missis- 
sippi Valley  to  Puget  Sound,  on  the  Pacific 
Coast,  in  18S3,"  by  Eugene  V.  Smalley.  The 
volume  will  have  maps,  and  forty-eight  full-page 
engravings  from  original  sbetcbes.  Other  booKS 
of  interest  to  be  expected  this  month  are  a  ^tw 
Ctnlrnnial  edition  of  Irvlng's  "  Life  of  Wash- 
ington" (omitting  the  documentary  material), 
printed  from  new  plates  in  a  handsome  quarto, 
with  illuBl rations,  bound  In  paper  and  in  cloth  : 


Leigh  Hunt,  Lamb,  De  Quincy,  Landor,  Thack- 
eray, Emerson,  Ruskin,  Carlyle,  Lowell,  and 
other  masters,  prepared  with  reference  to  pre- 
senting as  well  specimens  of  the  method  of 
thought  and  the  literary  style  of  their  several 
writers,  as  also  for  the  purpose  of  putting  into 
convenient  shape  for  direct  comparison,  the 
treatment  given  by  such  writers  to  similar  snb- 
jects  ;  "  Tbe  American  Girl's  Home-book  of 
Work  and  Play,"  by  Helen  Campbell,  a  volume 
giving  suggestions  and  instructions  for  In-door 
and  oul-door  amusements,  and  (or  occupations 
for  play  or  for  profit ;  and  "  Work  for  Women," 
a  volume  of  hints  to  aid  women  In  the  selec- 
tion of  a  vocation  In  life,  and  detcrlUng  the 
several  occupations  adapted  to  the  capat^^fU^^

KOTES  ON  AUTHORS. 

Hr.  Hawbis,  author  of  "  Music  and  Morals," 
has  just  completed  a  new  work,  which  be  en- 
titles "  My  Musical  Life,"  and  which  readers  of 
his  former  book  will  look  forward  to  with  much 
Interest.  Mr.  Haweis,  we  understand,  is  at 
present  compiling  a  Chaucer  hirlhday-hook  for 
children. 

Ma.  Eduund  Gosse,  in  bis  volume  entitled 
*' Seventeeth  Century  Studies,"  an  American 
edition  of  which  will  be  published  by  James  R. 
Osgood  ft  Co.,  discusses  ten  worthies,  each  se- 
lected as  a  typical  figure  of  his  decade.  Tbe 
subjects  of  the  essays  are  in  chronological  order, 
as  follows :  Lodge,  Webster,  Rowlands,  Ran- 
dolph, Herrick,  Crashaw,  Cowley,  Mrs.  Philippe, 
Etheridge,  and  Otway, 

Mr.  Gborur  W.  EDKI.MAN,  author  of  tbe 
"Bullion  Dealer's  Guide,"  has  published  a 
useful  compilation  in  the  form  of  a  Urge  card, 
containing  the  "Facts  about  Silver  in  a  Nut- 
shell." It  gives  tbe  gold  value  of  silver  in 
ounces.  9-10  fine,  pure,  in  subsidiary  coins,  and 
In  standard  and  trade  dollar*,  at  London  prices, 
from  ifid.  up  to  63  i-iCt/.,  together  with  a  num- 
ber of  facts  and  dates  which  It  Is  very  ct 
to  have  in  such  a  form.

dream.

dress 00  surgery  delivered  by  Dr.  Reginald  Har- 

Hereafter  Tht  Critic  will  be  published  week- 
ly from  the  middle  of  September  10  the  middle 
of  June,  and  fortnightly  from  the  middle  of 
June  to  the  middle  of  September.  The  pub- 
lishers announce  thai  as  the  new  arrangement 
reduces  the  number  of  copies  to  be  published 
during  the  year  from  fifty-two  to  (orly-five,  a 
corresponding  reduction  will  be  made  in  the 
subscription  price,  which  wilt  be  $3  per  annum, 
instead  of  ts-jo  as  heretofore. 

Thb  scene  of  Mr.  George  W.  Cable's  new 
novel,  "Dr.  Sevier,"  is  laid  in  New  Orleans,  the 
lime  being  the  eve  of  Ihe  late  Civil  War.  a  glimpse 
of  the  beginning  of  which  is  said  10  be  given  in 
the  closing  chapters.  Besides  the  Creole  type*, 
of  which  Mr.  Cable  is  known  as  the  originator 
in  fiction,  this  story  is  said  to  present  a  variety 
of  characters  of  dinerent  nationality,  drawn  with 
Mr.  Cable's  well-kuown  insight  and  sense  of 
humor.  The  novel  will  be  an  important  feature 
of  the  new  volume  of  Tht  Ctntury,  the  first  chap- 
ters appearing  in  the  November  number.

Square  Library.  The  volume  is  handy  in  shape, 
bound  in  a  distinctive  blue  paper  cover,  and  will 
no  doubt  become  very  popular.  The  first  vol- 
ume of  the  new  series  is  George  Eliot's  well- 
known   story,    ''  Silas   Mamer,  the   Weaver  of 

"For  some  time,"  says  the  K.  Y.  Times, 
"  Miss  Louisa  M.  Alcott  has  been  at  work  upon 
a  new  stoiy  for  boys  and  girU.  It  is  a  sequel  to 
'Little  Men,' and  is  to  be  called  'Joe's  Boys, 
and  How  They  Turned  Out.'  Miss  Alcott  hoped 
to  have  the  book  finished  for  the  fall,  but  owing 
to  the  illness  of  her  father,  she  has  been  obliged 
to  put  off  its  completion  Indefinitely." 

Roberts  Brothers  have  just  received  from 
Edwin  Arnold  the  original  manuscript  of  his  new 
"  Indian  Idyls,"  nith  the  request  that  the  book 
should  be  promptly  put  in  type  and  the  proof 
sent  to  him  for  revision  previous  lo  his  visit  to 
the  East  The  volume  will  be  about  the  same 
siie  as  "  The  Light  of  Asia,"  and  is  10  be  pub- 
lished simultaneously  in  England  and  in  America. 
CuppLF-s,  Upha-v  &  Co.  will  publish  on  the 
opening  day  of  the  forthcoming  Institute  Fair 
at  Boston,  a  superb  catalogue  of  the  fine  arts 
department.  Its  general  excellence  and  the  il- 
lustrations, mostly  etchings,  which  It  contains, 
make  it  in  every  sense  a  unlqucand  most  worthy 
publication,  a  credit  to  all  concerned  in  its  com- 
position. 

White,  Stokes  4  Allen,  in  calling  attention

havi-  had  any  dealings  with  the  book-publishing

"  Those  Pretty  St.  George  Giri^,"  the  new

for  the  complete  set. 

R.  WoRTHiNGTON  will  publish  on  the  ttb 
inst.,  "  Our  Northern  and  Eastern  Birds,"  coo-

song,  time  of  breeding,  and  descriptions  of  their

The  sixth  annual  meeting  of  the  Library  As- 
sociation of  the  United  Kingdom  will  be  held 
this  year  at  Liverpool  on  Tuesday,  September 
II,  and  the  three  following  days.  Itis proposed 
to  pay  special  attention  to  the  subject  of  class!- 
^cation.  Papers  are  promised  on  several  ques- 
tions relating  to  library  adminislrdtion  and 
bibliography  ;  and  reports  will  be  presented  on 
cataloguing  and  on  size  notation.  The  local 
committee  propose  to  bold  an  exhibition  of  ob- 
jects and  appliances  Ulusiiatlng  the  working  ar- 
rangements, etc.,  of  libraries  in  all  departments. 

"THKLiBRAaY  Hand-Book,"  we  learn  from 
the  London  Acadimy,  "  is  the  title  of  a  work 
upon  which  Messrs.  H.  R.  Tedder  and  C.  E. 
Thomas  have  been  for  a  long  time  occupied, 
and  which  they  hope  shortly  to  publish.  It 
will  contain  an  account  of  all  tbe  libraries  of 
Great  Britain  and  Ireland  of  any  imporUnce, 
as  well  as  the  chief  of  those  of  other  countries, 
with  an  introduction  devoted  to  library  manage* 
mcnt,  inchidlag  directions  for  arrauging  and 
cataloguing  books,  library  -  buildings  and  ap* 
pliances,  binding,  etc.  The  price  will  be  moder- 
ate, as  it  is  Intended  to  make  the  '  Library  Hand- 
Book'  of  use  to  all  persons  who  either  own 
books  or  frequent  libraries."

FROM  THE   PREFACE. 

Tht  following  story  hat  bten  taking  form  in  my  mind  for  several  years,  and  at 
•t  /  have  been  able  to  write  it  out.  With  a  regret  akin  to  sadness,  I  like  my  leave, 
s  August  Jay,  of  people  who  have  beeotne  very  real  to  tne,  whesf  joys  and  sorrows  / 
ve  made  my  own.  Although  a  Northern  man,  I  think  my  Southern  readers  will  feel 
it  I  have  sought  to  do  justice  to  their  motives.  At  this  distance  from  the  late  Civil 
'ar,  it  is  time  that  passion  and  prejudice  sank  beloiv  the  horizon,  and  among  the  sur- 
ing  soldiers  who  were  arrayed  against  each  other  I  think  they  have  practically  dis- 
leared.     Stern  and  prolonged  convict  taught  mutual  respect. 

I  hope  endeavored  to  portray  the  battle  of  Bull  Hun  as  it  could  appear  to  a  civilian 
etator  :  to  give  a  suggestive  picture  and  not  a  general  description.  The  folloiinng 
r-scenes  are  imaginary,  and  colored  by  personal  reminiscence.  I  was  in  the  service 
■rly  four  years,  two  of  which  were  spent  with  the  cavalry. 

The  title  of  the  story  will  naturally  lead  the  reader  to  expect  that  deep  shadoivs 
t  upon  mat^  of  its  pages,  I  know  it  is  scarcely  the  fashion  of  the  present  time  to 
■tray  men  and  women  who  feel  very  deeply  about  anything,  but  there  certainly  was 
t  feeling  at  the  time  of  which  I  write,  as,  in  truth,  there  is  to-day.  The  heart  of 
lanity  is  like  the  ocean.      There  are  depths  to  be  stirred  when  the  causes  are  adequate.

DODD,  Mead  &  Co.  announce  that  E.  P. 
Ro«'s  new  novel.  "His  Sombre  Rivals,"  is 
completed,  and  that  Ihe  first  edition  of  35,000 
copies  wilt  be  ready  atroui  September  ij.  The 
story  deala  with  our  Civil  War,  and  bas  therefore 
more  than  ordinary  interest  for  a  very  large  clan 
of  readers.  The  author,  who  was  in  active  service 
during  the  war.  has  endeavored  lo  be  impartial 
throughout  the  whole  narrative.  In  referring  to 
his  sentiments  in  the  matter,  in  his  preface,  be 
says:  "Although  a  Northern  man,  I  think  roy 
Soulhem  readers  will  feel  that  I  have  sought  to 
do  justice  to  their  motives." 

Houghton.  Mifflin  &  Co.  will  publish  00 
the  15th  the  first  of  their  holiday  books  for  this 
season,  "Twenty  Poems  from  Longfellow," 
with  about  50  illustrations  and  a  portrait,  all  by 
the  poet's  son,  Ernest  Longfellow,  The  book 
will  be  in  the  usual  form  of  holiday  volnmei, 
octavo,  very  handsomely  printed  and  bound ; 
and  the  conjunction  of  the  father  and  son  in  such 
1  volnme  will  lend  to  it  a  peculiar  interest.  At 
he  same  time  will  appear  the  Bodley  book  for 
883,  entitled  "The  English  Bodley  Family," 
describing  intercalinK  and  piclures<]ue  scenes 
and  incidents  in  English  history  in  tbe  attractive 
style  of  whicb  Mr,  Scudder  is  master,  and  illus- 
trated with  numernus  pictures  ;  a  dramatiiatioa 
of  Longfellow's  "  Courtship  of  Miles  Standish" 
for  tbe  use  of  schools  and  lor  private  theatricals ; 
a  new  edition  of  Richardson's  "Primer  of 
American  Literature."  with  portraits  of  twelve 
distinguished  American  authors ;  and  a  little 
book  by  Mrs.  James  T.  Fields,  entitled  "How 
to  Help  the  Poor."  containinK  practical  sugges- 
tions of  great  value,  the  results  of  her  observa- 
tion and  experience  during  many  years  of 
philanthropic  work.  On  the  3ld  they  will  pub- 
lish the  long-announced  Jii-mriitU  edition  of 
Shakespeare,  which  Richard  Grant  White  baa 
edited  in  3  crown  octavo  volumes,  the  first  con- 
taining tbe  Comedies,  the  second  the  Tragedies 
and  Poems,  and  the  third  the  Historical  Plays. 
A  short  introduction  is  given  to  each  play,  and 
explanatory  foot-notes,  brief  and  concise,  are 
placed  at  tbe  bottom  of  the  pages.  The  type  is 
large,  and  the  edition  can  hardlv  (ail  to  be  the 
most  popular,  as  it  certainly  is  nearly  if  not 
quite  the  best  popular  edition  of  Shakespeare 
ever  published.  With  this  will  be  published  the 
second  series  of  "  Essays"  and  "  Representative 
"  ,"  forming  the  third  and  fourth  volumes  of 
lew  Rivi'siHt  edition  of  Emerson's  com- 
works.  "Tbe  Voyage  of  the  Jeaonetle," 
which  gives  an  account  of  that  ill-f^ted  expedi- 
tion in  which  so  much  heroism  and  endurance 
were  displayed,  probably  one  of  tbe  most  in- 
teresting books  of  exploration  ever  written  ;  a 
book  on  "  Pledges,"  by  Leonard  A.  Jones,  author 
if  several  works  un  mortgages,  railroad  securi- 
tes.  and  related  subjects  ;  and  a  "  Diplomatic 
History  of  the  War  for  the  Union."  forming  the 
fifth  volume  of  Ihe  works  of  William  H,  Sew- 

,  and  comprising,  in  addition  to  a  Memoir  by 
editor,  George  E.  Baker,  the  Journal  which 

,  Seward  kept,  when  Secretary  of  Slate,  of 
progress,  and   incidents  of  the  Civil  War, 
several  important  diplomatic  papers,  a  number 
of  speeches  made    before   his   retirement  from 
office,  and   some   alter  that   period,  the   whole.  I 

completing  one  of  tbe  most  important  series  oI>OQIL 
political  works  ever  prodiiced  in  Ameitc*.  '^

fARPKK  A  Brotheks  hare  just  ready  the  im-

ich  Dr.  Philip  ScbafT,  the  president  of  ihe 
lerican  Coitiixilttee  on  the  Revision  of  the

grown  ont  of  Dr.  SchaS's  stodlea  in  con-

on  every  subject  relating  lo  the  history  and  the

DoDD,  Mead  &  Co.  announce  that  E.  P. 
Roe's  new  novel,  "His  Sombre  Rivals,"  is 
completed,  and  that  the  fir^t  edition  of  3S,ooo 
copies  will  be  ready  about  September  15.  The 
story  deals  with  our  Civil  War,  and  has  therefore 
more  than  ordinary  interest  for  a  very  large  class 
of  readers.  The  author,  who  was  in  active  service 
during  the  war,  has  endeavored  to  be  impartial 
throughout  the  whole  narrative.  In  referring  to 
bis  sentiments  in  the  matter,  in  bis  preface,  be 
says  :  "  Although  a  Northern  man,  I  think  my 
Sonthem  readers  will  feel  that  1  have  sought  to 
do  justice  to  their  motives." 

liouGHTON,  Mifflin  &  Co.  will  publish  on 
the  ijth  the  first  of  their  holiday  books  for  this 
season,  "Twenty  Poems  from  Longfellow," 
with  about  50  illustrations  and  a  portrait,  all  by 
the  poet's  son,  Ernest  LonRtelliiw.  The  book 
will  be  in  tbe  usual  form  of  holiday  volumes, 
octavo,  very  handsomely  printed  and  bound ; 
and  the  conjunction  o(  the  father  and  son  in  such 
a  volume  will  lend  to  it  a  peculiar  interest.  At 
the  same  time  will  appear  the  Bodley  book  for 
1883,  entitled  "The  English  Bodley  Family," 
describing  interesting  and  picturesque  scenes 
and  incidents  io  English  history  in  the  attractive 
style  of  which  Mr.  Scudder  is  master,  and  illus- 
trated with  numerous  pictures  ;  a  dramalizalloQ 
of  Longfellow's  "  Courtship  of  Miles  Standish" 
for  the  use  of  schools  and  for  private  theatricals  : 
a  new  edition  of  Richardson's  "Primer  of 
American  Literature,"  with  portraits  of  twelve 
distinguished  American  authors  :  and  a  little 
book  by  Mrs.  James  T.  Fields,  entitled  "  How 
to  Help  the  Poor,"  containing  practical  sugges- 
tions of  great  value,  the  results  of  her  ot>serva- 
tion  and    experience    during    many    years    of 

fihilanthropic  work.  Go  the  33d  they  will  pnb- 
lih  the  long-announced  Riveraidt  edition  of 
Shakeapeare,  which  Richard  Grant  White  ha* 
edited  in  3  crown  octavo  volumes,  the  first  con- 
taining the  Cumedies,  the  second  the  Tragedies 
and  Poems,  and  the  third  the  Historical  Plays. 
A  short  introduction  is  given  to  each  play,  and 
explanatory  foot-notes,  brief  and  concise,  ai« 
placed  at  the  bottom  of  the  pages.  The  type  is 
large,  and  the  edition  can  hardly  fail  to  be  the 
most  popular,  as  it  certainly  Is  nearly  if  not 
quite  the  best  popular  edition  of  Shakespeare 
ever  published.  With  this  will  be  published  the 
second  scries  of "  Essays"  and  "  Representative 
Men,"  forming  the  third  and  fourth  volumes  of 
the  new  Rivirsidt  edition  of  Emerson's  com- 
plete works.  "  The  Voyage  of  the  Jeanaetie," 
which  gives  an  account  of  that  ill-f.4ted  expedi- 
tion in  which  so  mucb  heroism  and  endurance 
were  displayed,  probably  one  of  the  most  in- 
teresting books  of  exploration  ever  written  ;  « 
book  on"  Pledges."by  Leonard  A.  Jones,  author 
of  several  works  on  mortgages,  railroad  sectiri- 
ties,  and  related  subjects;  and  a  "Diplomatic 
History  of  the  War  for  the  Union."  forming  the 
filth  volume  of  the  works  of  William  H.  Sew- 
ard, and  comprising,  in  addition  to  ■  Memoir  by 
the  editor,  George  E.  Baker,  tbe  Jonmal  which 
Mr.  Seward  kept,  when  Secretary  of  State,  of 
the  progress,  and  incidents  of  the  Civil  War, 
several  important  diplomatic  papers,  a  number 
of  speeches  made  before  his  retirement  from  . 
office,  and  some  after  that  period,  the  *h^lc(^T|p

Edward  Medu,  for  Philnddphia,  Fa. 
The  Association  Is  In  excellent  condition,  no 
death  having  occurred  in  nearly  sixteen  months, 
while  Its  membership  is  being  rapidly  increased. 
Ninety-one  new  names  were  added  during  the 
summer  moniba.

The  analogy  of  patents  is  cited  in  renewal 
cases,  and  especially  the  following  ar([timent : 
"  No  (natter  how  much  any  one  assignee,  or  all 
the  assignees,  may  desire  the  extension  of  a 
patent — no  matter  Ibo  the  whole  of  the  original 
franchise  may  have  been  assigned  to  one  per- 
son, and  he  may  desire  an  extension — no  matter 
ibo  assignee  of  a  part.  01  even  of  the  whole,  make 
application  in  writing,  and  set  forth  the  grounds 
thereof — no  matter  what  grounds  they  set  forth 
— no  matter  tho  among  such  grounds,  they  sho 
that  Ihry  have  furnish^  all  the  capital  which  has 
b«cn  used  in  perfecting  tbe  discovery  and  bring- 
ing it  into  use— no  matter  tho  they  prove,  by 
indisputable  evidence,  that  they  hav  been  not 
merely  just,  but  even  generiios  to  the  inventor, 
and  liberal  to  the  public,  and  hav  been  im- 
poverished by  their  generosity,  while  the  in- 
ventor has  become  opulent,  and  the  country  has 
grown  in  wealth  and  prosperity  by  the  expendi- 
tarc  of  their  capital  in  bringing  the  Invention 
into  use — no  matter  tho  they  sho  all  this,  and 
sho  besides,  that  the  patentee,  altho  indemnified 
by  them,  refuses  to  apply  on  their  behalf  and  to 
set  forth  his  accounts  on  oath,  yet  the  assignees 
must  be  dismissed  without  a  hearing.  It  thus 
appears,  that  not  only  is  the  patentee  a  neces- 
sary agent  in  obtaining  the  extension,  but  that 
tbe  policy  of  the  law  is  to  encourage  genius  by 
furnishing  additional  stimulants  to  inventors, 
tt^t  10  assignees,  or  even  patrons  of  Inventors, 
and  the  law  can  hav  effect  only  at  the  solicitation 
of  Ihe  invmlvr,  in  consideration  of  Aii  tjcclusiv 
merits  and  for  kit  tienefit  and  his  hmtfil  aleni. 
.  .  .  Why  this  careful  consideration  of  the 
inventor?  And  why  this  studied  neglect  of  the 
assignee  ?  Simply  because  the  constitution  dis- 
claims all  care  of  assignees,  while  it  positivly 
enjoins  tbe  protection  of  inventors." 
(Wilson  V.  Rousseau,  i  Blatchford.  25.) 

Spectacviar  plays  not  subject  of  copyright. 
"  AH  the  witnesses  agree  that  the  play  has  no 
originality,  and  that  tt  consists  almost  wholly  of 
mere  scenic  effects  taken  from  well  known 
dramas  and  opera*.  .  .  The  dialog  is  very 
scant,  and  apfteais  in  the  light  of  a  new  acces- 
sory.—a  piece  of  word  machinery  tacked  on  the 
bailet  and  tableaux.  .  .  To  call  such  a  spec- 
tacle a  '  dramatic  composition  '  is  an  abuse  of 
language."  (Oeady,  d.  j..Fcb.  1S67,  "  Magulre 
V.  Hartioetti,"  i  Abbott.  364.)  Injunction 
denied. 

Stahk.  a  performance  on  the  stage  is  not 
surfa  a  publication  as  wil  destroy  the  exdusiv 
common-taw  right  of  the  author  and  bis  assigns 
lu  a  dramatical  or  lyrical  composition,  tho  the 
compoaerbtn  alien.  ("Thomas  t.  Lincoln," 
circuit,  Jan.,  1883.  Lowell,  c  j.— 14  Fed.   Rep. 

Title,  [see  Trade-Marli]  aside  from  contents. 
aol  subject  of  copyright.  "  The  right  secured 
by  ttac  act,  however,  is  the  property  in    the 


literary  composition, — the  product  of  the  mind 
and  genius  of  the  author,  and  not  in  the  name  or 
title  given  to  it.  The  title  does  not  necessarily 
involve  any  literary  composition  ;  it  ma^  not  be, 
and  certainly  the  statute  does  not  require  that  It 
should  be,  the  product  of  the  author's  mind. 
.  .  It  is  a  mere  appendage,  which  only  identi- 
fies, and  frequently  does  not  in  any  way  de- 
scribe, the  literary  composition  itself.  .  .  If 
there  were  no  piracy  of  the  copyrighted  book, 
there  could  be  no  remedy  ...  (or  the  use 
of  a  title  which  could  not  be  copyrighted  inde- 
pendently of  the  book."  (Shepley,  e.  j.,  "Os- 
good V.  Allen,"  Nov.,  1873,  I  Holmes,  igj.— 
compare.  Nelson,  j.,  "Jollie  v,  Jaques,"  I  Blatch- 
ford, 6J7.) 

But  Utle  of  a  dramatic  composition  can  be 
protected  at  common  law. — Defendant  pleads 
thaliho  title  was  the  same,  his  play  is  another  play 
from  that  owned  by  plaintifs. — "The  name  of 
the  'The  Two  Orphans'  as  characteriiing  a 
particular  dramatic  representation,  has  great 
value  to  plaintifs.  It  is  the  name  by  which  their 
play  is  known  to  the  general  public,  and  when 
defendant  announced  the  performance  of  '  Les 
Deux  Orphelinei,'  or  '  The  Two  Orphans.' 
under  both  the  French  and  English  titles,  if  it 
was  not  his  jntention  to  produce  that  play,  the 
effect  of  it  was  to  mislead  the  public,  and 
thereby  injure  the  plainiitfs  in  their  future  busi- 
ness. .  .  From  auth  a  use  of  this  name  the 
court  is  of  the  opinion  that  the  defendant  should 
be  restrained."  (Philadelphia  common-pleas, 
July,  i87S,  Pratt,  j..  "Shook  v.  Wood,"  10 
Phila.,  374.)     Injunction  ordered. 

Trade-marks.  Title  in  name  of  a  oeriodi- 
cal  as  a  trademark  may  exist,  but  "there  is 
no  aucfa  thing  as  property  in  a  trade- 
mark as  an  abstract  name.  It  is  the  right 
which  a  person  has  to  use  a  certain  name 
for  articles  which  he  has  manufactured,  so  he 
may  prevent  another  person  from  using  it,  be- 
cause the  mark  or  name  denotes  that  articles  so 
marked  were  manufactured  iy  a  eertain  person, 
and  no  one  else  can  bav  the  right  to  put  the 
same  name  upon  the  his  goods,  and  then  repre- 
sent them  to  bav  been  manufactured  by  the 
person  whose  mark  it  is."  (Langdale,  cited  by 
Shepley,  j.,  Nov..  1B73,  l  Holmes,  195.) 


This  Exposition  will  open  at  Madrid.  Decem- 
ber IS,  1883,  and  will  include  autographs,  pam- 
phlets, newspapers,  books,  musical  compositions, 
architectural  plans,  drawings,  paintings,  engrav- 
ings, works  of  sculpture,  and  other  analogous 
productions.  It  will  also  give  room  to  the 
necessary  implements  for  such  work,  such  as 
ink,  pens,  stationery,  printers'  and  binders' 
tools,  musical  instruments,  pencils,  brushes, 
colors,  palettes,  drawing-maps,  and  all  objects 
required  in  tbe  execution  and  furtherance  of  art 
and  literature.  All  objects  exhibited  will  be  for 
sale.  Tbe  pritcs  offered  will  be  in  money, 
gold,  silver,  and  bronze  medals,  diplomas, 
etc.  It  is  not  necessary  to  dwell  upon  the  im- 
portance of  soch  an  exhibition  for  introducing 
foreign  productions  into  the  Spanish  kingdom. 
Any  information  required  by  those  desiring  to 
exhibit,  can  be  had  by  addressing  the  Agenda 
Intemaeional,  fara   Cffmisimtt  lAlerarias,   Mad-

OBITUARY. 
MAX  CKIEBEL. 

His  many  friends  in  the  trade  will  do  doubt  feel 
•1  niDch  shocked  as  we  were  to  team  of  the  sud- 
den death  o[  Mdx  Griebel.  the  manager  of  the 
New  York  branch  of  L.  Prang  &  Co.  Mr. 
Griebel  was  known  10  be  suSeilng  from  a  chronic 
disease  of  ibe  stomach,  but  this  was  not  consid- 
ered of  an  aUrming  character.  A  few  weeks 
•4[0,  by  advice  ol  his  physician,  he  went  to  the 
White  Mounuins,  and  the  mountain  air  seemed 
to  greatly  tfcnelit  him,  but  two  weeks  ago  his 
condition  niiide  it  necessary  for  him  to  reium  to 
his  home  in  Brooklyn.  From  that  time  up  lo  his 
death  he  was  confined  to  his  bed.  On  Saturday 
inotning  [Seplemtwr  i)  he  expressed  a  hope  of 
getting  round  again,  but  in  the  afternoon  he 
suddenly  Krew  worse  and  expired, 

Mr.  Griebel  was  born  December  13,  1841,  at 
Berlin,  Germany.  Aiter  receiving  an  excellent 
college  cducatiun.  he  was  apprenticed  in  the 
large  linen  house  of  Goschenbofer  &  Roesickc. 
for  four  years.  After  serving  liis  time  he  entered 
the  celebrated  house  of  Hermann  Gerson,  the 
A.  T-  Stewart  of  Berlin,  where  he  filled  a  very 
important  position.  In  Octotier,  1B64,  be  had  to 
give  up  his  position  [o  serve  one- year  in  the 
Prussian  army.  In  consequence  of  the  humili- 
ating treatment  there  received,  he  resolved  10 
leave  the  country  and  emigrate  to  Amerira.  He 
landed  in  New  York  in  February.  1866,  and 
about  three  years  later  joined  Mr.  E.  Stone  in 
the  manufacture  of  ladies'  linen  and  lace  collars 
and  hand-knit  worsted  goods,  but  did  not  find 
the  business  profitable.  In  1E74  he  formed  (he 
acquamtance  of  Mr.  Louis  Prang,  of  Boston, 
who  was  then  looking  for  an  agent  (or  the  intro- 
duction of  his  illuminated  advertising  cards, 
which  he  was  about  to  publish.  Mr.  Griebel 
accepted  the  agency,  which,  however,  was  not  for 
Mr.  Prang's  lull  line  of  publications,  but  only 
for  the  new  advertising  cards,  for  which  a  field 
was  to  be  opened.  We  all  know  how  hard  Mr. 
Griebel  worked  from  the  time  he  first  opened 
the  little  room  at  29  Broadway,  where  be  did 
all  the  work  wiihoul  any  help,  until  the  success 
of  the  enterprise  under  his  direction  enabled  him 
10  establish  the  commodious  quarters  at  3S  Bond 
St..  and  sustain  twelve  assistants. 

The  deceased  was  a  man  of  fine  and  com- 
manding personal  appearance,  and  of  a  genial, 
litwial  nature.  His  loss  will  be  felt  quite  as 
much  in  social  and  artistic  circles  as  in  the  trade. 

Owing  to  the  illness  of  Mrs.  Giiebel,  the 
funeral  services  were  of  a  strictly  private  charac' 
ter.  Not  withstanding  this  there  were  many  ir 
attendance  in  addition  to  the  immediate  relatives 
of  the  deceased.  chicSy  neighbors  and  intimate 
friends.  There  were  also  a  few  present  promi- 
nently connected  with  the  trade,  among  whom 
was  L.  Prang,  who  attended,  together  with  a 
number  of  employes  of  the  New  York  house  and 
of  the  Palmer  Art  Company,  of  which  Mr.  Grie- 
bel was  treasurer.  The  services,  on  Monday  last. 
were  very  brief,  and  at  their  close  the  remains 
of  the  deceased  were  taken  to  Greenwood  Ceme- 
tery and  interred  in  the  family  plot. 

IVAN  TURGENIBFF. 

Ivan  Sbrgbvitch  TuRCiBNiEFF,  the  great 
Russian  novelist,  died  at  Bougival,  France,  on 
the  3d  of  September,  after  a  long  and  painlal 


illness.  Tutgenieff  was  born  on  the  Qth  of  No- 
vember, 181S,  in  the  little  province  of  Orel,  inihe 
interior  of  Russia.  His  parents  were  of  the 
wealthy  nobility,  but  he  very  early  in  lite  bq;aii 
to  display  the  leaning  toward  radicalism  and 
liberalism  that  inspired  him  in  his  great  miasioa, 
and  made  him  indirectly  one  of  the  causes  of  tbt 
abolition  of  serfdom  in  the  Russian  Empiie. 
He  studied  at  the  University  of  Moscow,  wheie 
his  opinions  upon  liberty  and  bis  great  admira- 
tion for  the  United  States  caus«i  him  to  be 
called  the  American.  Upon  leaving  Moscow  be 
studied  German  literature  and  the  philosophy  of 
Hegel  at  Berlin  foi  three  years,  thoroughly  mas- 
tering the  German  language.  French  he  spoke 
perfectly,  as  is  customary  among  educated  Rus- 
sians, and  he  also  acquired  great  facility  io 
speaking  English.  He  returned  to  Russia  in 
1841  and  began  his  life's  work  as  a  literary  man. 
He  ptiblisbed  vatious  sketches  and  contributions 
to  periodicals,  went  abroad  to  Germany.  Fiance. 
and  Italy  Irom  1846  to  iSjo,  and  while  travelling 
during  these  years  wrote  the  work  that  brouebt 
him  fame  and  determined  his  special  field  of  lit- 
erature. The  "  Journal  ol  a  Sportsman"  made 
TurgenicB  the  hero  of  the  day  and  alarmed  ibe 
Russian  Government,  and  the  Emperor  Nicholas 
banished  him  for  five  years  fur  having  daicd  lo 
criticise  the  government.  He  was  released  im- 
mediately before  the  death  of  Nicholas,  and  the 
Ciar  Alexander  is  said  tu  have  admitted  that 
Turgenieff's  writings  hastened  the  great  work  of 
emancipation  in  Russia.  Even  this  liberal  man 
thought  the  revolutionary  influence  ol  the  young 
wiiler  dangerous,  and  tuuii^hed  him  upon  three 
occasions.  TurgenieS  finally  made  Paris  hii 
home,  and  there  the  last  years  ol  his  life  were 
spent  in  unremitting  activity,  strengthened  and 
gladdened  by  the  gratitude  and  sympathy  he  bai 
always  received  from  the  race  he  was  iiutm- 
mental  in  freeing  and  raising  from  boodage. 
His  remarkable  books  are  all  in  the  form  of  fic- 
tion, sad  in  their  tone  and  stamped  with  the 
unmistakable  impress  of  truth  and  purpose. 
Several  of  them  were  brought  out  in  this  country 
between  1S67  and  1B74  by  Henry  Holt  &  Co.  ia 
excellent  translations,  and  made  a  great  sensa- 
tion. The  must  notable  are  "  Fathers  and  Sons  l" 
"Smoke  ;"  "Lira  ;""  On  the  Eve;'"  "  Dtmitri 
Rondioi  ;"  "  Spring  Floods,"  and  ''Virgin  SoiL" 
Turgeniefi's  influence  on  the  liletalurc  of  the  day 
will  be  more  justly  appreciated  by  a  later  and 
less  partisan  generation.  He  has  demonstrated 
that  the  novel  in  gilted  hands  can  be  made  as 
important  factor  in  rousing  enthusiasm,  spread- 
ing truth,  righting  wrongs,  and  influenciog  the 
history  of  civiliiatioo.

a  new  edition  of  Gray's  "Anatomy."  The 
more  elaborate  work  on  the  same  subject  by  Dr. 
Harrison  Allen  is  now  approaching  completion  ; 
the  fifth  fasciculus  will  be  issued  in  a  little  over  a 
month,  and  the  sixth  and  last  before  the  year 
closes.  Klein's  "  Elements  of  Histology"  is  the 
(itst  of  a  Studtnl's  Manual  series  to  be  published 
by  the  same  firm.  These  ilKle  works  are  issued

ume will  be  Pepper's  "Surgical  Pathology,"  to

"  Pathological  Chemistry."  As  a  succinct  con- 
densation of  (he  various  branches  of  which  they

daring  the  collegiate  course,  while  they  will

busy  practitioner,  regarding  subjects  very  readi- 
ly Kjrgolten.  Among  the  forthcoming  volumes 
announced  as  being  nearly  ready  are  Coats' 
"Treatise  on  Pathology;'  a  new  edilion  of 
Bumstead  and  Taylor  on  "Venereal  Diseases;" 
while  the  "Treatise  on  Midwifery,"  by  Dr.

of  Obstetrics  at  Jefferson  Medical  College,  is 
announced  as  in  press.

VIILL  PUBLISH  SEPTEMBER  15th: 

Twenty  Poems  from  Henry  Wadsworth 
Longfellow. 

Illusltaied  from  Paintings  b;  his  son,  Ernest  W.  Longfellow,     i  vol.,  8vo,  cloih,  Uilelully 

siamped,  full  gill,  $4  ;  morocco  or  tree  calf,  fg. 

The  poems  chosen  for  illustration  are  :  It  is  not  Always  May,  Daybreak,  The  Village  Blict 
smith.  The  Day  is  Ooao,  Seaweed,  The  Evening  Star,  Cadenabbia.  Amalfi,  Snowflakes.  SoDgo 
River,  Chiysaor,  Moonlight,  The  Lighthouse,  The  Bells  of  Lynn,  Three  Friends  of  Mine,  Tht 
Tides.  Elegiac.  The  Tide  Rises— the  Tide  Falls.  Mad  River  in  the  White  Mountains,  Becalmtd. 

These  poems  are  illustrated  with  fifty  designs,  in  which  Mr.  Ernest  Longfellow  interprets  their 
spirit  with  rare  grace  and  beauty,  A  bne  portiait  of  Mr.  Longfellow  is  prefixed  to  the  volumt, 
and  peculiar  interest  attache*  10  the  book  from  the  fact  thai  the  portrait  and  all  the  illusiradoni 
are  from  the  hand  of  the  poet's  son.     It  is  in  all  respects  a  very  tasteful  gift-book. 

The  Bodley  Family  in  England. 

By  Horace  £.  Scudi>sr.     Fully  illustrated.     With  an  ornamental  cover,  $1.50. 

In  this  book  the  Bodleys,  who  had  been  travelling  in  Holland,  go  to  England,  and  are  so  lotl- 
unate  as  to  find  other  Bodleys,  distant  cousins,  who  receive  them  very  heanily.  With  tbcn 
they  visit  many  noteworthy  and  interesting  places,  and  talk  of  tnany  persons  and  incidcDlE 
famous  in  English  history,  all  of  which  are  by  Mr.  Scudder's  skill  made  peculiarly  fresh  and 
attractive.  The  book  is  fully  illustrated,  bound  in  an  omamenul  covet,  and  will  renew  xai 
extend  the  satisfaction  with  which  hundreds  of  thousands  have  read  the  previous  Bodley  books. 

How  to  Help  the  Poor. 

By  Mrs.  James  T.  Fields,    i  vol.,  i6mD. 
A  small  book,  embodying  the  result  of  many  years'  experience  and  observation  in  charitable 
work.    It  is  not  a  sketch  of  theoretical  pbilaalhropy,  but  many  eiamplesare  cited  to  illusiraietht 
practical  views  which  it  is  hoped  this  book  may  tender  more  effective. 

Longfellow's  Courtship  of  Miles  Standish. 

Dramatized  for  private  theatricals  in  schoali  and  families,     Jtivenidt  Littnry  Seritt.     Papci. 

In  this  dramatization  Mr.  Longfellow's  work  Is  unchanged,  but  some  parts  are  omjtttd  ani^ 
other  parts  are  included  in  parentheses  to  make  perfectly  clear  the  portions  retained  in  the  pli> 
Full  directions  are  given' concerning  scenery  and  costumes. 

A  Primer  of  American  Literature. 

By  Charles  F.  Richaruson,  Professor  of  Literature  in  Dartmouth  College,  New  and  Revised 
Edition,  with  twelve  Portraits  of  American  authors.  Ttotnly-jSrit  lAoiuand.  iSmo,  red 
edges,  30  cents.

DAISY  MILLER: 

A    COMEDY    IN    THREE   ACTS.

A  BIRD'S-EYE  VIEW  OF

ol  indisputable  accuracy  from  the  military  standpoint,  and  yet  so  clear  and  lucid  as  10  be  easily 
comprehended  by  the  non-military  reader.  It  is  an  Invaluable  epitome  of  the  battles,  sieges,  and 
strategic  movements  of  iS6i-6s,  based  on  the  best  aulborities.  Northern,  Southern,  and  foreign.

•ican  Commonwealths. 

ited  by  Horace  E.  Scudder.  A  series  of  volumes  narrat- 
the  history  of  such  States  of  the  Union  as  have  exerted  a 
itive  influence  in  the  shaping  of  the  national  government,     iiyCooglc 


36  The  PuMhhtr^  Wetklj.      [Nos.  60S-9I  i. 

or  have  illustrated  in  a  noteworthy  degree  any  d 
political  principles,  or  have  a  striking  political,  socia 
nomical  history. 

The  commonwealth  has  always  been  a  positive  force  in  / 
history,  and  it  is  believed  that  no  better  time  could  be  foi 
statement  of  the  life  inherent  in  the  States  than  when  the 
the  nation  has  been  assured ;  it  is  hoped  by  this  means 
new  light  upon  the  development  of  the  country  and  to  giv 
point  of  view  for  the  study  of  American  history.  In  the  ca 
older  commonwealths,  which  owe  their  origin  to  Europeai 
zation  in  the  seventeenth  and  eighteenth  centuries,  an  atte 
be  made  to  trace  institutions,  laws,  and  personal  influences ; 
acteiiize  the  people  who  worked  out  the  problems  of  societj 
follow  the  development  of  state  life,  both  in  the  colonial  ar 
federal  periods. 

The  migration  from  the  Atlantic  to  the  Pacific  will  be  s1 
the  records  of  the  several  commonwealths  which  have  ri 
that  migration  and  added  their  characteristics  to  the  natio 
The  romance  of  their  early  history,  the  adventure  and  for< 
their  founders,  the  application  of  principles  of  govemmer 
lated  in  the  older  communities,  the  contributions  made  by  t 
pean  colonists  of  the  nineteenth  century,  and  the  material  c{ 
of  growth  will  receive  close  attention. 

This  series  will  be  brought  out  under  the  editorial  car 
Horace  E.  Scudder,  who  is  well  known  as  a  student  of  / 
history,  and  as  a  writer.  It  will  be  his  aim  to  secure  tru 
and  graphic  narratives,  which  shall  have  substantial  value  : 
ical  monographs,  and  at  the  same  time  do  full  justice  to  th 
esque  elements  of  the  subjects. 

The  volumes  will  be  uniform  in  size  and  general  style 
series  of  American  Statesmen  and  American  Men  of  Let 
will  be  furnished  with  maps,  indexes,  and  such  brief  critii 
ratus  as  may  in  any  case  add  to  the  thoroughness  of  the  wi

Mr.  Emerson's  Works.    Edition  de  Luxe. 

This  edition  will  be  printed  on  paper  of  the  best  quality, 
volumes  as  perfect  in  workmanship  and  as  attractive  to  the 
the  best  skill  of  the  Riverside  Press  can  produce.  It  will  b 
important  respects  equal  to  the  large  paper  edition  of  Haw 
Works  recently  published,  which  has  won  the  hearty  praise 
noisseurs  in  the  art  of  book-making. 

The  edition  will  be,  as  far  as  care  and  skill  can  make  it,  a 
presentation  of  writings  which  have  exerted  so  potent  an  in 
and  which  seem  secure  of  the  immortality  with  which  the 
literature  is  endowed. 

It  will  contain  two  new  portraits  of  Mr.  Emerson,  —  one  : 
ing  by  SchofE,  and  the  other  an  engraving  by  Wilcox.

Edgar  Fawcett. 

An  Ambitious  Woman.  A  Novel.  By  Edgar  Fawcett, 
of  "  A  Hopeless  Case,"  "  A  Gentleman  of  Leisure,"  etc. 
i2mo. 

The  scene  of  this  novel,  as  of  Mr.  Fawcett's  previous  sti 
New  York  city.  It  is  a  story  of  current  life,  dealing  w 
striking  social  fact  of  the  tendency  among  American  womei 
discontented  with  their  surroundings,  and  depicting  their  ef 
achieve  higher  positions.  It  contrasts,  not  didactically  but  < 
ically,  a  merely  selfish  ambition  and  the  success  it  frequei 
tains  with  the  possibilities  open  to  a  wider  and  worthier  aim. 
story  has  many  fresh  types  of  character,  and,  with  Mr,  Fa 
familiar  knowledge  of  New  York  society  and  his  well-know 
ary  skill,  it  cannot  fail  to  be  one  of  the  most  notable  novels 
year.     {November)

FLETE  Works  of  William  H,  Seward.  Edited  by  George 
.  Baker.  With  Portraits  and  Memoir.  In  five  volumes, 
.'o,  cloth,  $15.00;  half  calf,  $30.00. 

the  roll  of  American  statesmen  few  names  are  worthy  to 
io  high  as  that  of  William  H.  Seward.  His  public  life  covered 
ban  forty  years,  in  which  many  questions  of  the  highest  tra- 
ce challenged  consideration.  Among  these  questions  were : 
ir  Education  in  all  its  phases ;  Internal  Improvements,  em- 
g  the  entire  history  of  the  origin,  completion,  and  proposed 
sment  of  the  Erie  Canal,  and  of  the  New  York  and  Erie  and 
■ailroads;  Slavery,  its  rights  and  prerogatives,  the  duties  and 
;ions  of  the  free  States  in  regard  to  it ;  the  Public  Land  Ques- 
ith  a  history  and  discussion  of  the  Anti-Rent  troubles  in  New 
Crime  and  its  Penalties ;  Political  Economy,  in  its  adaptation 
national  condition ;  the  Fugitive  Slave  Law ;  the  Annexation 
la;  the  Maintenance  of  the  National  Honor;  the  Protection 
lerican  Rights ;  Treaty  for  the  Suppression  of  the  Slave 
;  Treaty  fbr  the  Acquisition  of  Alaska ;  Treaty  with  China ; 
'  for  an  Interoceanic  Canal  at  Darien ;  Treaties  affecting 
Uization  ;  the  Trent  Affair;  the  Emancipation  Proclamation; 
tiirteenth  and  Fourteenth  Amendments  to  the  Constitution, 

these  questions  were  discussed  by  Mr.  Seward  with  a  free- 
igor,  and  clearness  seldom,  if  ever,  equaled.

ime  of  religious  selections  designed  for  the  cheer  and  consolation  of  sorrowii 
lympathy  for  a.  friend  in  sorrow  can  be  expressed  in  no  more  delicate  or  accej

IKTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT. 

Fnw,  tkt  Lmndrm  PuUUlurf  drcnUT. 

An  American,  writing  to  the  Times  from  Fl' 
ence,  makes  yet  another  contribution  to  the  wei 
some  li[era[ure  of  the  international  copyrij 
question  ;  and,  In  order  thai  his  own  standpo 
may  be  made  perfectly  clear,  proclaims  hiroi 
as  an  ultra  freetrader,  having  no  personal  sy 
pathy  with  the  protection  of  tbe  book  busine 
and  no  personal  interests  in  the  question  ;  but 
detesting  with  all  his  heart  thconly  form  of  111 
that  the  laws  of  the  United  Stales  do  not  punisl 
that  of  literary  property.  There  are,  he  bol 
but  two  solutions  of  the  problem  possible — 
conclusion  of  a  publishers'  treaty,  permitli 
[he  copyrighting  of  books  printed  in  Englai 
and  Ibat  of  an  authors'  treaty,  putting  the  Engl 
author  on  ^ecisely  the  same  fooling  as 
American.  The  first  he  claims  the  honor  of  hi 
ing  exposed  in  the  Timti  avtt  ten  years  ■ 
as  impracticable  ;  so  utterly  hopeless  that  il 
sheer  waste  of  time  to  negotiate  in  reference 
it ;  and  the  United  States  has.  he  thinks,  unfc 
unalety  (or  the  country,  decided  long  ago  on 
protectionist  system,  and  under  this  syste 
among  other  things,  a  great  production  01  boi 
has  arisen,  which,  as  matters  now  stand,  has 
immense  industrial  importance.  If  a  copyri| 
in  the  interest  of  publishers  were  created,  . 
only  would,  first,  the  American  publication 
English  boolcs  t>e  prevented,  which  it  may  be 
interest  of  the  English  author  to  require,  t 
secondly,  il  would  enable  American  authors 
go.  abroad  to  have  their  books  printed  where 
they  could  get  the  work  done  cheapest,  and 
destroy  entirely  the  home  production  of  l 
class  of  works  which  offers  the  greatest  cbanci 
profit,  and  ruin  tfre  American  publishers.  Eit 
of  these  difficulties  must  be  met  by  imposing  h 
import  duties  on  boolcs,  because  the  vested  inl 
est  in  book  production  would  (or  many  ye 
control  legislation.  The  second,  a  purely  au( 
rial  copyright,  would  be,  he  thinks,  not  obni 
ious  to  any  class  of  Americans,  as  is  indica 
by  their  policy  in  regard  to  patent  rights.  T 
would  be  attained  by  simply  abolishing  tbe  r 
uisite  of  nationality  in  obtaining  a  copyrig 
giving  the  English  author  every  right  possesi

nal  ones  will,  he  believes,  certainly  fail,  so  tc

"  OuiDA  "  has  evidently  no  intention  of  allc

copyright  question,  and  in  another  letter  to  1

English  novels  into  the  English  market  I 
never  been  mooted  in  connection  with  Ibe  coi
