The  Publishers'  Wteicly. 


which  she  expressed  of  any  possible  treaty 
which  should  enable  the  American  publisher  to 
sell  his  editions  of  English  novels  in  Europe. 
"  Ouida  "  [hen  proceeds  W  attack  her  bSlr-noirc, 
the  English  adapter  of  novels  for  use  on  the 
stage  :  "  Nor  can  I  admit  that  I  made  any  ex- 
aggerated claim  for  English  authors  when  I  said 
chat  they  should  accept  nothing  less  than  the 
right  to  select  their  publishers  in  each  country ; 
indeed,  nothing  less  would  constitute  interna- 
tional copyright  law  at  all.  1  do  not  ask.  as 
some  do,  tor  the  exclusive  right  to  send  the  Eng- 
lish edition  into  America  ;  I  do  not  even  believe 
that  this  wouW  be  desirable,  and  it  would  cer- 
tainly never  be  conceded  by  America;  but  1  do  ask 
for  the  right  to  sell  my  writings  to  an  American 
publisher,  with  power  to  punish  all  such  as  shall 
issue  unauthorized  editions  of  them.  But  as  I 
said  previously.  I  never  expect  to  see  thus  much 
granted  ;  and  it  is  true  that  America  may  retort 
with  correctness  that  so  long  as  England  admits 
in  her  own  law  such  shameless  thefl  as  that  com- 
mitted by  stage  adaptations  of  novels  It  is  some- 
what incongruous  to  call  on  another  country  to 
rectify  an  analogous  but  leas  offensive  wrong. 
The  American  pirates,  at  their  worst,  do  me  less 
injury  than  the  English  stage-stealers  ;  the  former 
do,  at  the  least,  reproduce  my  tent  uiimutilatcd 
and  unadded  to  ;  the  latter  take  my  name  and 
the  names  of  my  characters  and  of  my  works  to 
carry  off  their  own  paltry  mediocrity  and  vulgar 
caricature.  That  England  denies  stage  right  to 
novelists  is  a  more  gross  injustice  than  even  the 
refusal  of  America  to  concede  some  fonn  of  in- 
ternational literary  law."* 

SPECIFICATIONS  FOR  BINDING. 

Frgm  Hu  Litrtr,  7«>'nu/. 

After  considerable  experimenting  the  follow- 
ing specifications  have  been  found  to  answer  the 
purpose  best. 

Binding  done  in  accordance  with  them  is,  I 
think,  more  durable  than  any  other  kind  now 
used,  and  has  moreover  the  advantages  of  open^ 
ing  very  freely  and  of  costing  no  more  than  the 
ordinary  library  binding  now  generally  used. 

Peoria,  III.  F.  J.  Soldan. 


1.  In  pulling  books  apart  care  should  be  taken 
not  to  injure  the  backs. 

2.  Each  book  must  he  Carefully  collated,  all 
single  leaves  and  pictures  securely  pasted  in, 
and  all  torn  pages  should  be  thoroughly  repaired 

3.  Books  from  which  cither  title-page,  leaves, 
or  pictures  are  missing  must  not  be  bound,  ex- 
cept by  special  instructions. 

4.  In  pasting  in  leaves  or  pictures  the  paste 
should  not  be  tipped  on  with  the  finger,  but  with 
the  brush  on  the  pasting  board,  and  with  a  piece 
of  waste  paper  over  the  leaf  or  picture  the  re- 
quired distance  from  the  edge  to  t>e  pasted,  so 
that  the  paste  is  deposited  evenly  in  a  straight 


6.  The  booksare  to  bescwedoolinentift!! 
of  an  inch  wide,  and  there  should  be  used  for  i;tc> 

volumes  at  least  2  upes.  and  if  they  are  ihiS'?' 
than  ii  inchsiapes;  for  Bvoand  largervoicJO 

7.  In  cutting  apart  there  should  be  letlii:tiE 
2ji  inches  of  tape  on  each  side  of  the  tHt 
The  first  and  last  sections  and  the  waste  pii-n 
should  be  overcast  with  muslin,  and  eacfiH:::^ 
be  sewed  "all  along"  with  the  best  Mii^ni. 
thread.  The  begining  of  the  thread  musibeuit- 
fully  secured,  and  as  each  thread  is  termini:^ 
it  must  be  securely  and  neatly  joined  tu  anat;:! 
and  the  ends  cut  off. 

8.  The  back  of  the  book  must  not  be  cm  off  :fr 
whip- stitching,  except  when  the  book  a  =onitw 
much  to  be  sewed  in  the  regular  waj',  and  3 
such  case  the  consent  of  the  librarian  must  be 
obtained. 

g.  Each  section  should  be  opened  up  if  ^'■' 
back  in  order  that  all  leaves  be  caught  in  term 

to.  Each  volume  should  be  provided  vabtio 
waste  papers. 

Trimming. 

IT.  Not  more  of  themar^nof  thebooksboGdi 
be  trimmed  off  than  the  rough  dirty  edge,  leai-ii; 
the  book  as  large  as  possible.  Books  with  iw- 
row  margins  should  be  trimmed  on  top  on!v.  in- 
if  Che  top  margin  is  too  narrow  it  should  doi  k 
trimmed  at  all. 

12.  In  glueing,  the  glue  should  notbelooili^^t. 
hut  very  hot,  and  must  be  well  rubbed  in  bet«Ki 
the  sections. 

13.  The  back  ibould  herounded  when  lbe|I^: 
is  cool,  but  before  it  is  hMd. 

14.  The  boards  are  to  be  Davey's  tac  boir. 
and  for  ijmo  or  Svo  volumes  which  are  thin: 
than  I  inch  No  35  should  lie  used  ;  for  Ui:ctK 
or  larger  volumes  No.  10  or  heavier. 

tj.  The  tape*  must  be  passed  through  slits  -3 
the  boards,  cut  the  size  of  (he  I«pc  from  the  in^.^ 
to  the  outside,  and  securely  glued  down  on  tK'.J 
sides  and  well  hammered. 

16.  The  book  should  then  be  pressed  wiihniB 
between  boards  and  book. 

17.  The  backs  of  books  should  then  be  w^'br^ 
with  thin  paste  and  allowed  to  stand  lor  a  it" 
minutes  to  permit  the  glue  to  soften,  then  nibbr.! 
off  carefully  and  allowed  to  dry. 

iS.   All  books  to  have  loose  spring  backs. 

19.  The  lining  should  then  be  put  on  the  Uci; 
of  hooks  one  on  and  two  oR,  or  on  larger  r.-r 
umes  two  on  and  two  off.

.  Books  to  have  from  4  to  s

of  the  color  ordered  ;  thin  skins  to  be  Hie 
small  work  to  avoid  clumsmess.  and  be 
skins  for  large  books.  The  leather  mui

pressed  again. 

Half  Xnan  Bindings.

ibe  sides  with  marble  paper.  There  Is  to  be  no 
gilding  on  the  back  except  author,  title,  and 
class-number. 


OBITUARY. 

John  Payne  Cdlliek,  the  aged  philologist, 
bibliographer,  ftnd  commentator  on  Shakespeare, 
died  in  London  September  iS.  His  "  History 
oi  Draroatic  Poetry"  was  published  in  1831, 
The  Duke  of  Devonshire  (to  whom  Mr.  Collier 
became  librarian)  and  other*  opened  their  valu- 
able libraries  to  his  researches,  and  enabled  him 
to  compile  his  "Bibliographical  nod  Critical 
Catalogue."  His  last  published  work  was  a 
"  Bibliographical  Account  of  Rare  Books,"  in 
1865. 

Mas,  Julia  P.  Smith,  the  authoress,  during 
an  early  morning  drive  near  her  summer  resi- 
dence at  Hartford,  Coon.,  on  the  7th  inst.,  was 
thrown  from  her  carriage  and  sustained  injuries 
that  proved  fatal.  Mrs.  Smith  was  about  SO 
years  old.  and  was  a  reuiarkably  intelligent 
wotnan,  with  an  intense  love  for  literature. 
Among  Ibe  most  popular  of  her  works  are  : 
"Widow  Goldsmith's  Daughter,"  "Chiis  and 
Otho,"  "The  Widower,"  "The  Married  Belle," 
and  "  Courting  and  Fanning."  At  the  lime 
'The  Widow  Goldsmith's  Daughter"  was  pub- 
lished it  was  very  popular,  and  was  la  great  de- 
mand. The  characters  in  this  work  were  gen- 
erally Hartlord  people. 

William  J,  Tekney,  critic  for  Appletons' 
>ublishiiig  bouse,  died  in  Newark,  N.  J..  Thurs- 
lay.  September  30.  He  was  t>om  at  Newport, 
t.  I.,  and  was  graduated  at  Yale  College  in 
330.  He  was  instructor  on  a  ship  in  the  navy 
or  some  years,  and  afterward  he  lived  at 
Elizabeth,  N.  J.  He  studied  law  and  medicine. 
tenioving  to  Brooklyn,  he  became  judge  of  one 
f  the  courts.  About  30  years  ago  he  became 
onnected  with  the  Appletons.  He  was  an  edi- 
nr  oi  their  Cyclopaedia,  when  it  was  started 
vcr  20  years  ago,  and  he  continued  on  it  until 
e  was  incapacitated  for  active  labor.  He  whs 
n  author  of  a  history  of  the  rebellion,  and  as- 
isted  Jefferson  Davis  In  the  preparation  of  bis 
ork,  the  "Rise  and  Fall  of  the  Confederacy." 
le  leaves  two  daughters  and  three  sons. 
Di'TTOK  Cook,  oneof  the  best  known  of  the 
nglish  dramatic  critics,  died  in  London  Septem- 
:r  I31h,  aged  51  years.  Mr.  Cook  did  not  con- 
ne  bis  work  to  writing  about  the  theatres.  He 
rote  upon  the  fine  arts  (or  various  reviews,  and 
;  is  the  author  of  a  book  called  "  Art  in  Eng- 
nd. "       He   wrote  also  several  novels,  among 


them  "A  Prodigal  Son,"  "  Paul  Foster's  Daugh- 
ter," "  The  Trials  of  the  Tredgolds,"  "  Sir  Felix 
Foy,  Bart.,"  and  "The  Banns  of  Marriage." 
His  books  of  criticism  and  theatrical  record  are  : 
"  A  Book  of  the  Play  ;  Studies  and  Illustrations 
of  Histrionic  Story,  Life,  and  Character," 
"Nights  at  the  Play,"  and  "Hours  with  the 
PUycrs."  His  "  Nights  at  the  Play  "  covers  the 
period  between  1867  and  1S81. 

Henri  Conscience,  the  most  popular  Belgian 
writer,  died  in  Paris,  September  gth,  aged  71 
years.  Henri  Conscience  has  produced  a  variety 
of  interesting  sketches,  illustrative  of  Flemish 
manners,  such  as  "Evening  Hours,"  "The 
Executioner's  Child,"  "  The  New  Niobe,"  "The 
Conscript,"  "The  Poor  Gentleman,"  "  Quintiri 
Mctiys."  "Pages  from  the  Book  of  Nature." 
"Jacob  Van  Artevelde,"  "Blind  Rosa,"  and 
several  other  works,  which  have  been  trans- 
lated into  English,  German,  Danish,  and  Italian. 
In  1870  he  once  more  gained  the  prize  of  litera- 
ture given  every  tifth  year,  by  his  romance 
"Bavo  en  Licveken."  which  may  be  classed 
among  his  best  works.  One  of  his  latest  works 
is  "De  Kerels  van  Vlaaderen,"  an  historical 
romance,  1871.  The  following  tales  by  M.  Con- 
science have  been  published  in  English  :  "The 
Progress  of  a  Painter,"  "The  Good  Mother," 
"The  Lion  of  Flanders  :  or, the  Battle  of  the 
Golden  Spurs,"  "The  Curse  o(  the  Village," 
"The  Happiness  of  being  Rich."  "The  Miser," 
"Talcs  of  Old  Flanders."  "Count  Hugo  of 
Craenhove,"  "  Wooden  Clara,"  "  Veva  :  or,  the 
Watof  the  Peasants,"  "The  Conscript,"  "The 
Demon  of  Gold,"  "The  Poor  Gentleman," 
"  Ricketicketack,"  "Blind  Rosa,"  and  "The 
Village  Innkeeper."

Published  September  28th: 

IHE  GREAT  HOLIDAY  BOOK  OF  1888: 

AH  ENTIRELY  NEW  EDITION  OF  ALFRED  TENNYSON'S 

THE    PRINCESS. 

With  I30  new  and  beautiful  illustrations  on  wood.  An  elegant  octavo  volume 
of  nearly  230  pages,  beautifully  bound,  with  'full  gilt  edges,  in  box.  In 
cloth,  $6  ;  in  tree  calf  or  antique  morocco,  (io;  in  calf  or  morocco,  inlaid 
mosaic  patterns,  fti2.5o.    'A  few  copies  in  crushed  Uvant,  with  sijk  linings, 

"  The  Princess  "  is  probably  the  most  famous  of  the  longer  works 
0/  the  poet-laureate  of  England,  and  abounds  in  the  most  picturesque 
and  attractive  scenes  and  subjects  for  illustration.  The  best  American 
artists  have  drawn  these  illustrations,  con  amore,  producing  a  great 
number  of  choice  examples  of  the  high  perfection  which  wood-engrav- 
ing has  reached  in  the  New  World.  The  general  supervision  of  the 
work  has  devolved  upon  Mr.  A.  V.  S.  Anthony,  who  held  the  same 
relation  to  the  recent  magnificent  editions  of  "  Lucile"  and"  The  Lady 
■}f  the  Lake;'^  thus  insuring  for  these  illustrations  the  utmost  accu- 
racy  in  study,  taste,  and  composition^.and  elegance  in  finish. 

An  eminent  poet  and  critic,  Richard  H.Stoddard,  in  referetue  to 
'his  edition,  speaks  of  "  The  Princess  "  as  "  That  sweetest  and  purest 
f  all  modern  epics,  Tennyson's  'Princess!  It  is  the  truest  and  noblest 
^oetn  of  which  womanhood  is  the  theme  in  any  language!'  The  pub- 
ishers  believe  that  in  this  form,  and  with  this  elegance  of  finish,  the 
'jork  "will  be  widely  welcomed  on  its  merits  as  a  fine-art  edition,  and 
ecome

For  1883  {Eleventh  Year), 

A  Lar^e  8to,  of  over  2500  pages,  is  now  ready. 


The  improvements  in  ibe  Trade-List  Aonual  last  ^eai  have  given  such  gcneial  satiEfaction 
Ihat  there  has  been  no  change  in  ihe  preseol  issue  {fUventh  year)  as  lo  the  essential  features, 

I.  The  latest  Catalogues  of  Ameiican  Publishers,  coutributed  by  themselves  and  arranged  alpha- 
betically by  Ihe  names  of  the  publishers. 
I.  A  complete  reprint  of  the  "Publishers'  Weekly  "/h//AV/(  Records  of  Books  published  (inclu- 
sive of  all  the  descriptive  notes)  from  July  8,  18S2,  to  June  30,  1SS3.  In  bider  lo  facilitate 
reference,  it  will  be  accompanied  by  a  full  index,  by  which  every  book  on  record  can  be 
found,  whether  it  Is  looked  for  under  aulhor,  title,  or  tubjcet. 
\.  The  "Educational  Catalogue"  for  1883.  which  Is  used  by  Ihe  entire  trade  and  education^ 
interests  as  the  most  repreaeniaiive  refereace-4ist  of  Scbool-books. 

The  above  Lists,  all  bound  in  one  volume,  present  in  their  combination  so  convenient  and 
ime-saving  a  working-tool  as  to  make  it  Indispensable  lo  etKtj  one  who  has  any  interest  in  the 
'urchase  and  sale  o(  books. 

The  price  is  One  Dollar  and  a  half  pet  copy  net,  bound  in  cloth,  or  can  be  sent  poat- 
•atd   for  Two  DoUara  nel,  payable  in  advance. 

RemitUDces  should  be  made  by  check,  money-order  on  New  York,  or  registered  letter,  as 
e  canaot  be  responsible  for  any  losses.     Receipt  for  remittance  will  be  sent  by  return  mail.  ' 

Unless  special  shipping  directions  are  received,  copies  ready  for  delivery  will  be  dispatched 
r  express  unpaid. 

tW  Parties  so  desirinit  can  have  iLeir  volumes  supplied  with  the  " SILVERA  DU- 
'I^EX  INDEX,"  al  the  itigM  extra  charge  ef  50  eenfi  per  copy.  The  Sitvera  Index  is  some- 
hat  on  the  plan  of  Denlson's,  with  the  practical  improvement,  however,  of  having  the  alphabet 
intcd  on  the  concave  surface  (instead  of  on  the  margin  of  the  page),,  which  enables  instantane- 
is  reference,  whether  the  book  is  open  or  shut.

Roberts  Bros,  have  published  tntheir.^amoH^ 
Wtmm  series  "Margaret  Fuller."  by  Julia 
Ward  Howe.  Mrs,  Howe  la  said  to  have  made 
a  very  readable  volume  and  to  have  displayed

crusade  against  the  use  of  tobacco  in  all  forms. 
In  the  new  Clatiic  series  they  will  issue  at  once 
Southey's  "  Life  of  Nelson,  with  illnstratioos 
by  Birkel  Foster,  and  Maria  Edgeworth's  re- 
nowned "Classic  Tales,"  which  will  include  a 
bif^raphical  sketch  by  Grace  A.  Oliver. 

Jambs  R.  Osgood  ft  Co.  have  just  ready  their 
new  and  handsomely  illustrated  holiday  book, 
"  The  Princess."  by  Alfred  Tennyson.  The  vol- 
ume contains  one  hundred  and  twenty  new  and 
beautiful  woodcuts  by  some  of  the  best  American 
artists,  engraved  under  the  general  supervision 
of  A.  V.  S.  Anthony,  of  Boston.  Tbe  poem 
abounds  in  the  most  picturesque  and  auractive 
subjects  lor  ilttutration,  and  the  general  excel- 
lence and  skill  evinced  by  the  artists  and  engrav- 
ers in  its  production  places  it  among  the  finest 
^ft-books  of  the  year.  They  have  also  just  pub- 
lished "  A  Woman  of  Honor,"  a  piquant  novel 
of  American  society,  full  of  neir  and  interesting 
sltuatious.  and  a  close  study  of  life  in  tbe  metrop- 
olis, by  H.  C.  Bunner,  well  known  through  his 
connection  with  Puck  and  Tkt  CtHlury.  "  Fort- 
une's Fool,"  a  novel  by  Julian  Hawthorne, 
which  is  described  as  "  a  work  of  great  power, 
destined  to  be  ranked  aa  a  classic  ;"  "  The  Rec- 
ollections of  a  Drummer-Boy,"  by  Rev.  Harry 
M.  Keifer,  late  of  the  ijoth  Penn.  Vols.,  a  vivid 
and  impressive  picture  of  scenes  and  adventures 
during  the  late  rebellion  told  as  only  an  eye-wit- 
ness could  tell  them  )  and  "A  Year  of  Sunshine," 
by  Kate  Sanborn,  a  volume  of  choice  and  cheer- 
ing selections  for  every  day  of  the  year,  destined 
to  bring  "sunshine"  wherever  it  appears,  and 
to  drive  away  the  "blues"  and  all  gloomy 
forebodings.  This  latter  work  was  formerly 
published  as  a  Calendar.  It  is  now  put  Into  . 
boolc-form,  with  spacious  margins  for  ainajopc- 
decorations  and  memoranda.

DANIEL  SMITH. 
>.\>-IFX  Shcth  of  Newburg,  N.  Y.,  died  Sep- 
itMir  36,  aged  73  years.  Mr.  Smith  was  born 
Veroan,  Tolland  County,  Connecticut,  Sep- 
iber.  1810.  and  received  at  that  place  a  com- 
n  school  education.  In  1838  he  came  to  Ncn- 
H  and  became  a  teacher  in  tbe  High  School. 
.b«:  ;ear  1S41  be  went  into  the  boot  and  ibue 


business  with  Mr.  Victor  M.  Watkins  as  partner, 
remaining  in  that  business  two  years.  la  1S43 
he  bought  the  book   and  stationery  business  of 

Joseph  Lomas,  and  until  July,  18S3,  was  contin- 
uously in  that  trade.  At  that  time  he  sold  out 
to  one  of  his  sons,  Nathan  S.  Smith,  who  now 
carries  on  the  business.  Mr.  Smith  leaves  three 
children,  two  sons  and  a  daughter,  all  of  whom 
are  married.  Speaking  of  his  htisiness  life  la 
Newburg,  the  Newburg  Daily  Jfumal  lays  :  "  It 
Is  stated  that  he  was  one  of  the  pioneer  merchant* 
on  Water  Street,  and  when  he  retired  over  a  year 
ago  he  left  no  one  on  the  street  who  was  in  busi- 
ness there  when  he  began.  When  Mr.  Smith 
first  opened  his  doors  for  trade,  Newbnrg  was 
then  but  a  small  village.  There  were  few  mer^ 
chants  on  Water  Street.  He  continued  on  in  a 
steady,  quiet  way,  growing  with  the  growth  ol  the 
place,  until  he  found  himself,  in  the  evening  of 
lite,  less  disposed  to  the  active  pursuits  than  he 
once  was,  and  left  to  enjoy  his  leisure  which  hip 
attention  to  business  brought  him  the  means  tu 
take.  Mr.  Smith's  store  has  for  year*  been  ■ 
great  resort  for  people  of  a  horticultural  turn. 
and  he  himself  bad  much  taste  for  pursuits  of 
that  nature.  He  also  has  been  for  about  s;  years 
the  Depositary  of  the  Newburg  Bible  Society." 
CHARL£S  MEGARGE. 

Charles  Meoakgb,  tbe  well-known  pa^r 
manufacturer  and  founder  and  head  of  the  nrai 
of  Charles  Megarge  i  Co.,  Philadelphia,  died 
October  a,  1883.  at  his  residence  in  Germanlown. 
He  was  born  in  Philadelphia,  September  30, 
1B04,  and  early  in  life  entered  the  p3p<:r  business 
in  the  store  of  Samuel  Ekstein.  In  1834  he 
and  his  brother  began  business  for  themselves 
as  C.  &  W.  H.  Megarge.  He  was  a  director 
and  from  1848  to  1866  was  president  of  the 
Germantown  Bank.  Mr.  Megarge  left  active 
business  life  several  years  ago,  at  tbe  time  of 
the  failure  of  his  firm. 

CORRECTION  OF  EDUCATIONAL 
CATALOGUE. 
Messrs.  Houghton,  Mifflin  &  Co.  wish  us 
to  say  that  it  was  their  intention  to  give  only 
the  retail  prices  of  their  educational  publica- 
tions in  the  Educational  Catalogue  for  1883, 
just  as  these  were  given  in  the  Catalogue  for 
1E89.  By  a  misunderstanding  their  Intro- 
ductory prices  were  inserted  as  net  whole- 
sale rates.  This  correction  is  made  for  the  in- 
formation of  those  dealers  who  handle  school- 
books,  and  who  find  a  discrepancy  between  the 
net  wholesale  rates  quoted  in  the  Educational 
Catalogue  and  the  prices  at  which  goods  are 
billed  to  them  by  Houghton,  Mifflin  &  Co.

NOTES  ON  AUTHORS. 

M.  E.  DE  PressensI^.  Is  preparing  a  new 
ediiion  of  his  "  Vie  de  Jfesus." 

Victor  Hugo  has  written,  under  the  title  of 
"  L'Archipel  de  U  Manche,"  a  description  of 
the  life  and  manners  of  the  inhabitants  of  the 
Channel  Islands.  It  will  be  brought  out  serially 
in  a  periodical. 

Prof.  James  Baldwin,  of  Huntington,  Ind., 
who  has  written  "The  Slory  of  Roland"  for 
Charles  Scribner's  Sons,  is  the  author  of  the 
admirable  work  on  "English  Literature,"  pub- 
lished by  John  ,E  Potter  &Co.,of  Philadelphia. 

Mrs.  J.  R.  Green,  the  widow  of  the  historian, 
has  herself  been  completely  revising  her  hus- 
band's "Conquest  of  England,"  according  to 
bis  last  instructions.  The  book  carries  on  the 
story  of  England  up  to  the  period  of  the  Norman 
Conquest. 

Madame  "Henri  GrCvjlle,  "  the  well- 
known  author  of  "  Dosia."  "Sonia,"  "  Un  Vio- 
lon  Russe,"  and  so  many  other  charming  novels 
describing  Russian  and  Norman  life,  is  about  to 
publish  a  work  entitled  "■ftconomie  Domcs- 
lique."  It  will  be  written,  says  the  London 
Athrnieum,  "  in  accordance  with  the  programme 
Issued  by  the  authorities  who  direct  the  '  en- 
aeignemcnt  secondaire  dcs  jcunes  filles '  in 
France.  But  Madame  Grfeville  will  not  confine 
herself  to  the  material  objects  to  which  the  pro- 
gramme calls  special  attention.  She  will  com- 
bine niih  her  practical  advice  to  the  future  house- 
wife much  of  thai  moral  teaching  which  renders 
so  valuable  to  young  Trench  girls — and.  indeed, 
to  all  girls,  whatever  may  be  their  nationality — 
her  excellent  little  work  entitled  'Instruction 
Morale  et  Civiquc  dcs  Jeunes  Filles,'  a  work  so 
truly  and  nubly  moral  and  civic  that  it  has  been, 
as  is  well  known,  honored  by  the  interdict 
of  the  Roman  Index."

story  by  Charlotte  M.  Yonge.  It  numbers  sixty- 
four  pages,  retailed  for  sixpence  in  London  and 
fifteen  cents  in  New  York.  Special  machines 
have  been  erected  (or  the  printing  of  this  maga- 
line  and  the  facilities  afforded  by  experience 
and  wealth  have  been  taken  advantage  of  in

Longman  &  Co.  have  in  press  a  work  by  Mr. 
C.  S.  Devas,  entitled  "The  Groundwork  of  Eco- 
nomics. "  Mr.  Devas  follows  the  historical 
method  of  the  German  economists,  but  takes 
Christian  ethics  for  a  foundation. 

Hamilto.v,  Adams  &  Co.,  London,  will  pub- 
lish at  once  a  volume  entitled  "Curious  Epi- 
taphs," collected  from  the  graveyards  of  Great 
Britain  and  Ireland,  with  biographical,  genealogi- 
cal, and  historical  notes,  by  Mr.  William 
Andrews,  of  Hull.  An  important  feature  ol  the 
book  will  be  "A  Biography  of  Epitaphs  "  extend- 
ing over  30  pages. 

Mr.  Redwav,  we  learn  from  the  LoHdan  Acad- 
tmy,  will  issue  very  shortly  a  verbatim  reprint  of 
the  article  00  George  Cruikshank  written  by

WtilmimUr  Kevum.  There  will  be  added  a

thorpc,  and  a  prefatory  note  by  the  editor,  Mr. 
W.  E.  Churcb,  on  Thackeray  as  an  artist  and 

Emile  deILavelbve's  recent  work  on  the 
"  Elements  of  Political  Economy"  is  being 
translated,  says  lhe  AihettaHtn  Met,  into  the  fol- 
lowing eight  languages :  English,  German, 
Russian,  Spanish,  Polish,  Servian,  Bulgarian, 
and  Greek.  His  short  sketch  of  the  life  of  Gar- 
field, originally  written  for  Swiss  schools,  ha* 
already  been  translated  into  Dutch,  Swedish, 
and  Portuguese. 

It  is  proposed  that  the  Paris  SocietS  Histori- 
que  et  Cercle  Saint-Simon  shall  publish  from  time 
to  time  inedited  documents  of  French  history  in  a 
uniform  manner.  Each  issue  will  constitute  a 
fiuillil  mobiU  [loose  sheet]  capable  of  being  in- 
serted in  its  proper  place  in  an;  historical  series, 
or  given  to  the  printer  without  inconvenience. 
Thus,  tbe  sheet  will  be  about  10x6,  sized  to 
allow  of  writing  upon  it,  printed  only  on  one 
side,  and  in  two  colunlns,  with  the  necessary 
information  as  to  the  origin  of  the  document. 
Each /thiV^/ will  fetch  two  centimes,  and  each 
volume  (of  150)  five  francs—*,  calculated) (l^lC

THE    UNION. 

By  William  H.  Srward.     Edited  by  George  E.  Baker.     Bvo.  (3. 

This  volume  Includes  the  diary  of  the  War  for  the  Union,  from  1861  to  1U5.  ahitliyT. 
Seward,  then  Secretary  of  State,  furnished  to  the  United  States  Hiaisiers  abroad  foi  iliei'  '■"• 
formation  concerning  the  contest. 

It  contains  also  selections  from  Mr.  Semrd's  Diplomatic  Correspondence,  embncisg ibc^ 
remarkable  dispatches  which  attracted  so  earnest  attention  at  home  and  abroad  ;  seTcrat  (A\^- 
papers,  never  before  published,  and  important  speeches. 

Prefixed  to  the  volume  is  a  memoir,  reciting  the  great  political  movemeats  which  occuiidi'. 
the  United  States  from  1B61  to  1869.  in  which  Mr.  Seward  bore  a  conspicuoui  pan.  with  icno::'  1 
of  bis  travels  round  the  globe,  and  of  the  occasional  speeches  which  he  made  alter  bis  letircst^  | 
(rom  office  in  1869  to  his  death  in  1873.

IN  PRESS: 

A  Nsw  Christmas  Story  by  Bdward  Everett  Hale. 

OUR    CHRISTMAS    IN    A    PALACE. 

READY  NOV.  loiA  ;

Car.  Mailing  the  best  of  Iheii  situation,  each  contributes  to  the  enjOTinent  of  (he  occasion,

D.  Afpleton  &  Co.  issue  this  week  Rhoda 
Broughtoa's  new  novel,  "  Belinda." 

George  A.  Leavitt  &  Co.  announce  that  the 
annual  Kuvember  Trade  Sale  of  books,  station- 
ery, stereotype  places,  etc.,  wilt  commence  No- 
vember 20.  Consignments  should  reach  the 
auctioneers  by  the  20th  inst. 

RoBEKTs  Bros,  have  just  ready  "  Seven  Span- 
ish Cities  and  the  Way  to  Them,"  by  Rev.  Edw. 
E.  Hale.  It  is  a  narrative  o(  the  author's  recent 
trip  to  some  of  the  most  important  cities  of  the 
kingdom,  comprising  Madrid.  CadU,  Seville, 
Cordova,  Toledo,  Malaga,  and  Xeres. 

SouLE  &  BiniHEE  have  just  ready  "  Elemcnu 
of  the  Law  of  Domestic  Relations,  and  of  Em- 
ployer and  Employed."  by  Irving  Browne,  a 
timely  and  valuable  worlc.  presenting  as  it  doe* 
a  brief  but  very  comprehensive  summary  ol  the 
body  of  the  law,  as  contained  in  the  more  vo- 
luminous and  exhaustive  works  of  Bi^op, 
Schoulcr,  and  Wood. 

White,  Stoke:^  &  Allen  issue  to-day  a  neat 
little  volume  for  the  benefit  of  gastronomists,  en- 
titled "  The  Oyster  Epicure  :  A  Collation  of  Au- 
thorities on  the  Gastronomy  and  Dietetics  of  the 
Oyster."  The  volume  contains  hints  as  to  when 
oysters  are  in  season,  how  to  serve  and  eat 
them,  what  to  drink  with  Ihem,  bow  many  to' 
eat,  etc.,  and  concludes  with  an  account  of  some 
famous  oyster  eaters  and  a  chapter  on  the  happi- 
ness of  the  oyster. 

James  R.  Osuood  &  Co.  have  just  published 
"  George  Eliot :  A  Critical  Study  of  her  Life, 
Writings  and  Philosophy,"  by  George  Willis 
Cooke,  who  treats  a  congenial  subject  in  an  able 
and  instructive  manner  ;  a  bii^raphy  of  John 
Greenleaf  Whittier,  by  Francis  H.  Underwood, 
who  has  written  the  work  with  the  full  sanction 
of  Mr.  Whittier,  and  who  has  succeeded  in  ob- 
taining much  new  mailer,  poems,  etc.,  and  a 
most  valuable  Whittier  bibliography.  They 
have  also  just  ready,  at  a  reduced  price,  a  new 
edition  of  J.  G.  Palfrey's  •'  History  of  New 
England,"  to  which  has  been  added  a  new  and 
complete  index,  which  greatly  adds  to  the  value 
of  this  standard  work. 

William  T.  Amies,  agent,  Phila.,  calls  at- 
tention to  several  illustrated  works  just  published 
by  him  in  sumptuous  style.  The  "  Fables"  of 
La  Fontaine,  translated  into  English  by  Walter 
Thornbury,  who  has  added  an  essay  on  La  Fon- 
taine's life  and  works,  and  also  a  sketch  of  .£sop, 
Che  great  Phrygian,  are  brought  out  in  a 
handsome  edition,  illustrated  with  one  hundred 
drawings  from  the  pencil  of  Gustave  Dor£,  print- 
ed from  entirely  new  plates,  on  paper  of  extra 
quality,  with  appropriate,  durable,  and  elegant 
bindings.  More  beautiful  still  is  Ihe  "  Dor6 
Family  Bible,"  In  quarto  siie,  with  hundreds  of 
added  pages  of  helpful  information,  copiously 
illustrated,  one  of  its  most  important  features 
being  the  "  Dor£  Bible  Gallery,"  which  is  also 
published  separately.  It  contains  one  hundred 
of  the  Bible  illustrations  with  an  explanatory 
page  to  each,  forming  a  pictorial  summary  of 
the  Bible  narrative  from  Genesis  to  Revelation, 
to  which  is  added  a  brief  sketch  of  Dore  and  an 
excellent  portrait  of  the  artist.  These  three 
works  are  brought  out  at  surprisingly  low  prices. 
The  "Fables"  and  "Bible  Gallery"  in  three, 
and  the  complete  "  Bible"  in  lix  new  and.  rick 
styles  of  binding.  1   .,^iOO^,^lL

A  VETERAN  PUBLISHER.

On  the  loth  of  September  Mr.  J.  C.  Derby 
celebrated  the  joth  anniversary  of  his  connection 
with  the  publishing  bualncss.  Some  zo  years 
ago  Mr.  Derby's  name  was  one  of  the  best 
known  in  publishing  circles  in  this  country,  for 
the  very  simple  reason  that  he  was  the  publisher 
of  the  most  successful  books  then  before  the 
public.  To-day  he  is  connected  with  the  bouse 
of  Houghton.  Mifflin  &  Co.,  ttnd  U  was  in  the 
handsome  parlors  of  this  firm  in  New  York  that 
I  found  him.  On  the  desk  before  him  was  an 
old  and  faded  sheet  of  paper,  with  the  ink,  once 
black,  now  gray.  The  way  this  sheet  was  folded 
and  addressed  on  the  hack  showed  that  the 
letter  was  written  before  the  days  of  envelopes. 
Mr.  Derby,  now  in  his  65th  year,  with  his  gray 
hair  and  sweeping  beard,  does  not  look  unlike 
^ichael   Angeio's   famous    Moses.      As   1   sat

author's  family.      Mrs.  Whitcher  was  (hen  dead.

liooo  was  accepted.  This  was  not  a  bad  arrange- 
ment for  the  publisher,  as  the  sale  very  sonn

wife  of  the  father  of  his  country.  Rev.  W.  H. 
Milburn,  "  the  blind  preacher,"  seems  to  be  the 
first  clergyman  on  Mr.  Derby's  list.  His  "  Rifle. 
Axe,  and  Saddle-Bags  "  and  "  Ten  Years  of  a 
Preacher's  Life"  were  both  published  by  Mr. 
Derby.  The  next  clergyman  on  his  list  was  Rev. 
Henry  Ward  Bcechcr,  whose  "Star  Papers" 
were  first  published  by  him.  The  "  Lectures  to 
Young  Men  "  had  been  published  by  a  Western 
publisher,  of  whom  Mr.  Derby  bought  the  right. 
In  i860  this  latter  book  had  reached  a  sale  of 
31,000  copies,  and  has  been  selling  ever  since. 
One  day  Mr.  Derby  was  sitting  in  his  office,  when 
Mrs.  Henry  Ward  Beechcr  entered  with  a  man- 
uscript under  her  arm.  She  looked  very  myste- 
rious as  she  laid  it  upon  Mr.  Derby's  desk,  and 
then  she  told  him  that  It  was  the  story  of  her  hus- 
band's and  her  Hfe  in  the  West,  "  I  want  you 
to  publish  it  and  not  to  say  a  word  to  Henry 
until  it  is  all  readytopul  on  the  market.  I  want 
to  surprise  him."  Mr.  Derby  replied  that  he 
would  be  pleased  to  publish  her  work,  but  that 
considering  its  nature,  he  did  not  think  it  would 
be  right  to  do  so  without  consulting  her  husband. 
She  seemed  disappointed  at  this,  hut  finally 
concluded  that  he  was  right.  So  the  next  time 
Mr.  Ueecher  called  at  his  office  he  said  ; 
"Mr.  Beccher  your  wife  has  written  a  book 
about  your  life  in  the  West,  and  she  wants  me 
to  publish  it.  Have  I  your  consent?"  "If 
you're  a  mind  to  be  such  a  fool."  said  Mr. 
Beecber,  with  a  laugh,  "but  don't  put  the  re- 
sponsibility on  my  shoulders."  Mr.  Derby,  who 
had  failh  in  Mrs.  Beecher's  ability,  published 
the  book.  The  success  of  "  From  Dawn  10  Day- 
light" is  well  known.  It  made  an  instant  hit. 
About  this  time  began  Mr,  Beecher's  acquain- 
tance with  Mr.  Rol>ert  Bonner,  which  was 
brought  about  by  Mr,  Derby.  "  1  learned 
through  Mrs.  Beecher,"  said  Mr.  Derby,  "  thai 
Mr.  Beccher  was  in  some  financial  distress  in 
relation  to  a  mortgage  on  his  house.  Incidental- 
ly I  spoke  of  this  to  my  friend  Robert  Bonner, 
who,  though  not  personally  acquainted  with  Mr. 
Beecher  at  that  time,  knew  him  by  reputation, 
and  was  one  of  his  many  admirers.  Mr.  Bonner 
seemed  very  much  interested  and  at  once  said  for 
mega  to  Mr.  Beecherandlotcllhim  that  he  would 
gladly  pay  of!  the  mortgage  if  he  would  allow 
him.  as  he  considered  it  a  disgrace  that  a  man  of 
Mr,  Beecher's  genius  and  distinction  should  suf- 
fer from  the  curse  of  debt,  I  bore  the  message 
to  Mr.  Beecher,  who  was  surprised  beyond 
measure,  particularly  as  there  was  no  such  thing 
as  security  or  pledge  asked  or  expected.  The 
amount,  $10,000,  Mr.  Bonner  handed  me  tn  cur- 
rency, and  it  was  deposited  in  the  Metropolitan 
Bank,  and  no  one  knew  of  this  transaction  at 
the  time  exccept  John  E.  Williams,  president  of 
ibc  bank,  and  the  parlies  directly  interested. 
Mr.  Beecher  subsequently  became  a  contributor 
lo  Ihe  Lidger,  but  without  any  reference  what- 
ever to  this  generous  act  on  the  part  of  Mr.  Bon- 
ner, which,  when  you  print  it.  will  be  for  the 
first  time  made  public." 

Of  successful  novels,  published  by  Mr.  Derby, 
"  The  Minister's  Wooing,"  by  Harriet  Beecher 
Slowe,  was  the  first.  His  next  success  was 
"Beulah,"  by  Augusta  J.  Evans.  One  day 
Miss  Evans  called  at  Mr,  Derby's  office  and  left 
Ihe  manuscripl.  which  he  took  home  and  read 
to  his   family,   who   all   thought   It  well   worth

publishiog.  Mr.  George  Ripley,  who  "  re&d" 
lor  him  un  a  salary,  as  well  as  for  otber  houMi, 
also  pronounced  favorably  aa  the  book,  and  it 
was  00  sooner  published  than  its  success  was 
made.  Few  novels  have  ever  reached  a  larger 
sale.  To  show  that  what  One  publisher  thinks 
good  is  not  necessarily  ihougfal  well  of  by 
another,  I  might  mention  that  "  Beulah"  was 
rejected  by  D.  Applelon  &  Co.,  their  reader, 
J.  W.  Palmer,  of  the  Baltimore  Exchange,  having 
pronounced  against  it.  The  lirst  review  of  the 
book  that  appeared  was  from  his  pen,  and  was 
condemnatory  ;  the  second  appeared  in  the  old 
Couritr  and  Inqvirer,  oi  M\^  city,  and  was  writ' 
ten  by  its  editor.  Mr.  Spaulding,  afterward  edi- 
tor 01  the  Times,  who  praised  it  in  the  strongest 
terms,  Soon  after  this  review  appeared.  Mr. 
Spaulding  wrote  to  Mr.  Derby,  and  asked  him 
who  was  the  author  of  Che  book,  and  if  it  would 
ttot  be  possible  for  him  to  meet  her.  He  had 
found  a  kindred  soul,  and  was  impatient  to  meet 
it  in  the  flesh.  Mr.  Derby  answered  that  the 
lady  was  a  Southerner,  but  thai  she  was  then  in 
New  York,  and  he  would  convey  Mr.  Spaulding's 
wishes  to  her  and  give  him  her  answer.  Miss 
Evans,  who  was  staying  at  the  Si.  Nicholas 
Hotel,  expressed  her  willingness  to  meet  Mr. 
Spaulding,  and  Mr.  Derby  brought  him  up  and 
introduced  him.  The  acquaintance  ripened  into 
friendship,  and  the  friendship  into  something 
more,  for  it  was  not  long  before  the  Northern 
editor  and  the  Southern  novelist  were  engaged 
to  be  married.  In  the  mean  time  the  civil  war 
broke  out.  Miss  Evans  was  a  secessionist  of 
the  strongest  type  and  Mr.  Spaulding  was  as 
Strang  for  th«  Union.  His  political  feelings, 
however,  did  not  interfere  with  his  private 
friendships,  and  tw  opportunity  offering,  he  went 
South  to  be  married.  To  his  surprise  he  found 
that  his  promised  bride  no  longer  (elt  the  same 
regard  for  him.  The  gulf  between  them  was 
Impassable,  and  so  they  parted.  Each  married 
later  on.  Miss  "Evans,  now  Mrs,  Wilson,  lives 
in  her  L  beloved  Southland.  Spaulding  is  no 
more.  "He  died  soon  after  his  marriage.  While 
the  war  was  raging.  Miss  Evans  wrote  another 
novel  called,  "  Macaria,"  which  was  pub-' 
lished  in  Richmond,  Va.  .  A  copy  of  Ibis  was 
sent  by  the  author  to  Mr.  Derby,  who  was  then 
temporarily  out  of  the  publishing  business. 
He,  however,  continued  bis  interest  in  this 
author,  and  he  took  the  book  to  Mr.  J.  B.  Lip- 
pincolt  to  see  if  he  would  not  publish  it.  Mr. 
Lippincott  at  once  agreed  to,  and  promised  the 
author  the  usual  10  per  cent  royalty,  though 
by  law  she  was  not  entitled  10  anything,  her 
book  having  been  published  in  (he  Confederacy. 
While  the  plates  were  being  made,  Michael 
Doolady.  of  this  city,  a  bookseller  of  practical 
methods,  got  hold  of  a  copy  of  "  Macaria,"  and 
was  getting  it  set  up  as  fast  as  the  printers  could 
work.  Mr.  Lippincott,  bearing  of  this,  wrote 
him  that  he  would  withdraw  from  the  lield  if  he, 
Doolady.  would  sign  a  contract  to  pay  him,  for 
the  author,  a  royalty  on  its  sales,  otherwise  he 
would  sell  the  plates  at  cost  to  anodier  publisher. 
Doolady  agreed  and  the  contract  was  signed.

of  reading  matter  and  illustrations,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superio: :: 
any  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore.  I 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  booksei--' 
who  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby.  |

men  illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  booki     |

price-list  of  the  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  Holiday  Li'  j 
erature.

Thomas  Nelson  &  Sons  have  just  ready  on 
edition  of  Coleridge's  "  Ancient  Mariner," 
with  characteristic  illustrations  by  David  Scott, 
and  a  life  of  the  artist  and  descriptive  notices  01 
the  piatei  by  the  Rev.  Dr.  A.  L,  Simpson. 

White,  Stokes  &  Allbn  announce  that  they 
will  shortly  issue  "  A  Daughter  of  the  Gods  ;  or. 
How  She  Came  into  Her  Kingdom,"  by  Charle* 
M.  CUy,  author  of  "The  Modern  Hagar." 
This  is  a  new  issue  of  Mrs.  Clark's  "  How  She 
Came  into  Her  Kingdom,"  formerly  published  by 
Jansen.  McClurg  &  Co.  This  house  will  here- 
after also  act  as  the  author's  publishers  for  her 
other  works. 

Wm.  R.  Jenkins.  N.  Y..  has  just  issued  "  L« 
Fte,"  a  pretty,  fanciful  one -act  comedy  by 
Octave  Feuillet,  which  forms  the  ilztb  number 
of  Z/  TAAIIrt  CoHlrmforain.  "  L'Abb*  Con- 
stanlin,"  Ludovic  Hal6vy's  very  charming  story, 
is  nearly  ready  and  will  be  published  during  the 
present  moolli.  It  forms  number  two  of  the 
scries,   Kamans   Cheiiis,   of   which  "  Dosia"   is 

Charles  Scribher's  Sons  will  publish,  on  the 
33d  inst.,  "  The  Merry  Adventures  of  Robla 
Hood,"  written  and  illustrated  by  Howard 
Pyle.  In  [his  book — perhaps  the  most  original 
and  elaborate  ever  produce!  by  an  American 
artist — Mr.  Pyle  has  lold  with  pencil  end  pen 
the  complete  and  consecutive  story  of  Robin 
Hood  and  his  merry  men  in  their  haunts  in 
Sherwood  forest,  gathered  from  the  old  ballads 
and  legends.  The  simple  English  ballad  haa 
been  most  successfully  preserved  in  Mr.  Pyle's 
easy  prose,  and,  as  regards  the  text,  this  edition 
is  in  all  respects  one  of  the  mast  complete  that 
has  ever  been  issued.  The  illustrations  by  Mr. 
Pyle  set  forth  most  grapfaicalty  every  eventful 
scene  in  the  nanaLve,  and  they  are  in  perfect 
keeping  with  the  story,  even  to  the  smallest 
detail.  The  leather  binding  and  general  make- 
up of  the  book  deserve  more  than  a  passing 
mention.  Every  detail  has  been  carefully  wrought 
out  by  the  auihor,  and  the  exterior  of  the  volume 
is  in  perfect  keeping  with  its  contents. 

Houghton,  Mifflik  Sc  Co.  will  publish  a 
notable  list  of  books  next  week.  The  one  likely 
10  make  the  most  immediate  impression  is  Miss 
Phelps's  story,  "  Beyond  the  Gates,"  a  story  of 
the  same  type  as  "  A  Little  Pilgrim,"  only  con- 
siderably longer  and  fuller.  It  is  said  to  be  ab- 
sorbingly interesting,  and  it  appeals  powerfully 
to  all  who  have  lost  dear  friends.  Mr.  Whit- 
tier's  new  book,  "The  Bay  of  Seven  Islands 
and  Other  Poems,"  will  be  heartily  welcomed  by 
a  host  of  readers ;  and  Mr.  Aldricb's  "  Mercedes 
and  Later  Lyrics"  will  be  so  handsome  a  book 
that  it  ought  to  be  in  great  demand  even  If  the 
drama  and  poems  it  contains  were  far  less  ex- 

Suisiteinliterary  an  than  they  are.  Herbert  Tut- 
e's  "History  of  Prussia  to  the  Accession  of 
Frederic  the  Great"  promises  to  be  a  positive 
addition  to  our  best  historical  literature,  as  it 
is  the  fruit  of  thorough  research  into  a  very  in- 
(eiesling  period  and  people.  "  He  and  She  :  A 
Poet's  Portfolio."  is  a  dainty  little  vellum-cov- 
ered volume  by  W,  W.  Story,  containing  many 
graceful  lyrics  connected  and  Introduced  by  a 
brilliant  dialogue.  The  last  book  on  this  list  is 
"  Examples  for  Elementary  Practice  in  Delinea^ 
tion,"  a  book  for  schools  and  home  students  of 
drawing,  by  Charles  H.  Moore,  Iastniei«y|». 
Drawing  In  Harvard  University.    ,--^^'-'^1"-

REMINISCENCES  OF  JOHN  KEESE. 

rn<K-y*hnKtKii,  WitandLillirattur:'    (AffUloo 


I  HAVE  preserved  a  record  of  a  booksellers' 
dinner— a  "  complimentary  eniertainmeni  by 
the  booksellers  of  New  York  to  autboi»  and 
other  liLerary  and  distinguished  men"— given  at 
the  Oily  Hotel.  March  3a  iS37.  This  was  over 
focty-five  years  ago,  and  few,  if  any,  indeed  of 
of  the  company  then  met  are  now  living.  Yet 
there  may  be  descendants  of  some  of  Ihem  who 

familiar  and  dear  to  them  appear. 

David  Fell  presided  on  the  occasion,  assisted 
by  Fletcher  Harper  and  others  as  vice-presidents  ; 
George  Dearborn,  a  well-known  publisher,  offici- 
ated as  master-of-ceremonies.  and  Mr.  Keese  as 
toast-master.  He  had  then  been  of  the  firm  of 
Collins.  Keese  &  Co,  (or  more  than  a  year,  and 
his  genial  qualities  had  gained  him  many  friends 
among  the  hooksellcrs  and  publishers  of  New 
York  and  elsewhere. 

Among  the  entertainers  and  guests  were  Al- 
bert Gallatin,  Chancellor  Kent,  Colonel  Trum- 
bull, Washinnton  Irving,  William  Cullen  Bryant, 
Fit!-Gteene  Halleck,  Rev.  Orville  Dewey,  James 
K.  Paulding,  William  L.  Stone.  Harrison  Gray, 
James  Harper,  Charles  King,  Major  Noah, 
Philip  Hone,  John  W.  Francis,  Lewis  Gaylord 
Clark,  George  P.  Morris,  Edgar  A,  Poe, 
Richard  Adams  Locke.  George  P.  Putnam, 
Henry    Inman,    J.    G.    Chapman,    and    many 

The  opening  address,  previous  to  introducing 
the  regular  toasts,  was  delivered  by  Mr.  Keese  ; 
and  in  the  course  of  it.  he  spoke  with  much  feeling 
of  the  occasion  which  had  brought  Ihem  together, 
and  of  the  fraternal  relation  between  author  and 
publisher,  which  it  was  designed  to  foster  and 
maintain  for  mutual  benefit.  Glancing  at  the 
progress  of  American  literature,  and  viewing 
the  animating  prospect  of  the  future,  he  said  : 


"  When  we  survey  the  geographical  pocilioo  rf 
these  United  Ststei—whcn  wc  behold  our  nsi

The  speech  of  Dr.  Francis  called  forth  the 
warmest  plaudits.  It  was  full  of  humorous  sug- 
gestion, and  eminently  characteristic  of  the  man. 
Washington  Irving,  in  proposing  the  health  of 
Samuel  Rogers,  the  banker-poet,  referred  to  a 
letter  he  had  received  the  previous  day  from  Mr. 
Rogers,  acknowledging  the  receipt  of  a  volume 
of  Halleck's  poems,  and  speaking  of  them  in  the 
highest  terms.  Of  "Marco  Bozzaris"  and 
"  Alnwick  Castle,"  "  They  are  better  than  any- 
thing we  can  do  just  now  on  our  side  of  the  At- 
lantic," said  Rogers.

many  others.

periodical  for  nearly  five  years.  He  promise* 
di■cuasion^  reviews,  and  criticiams  by  the  bcM

liieiature  uf  a  '  clean,  noble,  fascinating,  and

NOTES  ON  AUTHORS. 

Sfielhagen's  nent  novel  will  be  entitled 
"Uhlenhans,"  and  will  appear  very  shortly. 

Ernst  von  Wolzogen  will  contribute  the 
sixth  volume  to  Auerbach's  "  Literary  Portrait 
Gallery."  It  will  deal  with  George  Eliot,  and 
will  be  the  first  critical  estimate  of  her  published 
in  book-form  in  German. 

Senator  Blaine,  says  the  Washington  spe- 
cial correspondent  of  the  New  York  Jfor/if,  "has 
neaily  completed  bis  history  of  'From  Lincoln 
to  Garfield.'  He  will  then  begin  upon  a  history 
of  the  war  of  1812.  No  satisfactory  history  con- 
fined to  this  interesting  period  has  ever  been 
written.  Mr.  Blaine  proposes  to  fill  up  this  gap 
in  our  history,  and  has  already  gathered  a  mass 
of  material  for  it." 


BUSINESS  NOTES. 
Chicago,  III. — I.  A.  Hopkins,  formerly  pro- 
prietor of  the  Franklin  Book -store  at  Washington, 
where  he  earned  an  excellent  trade-standing,  has 
now  established  himself  in  Chicago,  and  writes 
very  hopefully  of  tbe  new  field  he  has  chosen. 
We  call  attention  to  his  advertisement,  in  whicb 
he  asks  publishers  to  mail  him  their  catalogues 
and  trade-lists.  Mr.  Hopkins  expects  to  control 
the  trade  within  five  hundred /milea  of -iWastem

of  J.  B.  Lippincoit  &  Co.  all  the  remaining

■WILL    PUBLISH   NEXT   WEEK: 

THE  BAY  OF  SEVEN  ISLANDS  AND  OTHER  POEMS. 

By  John  G.  Whiltier.     WUh  Ponrait.     I  vol.,  l6mo,  giU  top,  (i. 

Hr.  Whittler  gatheri  in  ibii  volume  the  poem«  he  has  written  since  the  publication  of  "The 
King's  MiHire  "  in  1S81. 

BEYOND  THE  GATES. 

B7  Elizabeth  Stuart  Phelpa,  author  of  "  The  Gates  Ajar,"  etc.     i^mo,  %t.%%. 

Ever  since  "  The  Gates  Ajar "  was  published,  many  of  the  readers  of  thai  remarkable  book 
have  hoped  Miss  Phelps  would  giveihem  a  fuller  vision  of  the  future  life,  o(  which,  in"  The  Gates 
Ajar"  she  only  f[3ve  a  glimpse.  This  hope  is  now  to  be  gratified.  The  subject  of  "  Beyond  the 
Gates"  is  sufficiently  indicated  by  its  title.  It  is  a  stoiy  of  the  unseen  world,  o(  its  possible 
activities,  illumination,  and  experiences,  as  a  reverent  imagination  forEsees  them.  Though  very 
different  from  "A  Little  Pilgrim,"  and  not  suggested  by  that  wonderful  sketch,  like  that  It 
appeals  to  the  hopes  and  longings  of  all  who  have  lost  dear  friends,  and  who  seek  to  realize  the 
life  into  which  they  have  entered. 

^F  The  Jirtt  large  ediHim  it  already  exMauilid,  and  a  second  largi  edttitm  it  new  frinling. 

MERCEDES  AND  LATER  LYRICS. 

Bj  ThoinM  Baiter  Aldrich,  author  of  "  Marjorie  Daw,"  "  Flower  and  Thorn,"  etc.     Beautifully 
printed  on  high  quality  paper,     i  vol.,  i6mo,  ti.aj. 

"  Mercedes  "  is  a  historical  drama  of  admirable  spirit  and  grace,  baaed  on  a  very  itrlking 
incident  of  a  Spanish  campaign  while  Napoleon  was  Emperor.  The  remaining  contents  of  the 
•volume  include  many  of  the  lyrics  written  hy  Mr.  Aldrich  during  the  last  seven  years.  Readers 
of  modern  poetry  do  not  need  to  be  told  that  these  seven  yean  have  produced  very  few  lyrics 
'worthy  to  he  compared  with  those  which  ate  embraced  in  this  book. 

THE  HISTORY  OF  PRUSSIA  TO  THE  ACCESSION 
OF  FREDERIC  THE  CREAT. 

■By  Herbert  Tuitte,  A.M.,  Professor  In  Cornell  University,  author  of  "  German  Political  Lead- 

ers."  etc.     1  vol.,  crown  Svo,  (3.35. 

Mr  Tuttle  spent  several  years  io  Berlin,  studying  with  the  utmost  care  the  Germany  of  the 
past  and  of  the  present.  In  this  volume  he  narrates  the  history  of  Prussia  from  the  remotest 
records  until  Frederic  the  Great  ascended  the  throne.  It  is  a  very  interesting  siory,  and  is  not 
merely  a  history  of  the  sovereigns  and  of  the  military  operations  in  which  Prussia  engaged,  but 
of  the  people,  of  the  development  of  its  poliiical,  social,  and  religious  institutions,  and  both  in  its 
design  and  in  its  execution  it  will  lake  rank  with  the  more  important  of  modern  historical  works. 

HE  AND  SHE:  A  POET'S  PORTFOLIO. 

By  William  W.  Story,  author  of  "  Roba  di  Roma,"  etc.     1  vol.,  l6mo,  vellum  cover,  (i. 

In  this  volume  Mr.  Story  has  collected  many  of  his  lyrical  poems,  and  strung  them  on  the 
thread  of  an  entertaining  dialogue  or  conversation  between  a  gentleman  and  a  lady.  The  variety 
of  theme  and  the  grace  of  treatment  render  the  volume  unusually  atiraciive,  while  the  conversa- 
>tioD  abounds  in  brilliant  and  suggestive  passages  wltich  add  to  the  value  and  charm  of  the  work.

NOTICE  TO  BOOKSELLERS. 

Ths  Litbsarv  News  for  Christm^  1S83,  will  embrace  thirty-two  piges 
■of  reading  matter  and  illustrations,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superior  to 
.any  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore. 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  booksdiK 
who  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby. 

The  chief  attraction  of  the  forthcoming  issue  will  be  the  numerous  sp«.- 
men  illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  books. 

The  most  valuable  feature,  besides  the  Literary  NoteSi  will  be  a  special 
price-list  of  the  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  Holiday  Lit- 
erature. 

To  those  who  desire  to  reach  a  wider  public  than  they  feel  they  can  a£o'^ 
to  seek  through  the  more  expensive  Christmas  Num,ber  of  the  PuBLtSHCtj 
Weekly,  the  Christmas  Number  of  the  Literary  News  is  recommended  as  U)' 
ibest  possible  medium  to  cultivate  a  holiday  trade.

The  system  of  "job  lots"  has  introduced  into 
'  the  business  an  element  of  uncertainty  and  of 
dissalisf action,  which  has  done  more  barm  than 
anything  else  within  the  present  century.'  The 
co-operative  stores  have  also  acted  baneftilly 
upon  the  bookselling  trade.  We  heard  of  an  in- 
,  stance  of  this  kind  in  a  northern  town  quite  re- 
'  cently.  A  well-known  and  old-established  book- 
\  seller  had  'been  in  the  habit  of  supplying  the 

Medical  School  for  many  years. 
'  Recently  an  enterprising  firm  of  drapers  open- 
I  ed  what  they  called  a  "  Bon  Marchi,"  and  the 
j  students  no  doubt  thought  that  they  might  as 
I  well  apply  the  "  Bon  Marchi"  principle  to  tbe 
I  purchase  ol  books.  Inquiries  were  insiituied, 
and  it  was  found  that  this  draper's  store  iraa 
supplying  nearly  the  whole  of  the  books,  and 
they  had  applied  themselves  so  actively  to 
this  business  as  actually  to  obtain  telephonic 
I  communication  between  their  "  Bun  Marchi" 
I  and  the  Medical  School.  Of  course  here  was  an 
I  important  branch  of  liusiness  completely  lost  to 
'  the  legitimate  bookseller. 

I  It  would  seem,  too.  that  in  the  bookfelling 
I  business  we  shall  soon  have  the  same  anoma- 
lies as  in  the  music-selling  trade.  The  marked 
price  of  a  sheet  of  music,  it  is  well  known, 
gives  no  indication  whatever  of  its  actual  price, 
wt.ich  may  be  one  third,  or  even  one  fourth. 
If  the  music-seller  does  not  know  the  current 
value  of  the  particular  sheet  be  may  suffer  very 
serious  loss  both  in  money  and  reputation.  If 
books  are  to  be  sold  at  threepence,  fourpence, 
and  even  fivepencein  the  shilling  off  their  pub- 
lished price,  according  to  the  facilities  which  the 
seller  has  for  buying  cheaply,  it  is  obvious  that 
a  bookseller  has  no  security  against  his  stock 
being  seriously  depreciated  from  causes  over 
which  he  has  no  control.  Tbe  twopence  in  the 
shilling  is  now  universal,  even  when  credit  is 
given.  Formerly  the  concession  was  only  made 
when  cash  was  paid,  but  now  tbe  competitioa  ia

the  trade  has  become  so  keen,  thai  booksellers 
are  farced  10  give  three  or  six  months'  credit  and 
at  the  end  10  allow  tbii  discount. 

The  lesull  of  this  is  that  very  few  booksellers 
at  the  present  day  confine  themselves  to  a. 
leptimale  trade  in  books.  They  sell  stationery, 
pictures,  and  fancy  articles,  very  often  relying 
on  these  lor  their  proht.  This  being  the  case, 
a  bookseller  cannot  be  enpected  to  devote  the 


buyer  formerly  expected  of  hira. 

Old  booksellers  even  huve  degenerated. 
They  ate  now  frequently  mere  dealers  in  re- 
mainders, or  brokers  nho  attend  sales  and  buy 
books  as  furniture  brokers  buy  articles  of  furni- 
ture. It  is  noticeable  that  in  nearly  all  the 
large  towns  the  number  uf  bookstalls  la  decreas- 
iog,  and  the  number  of  catalogues  issued  by  the 
body  becomes  less  and  Ies?i. 

In  these  days  of  unrestricted  trade  it  is.  of 
course,  quite  useless  to  propose  that  the  pub- 
lishers should  be  compelled  to  sell  only  to  rec- 
ognized dealers.  A  proposition  was  made 
lately  to  the  effect  that  publishers  who  sold  to 
drapers  should  be  boycotted,  but  this  would  be 
manifestly  impracticable,  for  those  who  wished 
to  obtain  books  in  quantities  could  always  get 
them  by  indirect  methods.  It  is  well  known 
that  the  large  buyers  who  sell  at  such  a  great 
reduction  from  the  published  price  almost  in- 
variably pay  cash,  while  the  ordinary  bookseller 
expects  to  have  three  or  six  months'  credit. 
Herein,  we  think,  lies  an  opportunity  for  im- 
proving the  condition  o(  the  trade. 

1(  booksellers  proper  would  pay  cash,  they 
coold  obtain  the  same  favorable  terms  as  Iheir 
non-legitimate  competitors.  And  if  they  again 
were  to  sell  for  cash  no  embarassment  would 
(dlow  from  the  practice. 

Long  credits,  indeed,  are  the  bane  of  the  book- 
selling business.  Until  the  book'buyers  are 
accustomed  to  pay  for  their  books  us  regularly 
as  they  settle  their  butcher's  bills,  ihe  book- 
seller is  placed  at  a  disadvantage,  and  we  think 
IS  unfairly  treated.  The  hrsi  step,  therefore, 
toward  improving  the  standing  of  the  trade. 
would  be  to  introduce  cash  payments  all  round, 
and  if  this  could  be  generally  followed,  it  woutil 
initiate  a  reform  of  the  most  valuable  character. 

We  think  that  booksellers,  as  a  rule,  are  not 
as  "  pushing"  as  they  might  be.  The  journals 
contain  very  few  advertisements  from  book- 
sellers, compared  with  those  of  the  rest  of  (he 
trading  community. 

Yet  the  bookselling  business  is  one  that  can 
be  developed  to  an  almost  unlimited  extent  if 
actively  and  energetically  followed.  Of  course, 
the  difficuliy  consists  in  the  (act  that  a  bookseller 
has  to  advertise  another  tradesman's  commodity, 
and  he  may  not  receive  any  reward  for  his  entei- 

If,  for  instance,  a  bookseller  in  any  town 
were  to  spend  twenty  pounds  in  acquainting  the 
public  with  the  tact  that  anew  book  by  Mr.  Ten- 
nyson were  on  sale  at  his  establishment,  he  has 
no  guarantee  that  the  public  wiit  not  buy  the 
book  from  some  other  shop, 

A  grocer  who  advertises  tea  of  course  never 
tilinks  of  stating  Ihe  name  of  the  wholesale  firm 
from)  which  he  obtained  it,  but  the  bookseller  is 
in  a  different  position.  Even  allowing  the  ex- 
istence of  Ibis  difficulty  we  think  that  it  would  be 
advisable  for  the  trade  to  adopt  more  of  the  or- 
dinary expedients  for  increasing  business.

ajo  editions  of  the  Greek  New  Testament,

N'eir  Testament,  critically  as  well  as  bibliographi-

rangement. It  Hi  arranged  in  twelve  chapters, 
.'losing  with  u  chronulogical  list  and  an  index. 
The  work  alrauods  in  historical  and  biographical 
□formation,  and  will  prove  invaluable  to  the 
icholar,  no  less  Chan  to  the  librarian  and  the

landsome  volume,  the  retail  price  of  which  is

Time,"  by  Sir  N.  W.  Wraxall.  who  shares  with

sources  of  information  for  the  social  and  political 
history  of  Ihe  l3th  century— Ihe  days  of  Pltl,

(from  bcr  autograph  MS,),  the  editor,  H.  B. 
Wheatly,  etc.  They  also  announce  a  new  edi-

ings, consisting  of  portraits,  pieces  of  armor,

G.  P.   PUTNAM'S  SONS,  NE^V  YORK, 

Publish  the  only  authorized  editions  and  the  only  ones  which  are  or  which  can  be  complete, 
tml  -which  contain  the  author's  final  revisions  and  additions.  Recent  announcements  of 
vis  of  "  Irving' s  Works"  in  "  four"  or  in  "six  volumes"  are  calculated  to  mislead 
'mkscllers  and  bookbuyers.  Irving'  s  complete  works  are  fully  protected  by  copyright,and 
siuh"sets"  can  contain  only  fragmentary  and  unrevised  portions  of  the  earlier  writings.

William  R.  Jenkins,  N.  Y,.  has  just  issued 
a  new  revised  and  enlarged  edition  of  Clarke's 
work  on  "  Horses'  Teeth."  As  the  work  has 
been  augmented  also  by  illustrations,  the  price 
has  been  raised  to  $2. 

J.  B.  LiPPiNCOTT  &  Co.  announce  for  im- 
mediate publication  Mary  Agnes  Tinclier's  new 

ivel,  "The  Jewel  in  the  Lotos,"  with  five  hand- 

me  full-paRe  illustrations  by  Hovenden.  This 
story  is  considered  by  critics  the  most  powerful 
the  author  has  yet  written. 

CurrLEs,  Upham  &  Co.  have  jusi  ready 
"  Poems  in  Prose,"  by  Ivan  TourgucneH,  with 
fine  portrait  frontispiece  and  an  introduction. 
The  "Poems"  are  the  ttiaiure  products  of  the 
author's  genius,  and  are  admirably  adapted  to 
indicate  the  scope  and  tendency  of  the  great 
novelist's  wonderful   powers  of  description  and 

A.  D.  F.  Randolph  &  Co.  will  issue  imme- 
diately "Sourccsof  History  in  the  Pentateuch," 
six  lectures  delivered  in  Princeton  Theological 
Seminary,  on  the  Stone  Foundation,  1882.  by  Dr. 
Samuel  C.  Bartlell,  President  of  Dartmouth 
College.  They  have  also  made  arrangements 
with  the  author  to  publish  at  once  "  The  yoke 
of  Christ,  in  the  Duties  and  Circumstances  of 
Life."  by  Rev.  Dr.  Anthony  W.  Thorold.  The 
author  discusses  in  this  volume  the  questions  of 
friendship,  money,  marriage,  illness,  toss  of 
friends,  and  letter- writing. 

J.  R.  Osc.ooti  &  Co.  have  ready  this  week, 
■'  Red-Letler  Days  Abroad,"  by  John  L.  Stod- 
dard, a  handsomely  illustrated  volume  of  travel, 
dealing  chielly  with  the  most  picturesque  and  re- 
markable localities  in  Spain,  the  Tyrol,  and  the 
Imperial  cities  of  Russia;  "  Nighta  with  Uncle 
Remus  ■  Myths  and  legends  of  the  Old  Planta- 
tion," by  Joel  Chandler  Harris,  another  of  those 
charming  books  of  Southern  legends  and  dialect, 
in  which  "  Brer.  Rabbit"  becomes  the  hero  of 
new  adventures  even  more  exciting  than  the 
others.  The  quaint  and  piquant  illustrations  by 
Church  and  Beard  add  greatly  to  the  reader's 
interest;  "  Vagabondia,"  formerly  ptiblished 
under  ihe  title  of  "  Dolly,"  by  Mrs.  Frances 
Hodgson  Burnett,  is  also  ready. 

HouoiiTON,  Mifflin  &  Co.  will  publish  next 
week,  in  the  series  of  Amirican  CBmmeniB/allit, 
a  volume  on  Oregon,  by  Rev.  William  Barrows,

printed  in  a  brown  tint,  making  an  exceedingly 
attractive  volume,  aside, from  Ihe  illustrations

JOURNALISTIC  NOTES, 
The  BibHttheta  Sacra  Is  to  follow  the  injunc- 
tion, "  Go  West,"  and  will  henceforth  be  edited 
and  published  in  Oberlin. 

Mrs.  Jessie  Benton  Fremont  has  a  very  en- 
tertaining paper  in  the  November  Widt  Axoaht, 
entitled  "My  Arizona  Class,"  in  which  she 
gives  an  account  of  her  work  in  the  Presciil 
School,  at  the  time  when  General  Fremont  was 
Governor  of  Arizona.  "A  Brave  Girl  "is  the 
title  of  the  serial  story  which  Miss  Elizabeth 
Stuart  Plielps  is  preparing  for  IVidi  Axuaki. 
The  scene  opens  at  Smith  College. 

The  Mai^atint  0/  Art  begins  a  new  volume 
with  the  December  issue.  In  entering  upon  its 
seventh  year  of  publication,  the  publishers  state 
that  no  effort  or  expense  will  be  spared  on  their 
pari  to  make  the  magazine  in  the  future,  what 
it  has  been  in  the  post,  "the  most  widely  es- 
teemed magazine  of  its  kind  in  eiistancc."  lis 
aim,  as  heretofore,  will  be  the  popularization  of 
art  and  the  gratification  of  the  growing  inter- 
est of  all  classes  in  whatever  is  beautiful  and 
refined.  It  is  not  only  for  the  artist  and  stu- 
dent, but  for  every  cultivated  reader  who  desires 
to  keep  informed  on  art  and  its  progless  in  all 
branches  and  in  all  countries.  Its  scope  em- 
braces not  only  the  puie  ideal  <□  painting  and 
sculpture,  but  the  practical— large  space  being 
devoted  10  decorative  and  industrial  art,  and  the 
many  forms  in  which  art  enters,  or  should  ; 
enter,  into  our  daily  life.  I

THE  LUTHER  BIOGRAPHIES. 
Elmir  In  tiki  Bttnt  Herald,  Oct.  tS. 

Martin  Luther  seems  to  be  the  chief  (euure 
if  ihe  literature  ot  tb«  day.  Pnblitbera  Are 
training  the  capacities  oE  their  presses  to  get 
heir  lives  and  whM  not  ready  for  the  linnlver- 
ary  on  the  itxh  of  November.  Some  of  them 
a.ve  gone  so  far  as  to  bring  out  very  slovenly  pub- 
ications,  which  have  little  more  than  the  name 
f  Luther  OD  their  title-page  to  recommend  them, 
'he  stratiKC  pan  of  this  is  that  the  five  books 
noounced  or  already  published  are  all  bj  the 
line  matt.  Prof.  Julius  KOstlio,  and  fouDdedoo 
ne  book.  At  leaat  two  years  ago  Prof.  KOstlin 
rote  a  life  of  Luther  tliat  was  at  once  accepted 
I  the  authority  on  matters  of  Lutheran  history. 

short  time  ago  be  prepared  a  popular  edition 
'  this  somewhat  voluminous  work,  to  which 
ucb    important  matter  was  added  in  tbe  way 

illastration.  A  number  of  portraits  that  had 
:ver  beeo  touched  by  an  engraver's  tool  were 
und  and  reproduced  and  a  fac-sitnite  of  the  fa- 
OU9  bull  that  excommunicated  Luther,  a  copy 

one  of  the  "indulgences"  against  which  he 
ide  war.  a  page  from  his  Bible  interlined  in 
i  ovrn  autograph,  a  copy  of  his  will,  and  no 
d  (o  interesting  bits.  A  tran^ation  of  Ibis 
ok  bas  been  made  under  the  supervision  of 
;  author,  and  it  is  this  edition  that  will  be  pub- 
tted  bere,  with  all  its  illustrations,  Eac-similes, 
j  portraits,  by  Messrs.  Charles  Scribner's 
ns  simultaneonsly  with  its  appearance  in  Lon- 
1.  It  will  be  issued  as  near  Che  loih  of  No- 
nber  as  possible.  The  Lutheran  Society  of 
iladelphla,  impatient  of  delay,  and  apprcciat- 
the  value  of  timeliness,  has  brought  out  a 
cbed'up  edition  of  this  book,  edited  by  John  D. 
rris,  of  Philadelphia,  and  translated  by  various 
id9  so  as  to  save  lime.     The  variety  of  hands 

not  added  to  the  literary  value  of  the  work, 
having  no  arrangement  with  the  German 
liisber,  it  was  impossible  to  gel  the  illustra- 
■^.  irhieh  are  an  Important  feature  of  the 
Inal.  wfibont  going  to  great  expense.  So 
r  bOiVe  thrown  in  oU  the  cuts  they  could  find 


bearing  any  relation  to  Luther  or  his  time,  and 
they  are  in  the  market  before  the  aulboclied  edi- 
tion. When  Prof.  KOstlin  made  the  edition  of 
Hblch  I  speak,  it  took  so  well  that  he  was  at  once 
solicited  to  bri[ig  out  an  abridged  edition  of  his 
book,  but  having,  perhaps,  got  a  little  tired  of 
writing  lives  of  Luther  by  this  time,  he  engaged 
some  one  else  to  make  Ihe  abridgment  under  his 
supervision,  and  it  is  this  that  Messrs.  Cassell 
ft  Co,  announce.  Messrs.  Funk  &  Wagnalls 
have  also  another  from  the  same  source,  so  it 
will  be  seen  that  but  lor  Prof.  Julius  KSsttln  ne 
would  be  in  a  bad  way  (or  Luther  material. 
There  are  other  books  on  this  subject,  however, 
I  have  before  me  a  new  edition  of  the  "  Homes 
and  Haunts  of  Luther,"  by  Dr.  Jobn  Sloughton, 
which  may  be  read  to  advantage  in  connection 
with  Kliatlin's  life.  Dr.  Stoughlon  has  travelled 
carefully  over  Luther's  footsteps  and  gathered 
illustrations  ol  all  the  places  that  may  now  be 
seen.  And  here  is  another  Luther  book  of 
a  different  son,  "  Luther  and  the  Cardinals," 
a  historic  biographical  tale,  by  Julie  Sutter. 
Still  another  Luther  work  is  being  prepared  by 
Dr.  Leonard  Bacon  for  the  Messrs.  Scrlbncr. 
It  is  the  "Luther  Hymn-Book,"  and  gives  the 
words  and  music  ot  Luther's  hymns,  some  36  in 
all,  taken  from  original  sources.  The  words  are 
given  in  German  as  well  as  English.

iVOTlCE   TO  BOOKSELLERS. 

The  Literary  News  for  Cliristmas,  1^83,  will  embrace  thirtj-two  pK- 
of  reading  matter  aad-illustraiions,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superoM 
any  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore. 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  i»ok«^ -■ 
who  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby. 

The  chief  attraction  of  the  forthcoming  issue  will  be  the  numerous  sj*;- 
men  illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  boolis. 

The  most  valuable  feature,  besides  the  Literary  Notes,  will  beaspeci 
price-Iist  of  tlie  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  HolidavL- 
erature. 

To  those  who  desire  to  reach  a  wider  public  than  they  feel  they  can  afi-- 
to  seek  through  the  more  expensive  Christmas  Number  of  the  Publish* 
Weekly,  the  Christmas  Number  of  the  Literary  News  is  recommended  iS '-' 
best  possible  medium  to  cultivate  a  holiday  trade.

MICHAEL  ANGELO. 

A  DRAMATIC  POEM. 
By  HENRY  WADSWORTH   LONGFELLOW. 

The  ccDlral  idea  of  this  poem  is  the  love  of  Michael  Aneelo  for  Vittoria  Colonna,  ud,

ognized  as  one  of  the  noblest  of  his  longer  pieces.  Throughout  the  poem  there  is  a  tender 
and  pathetic  charm,  which  is  a  peculiar  characteristic  of  X^ngfellow's  work.

"RED-LETTER  DAYS  ABROAD." 


Toledo,  Cordova,  gay  Seville, — the  home  of  bull-fights  and  miudora, "  Figaro"  and  "  Carmen, 
the  Moorish  city  of  Granada,  immoicaliied  by  Irving  in  his  "  Tales  of  Ihe  AJhambra,"  villi  il! 
comparable  architecture  and  Oriental  beauty,  are  all  graphically  portrayed.  The  Passion  Play 
witnessed  by  Mr.  Stoddard  at  its  last  representation  in  iSSo,  and  Russia  was  visited  by  bim 
before  the  late  Ciar's  assassination.  The  Passion  Play  is  described  in  detail,  and  many  inlcres 
facta  related  B.boitt  the  actors,  while  "  tlie  cities  of  the  Czar,"  with  ttieir  tnao]'  hinorjcal  assc 
lions,  urc  placed  before  the  reader  in  picturesque  guise. 

Our  object  in  going  into  the  minutix  of  the  text  has  been  not  only  to  point  out  its  claim 
special  attention,  but  also  to  shoiv  the  varied  character  of  the  pictures,  which  have  been  mod 
interpret  and  illustrate  every  phase  of  il.  In  the  Passion  Play  almost  every  scene  is  reprodi 
by  the  artist  with  correct  portraits,  in  costume,  of  the  actors.  The  Russian  scenes  embrace  1 
interior  views  of  celebrated  palaces,  landscapes,  famous  buildings,  churches,  and  groups  of  st 
ary.     "  Travels  in  sunny  Spain  "  is  rich  in  gypsies,  bulls  and  bull. fights,  toreadors  and  picado: 

The  Messrs.  Osgood  &  Co.  are  fortunate  in  having  a  volume  like  this  to  offer  to  the  hoi: 
trade.  The  artistic  character  of  the  illustrations,  specimens  of  which  we  reprodnce— the  vign 
being  the  Church  of  the  Nativity,  Moscow — the  fascination  of  the  text,  the  elegance  of  the  pi 
and  typography,  and  Ihe  richncES  of  the  binding,  make  it  altogether  an  exceptional  one.

Christmas  Number. 


GRAY'S   '•  ELEGY." 

Thomas  Grav's  repuutlon  resu  upon  a  single  poem  wriaen  almost  one  hundred 
fears  ago.  Hii  entire  poelical  works  would  scarcely  fill  a  volume,  and  yet  oul  of  » 
number  Utile  Is  remembered  aside  from  the  "  Elegy."  This  is  in  itself  a  gem  which  dese 
has  won  immortality.  No  anthology  is  without  it,  and  no  reader  of  even  moderate  cuUu 
like  to  profess  Ignorance  of  it.  It  Is  not  only  the  smoothness  and  elegance  of  il 
perfect  versification  which  have  given  it  an  exceptional  place  in  litetaturB.  but  the  tendt 
choly  of  its  sentiment  and  its  closeness  to  every-day  life  and  philosophy.  The  melanchi; 
marks  it  lor  her  own  would  seem  to  be  a  reflection  of  the  author's  own  sad.  rctrospeciivi 
tbc  result  of  an  unhappy  early  life,  which  neither  collegiate  nor  worldly  honors  seemed  I 

Of  lale  years,  Stoke  Pogis,  England,  has  been  identified  as  the  scene  of  the  poem.  Ne: 
be  found  the  "country  churchyard  "  in  which  the  elegy  was  written,  now  possessing  the  > 
poetical  association  of  being  the  poet's  last  resting-place.  Before  issuing  a  new  illuslraii 
of  this  classic  for  the  holiday  season,  the  Messrs.  Roberts  Brothers  bad  Mr.  Harry  Fenn  g 
England  and  make  at  Sioke  Pogis  itself  the  majority  ot  (be  sketches  from  which  his  desi 
been  reproduced.  In  this  way  not  only  an  exceedingly  strong  interest  has  been  gaine< 
edition,  but  an  originality  and  fidelity  in  its  illustrations  far  surpassing  those  of  all 
editions.  It  very  fittingly  beirs  the  distinction  of  being  (be  "  Harry  Fenn  Edition," 
which  it  will  always  in  the  future  be  known. 

Another  interest  almost  as  strong  as  the  one  just  mentioned  is  the  reprint  in  th 
of  the  "  Elegy  "  of  several  very  beautiful  verses  that  appeared  in  the  early  editions  of  I 
but  were  subsequently  omitted  by  the  author.  The  verses  are  not  only  a  literary  cur- 
have  sufficient  merit  to  make  them  well  worth  preserving.  An  interesting  appendix 
Gray's  motives  in  casting  these  lines  aside.  One  ot  the  so-called  rejected  verses  has  b 
the  subject  of  one  of  the  most  delicate  and  beautiful  illustrations  in  the  book,  where  I 
as  a  frontispiece.  We  have  reproduced  it  on  the  opposite  page,  believing  it  offers  a 
example  of  the  very  careful  work  which  is  so  noticeable  in  this  little  volume. 

Although  this  is  in  size  not  one  of  the  greatest  of  the  holiday  books,  il  is  certain 
the  most  important  artistically.  The  pictures  throughout  are  liille  gems,  occupyint;  ev 
and  interpreting  in  an  exquisite  fashion  the  various  moods  of  the  au(bor.  The  vigm

PLUTARCH  FOR  BOYS  AND  GIRLS. 


wntien  in  our  own  ceniurj.  (wm  in  ureece  aoa  olo- 
cMted  at  Delphi,  afterward  for  maay  years  cuablblKiI 
In  Rome.  Plutarch  thorouehly  understood  boib  Greciao  and  Roman  character,  and  baa  ihown  u 
his  knowledge  of  the  subiile  differences  produced  by  nationality,  in  the  peculiar  form  of  hit 
biographies,  which  he  wrote  in  parallels,  first  treating  a  Greek  and  then  a  Roman  bera,  and  ibea 
conirasiing  the  (wo. 

Although  not  always  historically  correct  as  to  facts,  owing  to  his  ideal  love  for  the  heroes  be 
portrays,  his  poetic  genius  and  his  stirring  imagination,  Plutarch  has  given  so  many  detaiti  o[  the 
home-life  as  well  as  of  the  heroic  achievements  of  Cxsar,  Alexander,  the  two  Gracchi,  Denos- 
Ihcnes,  Cato,  Cicero,  and  others,  that  a  careful  reading  of  his  biographies  give*  one  of  the  dearest 
pictures  of  the  manners  and  customs  of  Greece  and  Rome  at  the  various  perioda  in  which  bii 
heroes  lived,  and  no  young  person  can  read  his  works  without  being  stirred  to  emulate  the  deeds 
and  thoughts  he  has  here  brought  before  him  in  such  fascinating  and  inspitfog  manner.  Na- 
poleon is  said  to  have  ranked  Plutarch  beyond  alt  authors,  and  to  have  loved  hia  atoriei  from  bis 

Proi.  White  has  conlined  himself  closely  to  the  translation  known  as  the  Drydeit  tnmsUlioa. 
merely  eliminating  such  passages  as  render  it  unsuitable  for  young  people.  His  selection  of  the 
heroes  treated  is  a  happy  one,  including  almost  all  the  best-known  parallels.  He  bai  increased 
the  interest  o(  his  work  by  adding  a  chronological  table  of  the  accurate  dates  of  the  events  de- 
scribed, and  by  a  table  of  weights  and  measures  mentioned,  prepared  by  Dr.  Arbullinot.  The 
publishers  have  done  their  share  to  make  the  book  popular  with  boys  and  girls.  It  is  printed  in 
pica  type  on  thick  paper,  contains  about  litly  illustrations  and  (our  colored  maps,  and  in  its 
handsome  binding  makes  a  very  imposing  quarto  oF  four  hundred  and  eighty-six  pages.  Boys 
and  girls  will  thank  Prof,  White  for  the  chance  be  has  given  Ihem  to  become  acquainted  with  these 
famous  "  Lives,"  which,  says  Prof.  C.  K.  Adams,  "  are  among  the  most  delightful  sketches  ever 
written  :"  which  "  are  able  to  inspire,  to  charm,  to  instruct ;"  which  "raise  the  Greek  and  Roman 
heroes  from  the  dead  and  clothe  them  again  with  flesh  and  blood."

Christmas  Number. 


lerry  Adventures  of  Robin  Hood. 

OF    GREAT    RENOWN    IN    NOTTINGHAMSHIRE. 

Written  and  Illustrated  by  HOWARD   PYLE. 

e  volume   4to,  full  embossed  leather,  antique,  fi?om  the 
authoi-'s  designs,  #4.50. 

N  THIS  book,  undoubtedly  the  most  original  and  elaborate  ever 
produced  by  an  American  artist,  Mr.  Pyle  has  gathered  from 
the  old  ballads  and  legends,  and  told  with  pencil  and  i>en 
the  complete  and  consecutive  story  of  Robin  Hocxl  and  his 
merry  men  in  their  haunts  in  Sherwood  forest.    There  is  some- 
thing thoroughly  English  and  home-bred  in  these  episodes  in 
the  life  of  the  bold  outlaw.     His  sunny,  open-air  nature,  his 
:hless  skill  at  archery)  his  generous  disposition,  his  love  of  fair  play,  and  his  ever 
\TA  courtesy  to  women,  form  a  picture  that  has  no  counterpart  in  the  folk-lore  of  any 
r  people. 

But  it  has  other  claims  to  notice,  in  the  admirable  illustrations  which  Mr.  Pyle  has 
rn  profusely  throughout  his  book.  These  pictures  set  forth  most  graphically  every 
tful  scene  in  the  narrative,  and  they  are  in  perfect  keeping  with  the  story,  even  to  the 
lest  detail ;  as  specimens  of  figure-drawing,  they  form  the  most  admirable  and  artistic 
!  that  an  American  artist  has  created  for  many  years. 

I'he  superb  leather  binding  and  general  make-up  of  the  book  deserve  more  than  a 
ng  mention.  Every  detail  has  been  carefully  wrought  out  by  the  author,  and  the 
ii)r  of  the  volume  is  in  perfect  keeping  with  its  contents.  The  elaborate  chapter- 
ings,  initial  letters,  and  vignettes  are  especially  noteworthy  specimens  of  this  form  of 
ration.

NOTICE  TO  BOOKSELLERS. 

The  Literary  News  for  Christmris,  iS^Sj,  will  embrace  thirty-two  pages 
f  reading  matter  and  illustrations,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superior  to 
tiy  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore. 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  bookseller 
ho  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby. 

The  chief  attraction  of  the  forthcoming  issue  will  be  the  numerous  spcci- 
len  illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  books. 

The  most  valuable  feature,  besides  the  Literary  Notes,  will  be  a  special 
rice-list  of  the  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  Holiday  Lit- 
ature. 

To  those  who  desire  to  reach  a  wider  public  than  they  feel  they  can  afford 
I  seek  through  the  more  expensive  Christmas  Number  of  the  Publishers' 
'eeklv,  the  Christmas  Number  of  the  Literary  News  is  recommended  as  the 
:st  possible  medium  to  cultivate  a  holiday  trade.

id  Christian  writers  of  all  creeds,  and  are  precious  thoughts  for  hours  of  private  meditations  and

tmpathy  for  a  friend  in  sorrow  can  be  expressed  in  no  more  delSar'-W  acceptable  manner  than

LIVING   THOUGHTS.

ries,  from  the  character  of  the  writings  and  the  care  in  selection,  are  peculiarly  acceptable  tu  any
