The  Life  of  Luther. 

BY  JULIUS  KOSTLIN.  PROFESSOR  IN  THE  UNIVERSITY  OF  HALLE 
MORE  THAN  60  ILLUSTRATIONS  FROM  ORIGINAL  PORTRAITS  AN 
UMENTS.     I  vol.,  8vo,  |3.so. 

Professor  KSsilin  is  the  acknuwledged  aulhoiily  upon  everything  relating  10  ilii 
times  of  Litlher.  His  srcal  work,  published  seveial  years  ago,  has  taken  its  place  as  r 
Lutheran  history,  and  every  book  relating  lo  Luther  or  the  Reformation  published  iW' 
pearance  has  been  largely  indebted  to  its  pages.  The  volume  announced  above  has  bee 
prepared  with  a  view  10  the  present  renewed  interest  ia  the  great  Refuimer.  This  bio; 
addition  to  its  being  the  most  accurate  and  readable  history  or  Luther  and  the  Reform: 
published,  has  special  claims  to  notice  in  the  beautiful  and  unique  portraits  of  Luth 
most  of  the  prominent  characters  associated  in  any  way  with  the  Reformation. 

A  special  feature  is  theiepiaduction  m  fac-iimile  of  various  documents  of  thehigbei 
such  as  one  of  Pope  Leo's  Indulgences,  a  page  of  Luther's  Latin  Psalter,  with  his  mu 
interlinear  notes,  a  tetter  of  Luther  to  his  wife,  the  first  page  of  the  first  edition  of  bis  ti 
of  the  New  Testament,  the  concluding  part  of  bis  will,  with  the  signatures  of  Melaacli 
others,  etc.  In  addition,  there  are  numerous  engravings  of  places  and  objects  connt 
Luther's  memory  that  give  this  book  greater  interest  and  completeness  than  any  other  1 
of  Luther  could  ever  lay  claim  to. 

This  American  edition  is  published  with  the  special  sanction  of  the  author.

INTERNATIONAL  COPYRIGHT. 

THE  ANTHONY  TROLLOPS  CHARGE. 
Elttvir  in  1*1  BiilBM  Hmid,  Oct.  8. 
In  his  very  entcrlBining  aulobiography  Mr- 
ilhany  TtoUope  has  a  good  deal  to  say  on  the 
(ed  quCBlion  of  intciniitional  copyright,  as 
at  English  authors  have.  "I  cannot  say," 
writes.  "  that  I  have  never  had  a.  shilling  of 
neiican  money  on  behalf  of  reprints  of  my 
rk — but  I  have  been  consciooB  uf   no   such 


ng  then  in  the  States,  the  details  of  which 
sufficiently  amusing— that  I  could  not  my- 
[  succeed  in  dealing  with  American  boolc- 
lers.  I  have  sold  all  foreign  right  to  the 
glish  publishers  ;  and,  though  I  do  not  know 
t  I  have  raised  my  price  against  thcin  on  thai 
re.  I  may  in  this  way  have  bad  some  indirect 
lantage  from  the  American  market.  But  I 
know  that  what  the  publishers  have  received 
e  is  very  irifliiig.  I  doubt  whether  Messrs. 
apman  &  Hall,  my  present  publishers, get  for 
ly  sbeelE  sent  to  the  Slates  as  much  as  live  per 
t  on  the  price  they  pay  me  for  my  manu- 
ipi." 

have  taken  the  trouble  to  inquire  into  this 
[einent  of  Mr.  Trollope'a,  and  I  find  it  about 
Evide  of  the  mark  as  are  most  statements  on 
subject.  Being  in  the  neighborhood  of  the 
isrs,  Applelons'  establishment  and  remem- 
ing  tbat  TroUope's  "Ralph  the  Heir"  ran 
lugh  the  pages  of  the  regretted  Journal,  I 
It  in  and  asked  Mr.  Bunce.  who  was  editor 
he  yosimal,  whether  he  had  paid  Mr.  Trol- 
e  for  his  storv.  "We  didn't  pay  Trollope 
iself,"  laid  Mr.  Bunce,  "  but  we  paid  his 
ilisher.  Virtue,  to  whom  he  had  sold  all  in- 
!St,  (>5oo  for  the  right  of  publication,  which 
ran  a  sa  serial  through  the  yownalxaA  afier- 
•A  sold  to  Harper  &  Bros.,  nho  published  it  in 
k-form."  This  does  not  seem  to  me  to  be  very 
pay  for  a  rl|^t  that  was  only  one  of  cout- 
'.  Mj  next  step  was  to  Franklin  Square  to 
what  the  Messrs.  Harper  bad  to  say.  Being 
publishers  of  Mr.  Trollope's  autobiography. 
,-  had  of  course  seen  the  paragraph  to  which 


I  alluded,  and  this  was  their  answer :  "  We  have 
paid  over  /^30oo  to  Mr.  Anthony  Trollope  or  to 
his  publishers  for  his  books  republished  by  us, 
including  ji'750  for  one  book."  If  Mr.  Trollope 
received  no  money  for  the  American  publication 
of  his  books  it  was  not  because  no  such  money 
was  paid.  His  publishers  got  it,  and  if  they 
bought  all  rights  from  him  it  seems  to  me  that 
they  were  the  people  to  be  paid.  In  all  they 
received  $16,500  from  two  American  publishers, 
which  I  should  Ihink  was  more  than  five  pec  cent 
on  the  price  they  paid  for  the  manuscripts. 
English  authors  are  very  pig-headed  on  this 
question  of  payment.  They  sell  all  rights  to 
their  English  publishers,  and  he  then,  being  ab- 
solute owner  of  their  manuscript,  negotiates 
with  an  American  publisher  who  pays  him  what 
is  a  fair  sum  considering  the  circumstances. 
But  this  docs  not  satisfy  the  irate  author,  who 
wants  another  sum  paid  lu  him.  He  must  have 
a  very  strange  idea  of  the  way  business  is  con- 
ducted. English  publishers  may  pay  two  per- 
sons for  one  tiook.  but  it  is  a  trick  that  has  never 
been  told  of  them. 

"LEYPOLDT'S   LITTLE   HOLIDAY 

BOOK." 

Fnm  lit  Piiladilfiim  Evtmimg  BtiiUliH. 

Mr.  Leyfoldt's  little  holiday  book  of  some 
thousands  of  pages  and  weighing  some  pounds 
—The  Publishers'  Trade  List  Annual  of  18B3— 
has  appeared  and  looms,  not  lies,  before  us.  It 
must  be  a  treasure  to  every  one  connected  with 
the  book-trade,  and  though  we  cannot  recom- 
mend it  as  a  handy  volume  for  the  vest-pocket. 
ne  can  assert  that,  so  far  as  we  have  been  able 
to  discover  from  a  somewhat  cursory  perusal,  its 
moral  tone  is  pure.  Still  it  cannot  be  safely  put 
into  the  hands  uf  children.  Only  persons  who 
arc  physically  sirong  can  venture  to  grasp  it  and 
to  grapple  for  the  grand  things  hidden  in  its  mys- 
terious profundities. 

Frtm  iMt  H^n/trd  Phi. 

Mr.  Levpolot's  publication  is  indispensable 
to  booksellers,  librarians  and  owners  at  private 
libraries.  A  publishers'  trade  list  has  a  dreaiy 
mechanical  side  in  its  appearance,  but  there  is 
more  of  life  and  significance  behind  its  types 
than  in  any  other  one  book,  save,  perhaps,  an 
unabridged  dictionary,  and  it  might  well  dispute 
the  palm  with  that.  Mr.  Leypoldl's  annual 
shows  his  most  excellent  judgment  in  arrange- 
ment and  worthily  maintuns  the  precession. 

J-riK  Ikt  Brwrkljn  Timil. 

For  eleven  years  Mr.  Leypoldt  has  issued 
from  the  office  of  the  Publishers'  Weekly,  a 
bulky  annual  volume  embracing  between  its 
covers  all  available  information  respecting  the 
constantly-increasing  list  of  American  publica- 
tions. As  a  "  trade  help"  it  is  of  course  indis<- 
pensable  10  booksellers,  but  it  is  of  practical 
value  as  well  to  the  larger  class  of  bookbuyen, 
both  as  a  reference  volume  and  a  finding  llsL 
The  volume  for  1S83,  just  issued,  is  a  large  oc- 
tavo of  over  2500  pages  aod  contains,  tesldes 
the  catalogues  of  American  publishers,  alpha- 
betically arranged,  a  complete  record,  with  full 
title,  of  all  books  published  from  July,  iSSa,  to 
July,  1883.  An  index  accompanies  this  list,  by 
which  every  book  on  record  can  be  found  whether 
looked  for  under  author,  title,  or  subject.  Any 
one  who  is  a  purchaser  of  books,  whether  for 
private  use,  for  school  or  Sunday-school  librariet .  ^' 
or  whatever  purpose  will  find  this  volume  & 


The  \fumsherz'  WuUy.  \Ng.  6i81A>r.M."'r 


best  possible  means  of  keeping  up  with  [he  pub- 
licalians  of  each  year.  BookseUera  and  book- 
buyers  as  well,  arc  under  constant  obligation  to 
the  iodcfaligable  editor  of  the  PubLiSHRRs' 
Weekly  for  valuable  aids  toward  a  belter 
understanding  of  the  book-trade  and  for  better 
methods  of  reference  and  selection,  and  this 
annual  voltime  should  be  more  widely  known 
and  used  outside  the  trade  limits  lo  which  it  has 
been  heretofore  confined. 

OBITUARY. 

EDMUND  HARRISOS. 

Edmi'nd  Harrison,  talesman  and  traveller  for 
J.  B.  LippLncott  &  Co.,  died  suddenly  oh  the 
morning  of  the  15th  inst.,  at  his  home  in  Phila- 
delphia. He  was  born  near  Petersburg.  Va., 
October,  1S44.  While  completing  his  education 
at  the  University  of  Vitginia,  he,  with  all  the 
roembeis  of  his  class,  entered  the  army  at  the 
breaking  out  of  the  Civil  War.  He  served  in 
Pickett's  Division  during  the  whole  war.  and 
«»s  among  those  who  surrendered  with  Lee  ut 
Appomattox  Court  House.  After  the  war  he 
entered  the  book-store  of  Wilkins  &  Barbour,  of 
Petersburg,  and  remained  with  them  until  the 
early  part  of  the  year  1868,  when  he  went  to 
Philadelphia  and  accepted  the  position  which  he 
filled  at  the  time  of  his  death. 

Mr.  Harrison  was  well  tnown  and  highly  re- 
spected by  the  trade  in  the  South  and  West, 
where  he  was  accustomed  to  travel,  and  his 
genial  and  gentlemanly  manner  brought  him  a 
host  of  friends  at  home  and  among  the  trade 
wherever  he  went.  He  leaves  a  wife  and  one 
child. 

Dr.  Robert  D.  Joyce,  for  many  years  a  res- 
ident of  Boston,  died  in  Dublin,  Ireland,  on 
October  aj,  of  lung  disease.  Dr.  Joyce  was 
best  known  to  the  literary  world  through  his 
poems  "  Blanid  "  and  "  Deirdre,"  published  in 
the  No  Namt  series.  He  was  a  graduate  of 
Dublin  University.

ScRiBNEB  &  Welford  will  issuc  an  American

fessing the  same  aim  been  too  much  neglected. 
The  volume  is  the  result,  the  compiler  says,  of

names  are  included,  and  among  them  are  many 
American  mus ic- writers. 

WiLLARD  Small,  Boston,  has  just  published 
a  work  of  value  to  superintendents  and  teachers 
in  public  schools,  entitled  "School  Manage- 
ment." by  loseph  Langdon,  of  Ibe  training 
college,  Saltlcy,  England.  It  comprises  a  gen- 
eral view  of  the  work  of  education,  with  some 
account  of  the  intellectual  faculties,  from  the 
teacher's  point  of  view.  It  also  contains  hints 
upon  organization,  discipline,  and  moral  training.

CtJMUiN-G  &  Brinkeriioff.  N.  Y.,  announce 
"The  Gumming  Code,"  compiled  on  a  new  plan  by 
a  practical  tFlegraphi;r  to  meet  the  wants  ol  busi- 
ness men,  who  may  desire  a  short,  safe,  and  con- 
venient, telegraph  cipher  code.  It  contains  only 
words  of  ten  letters  or  under  (ihe  cable  limit), 
and  of  one  to  three  syllables  :  all  being  familiar 
colloquial  English  expressions,  and  selected 
carefully  in  regard  to  their  telegraphic,  phono- 
graphic and  chirographic  relations  to  one 
another,  so  as  to  prevent  blunders  through  the 
peculiarities  of  the  "  Morse  "  and  English  alpha- 
bets. 

T.  Y.  CroivullS  Co,  announce  "Bryce's 
Pearl  English  Dictionary."  containing  about 
1 5.000  words  in  384  pages  of  clear  pearl  type, 
comprising  besiJes  the  ordinary  and  newest 
words  in  rhe  language,  short  explanations  of 
a  large  number  of  scientific,  philosophical,  Hi' 
erary,  and  technical  terms.  The  work  is  in  small 
space,  but  sufficiently  comprehensive  for  practi- 
cal purposes.  It  Is  printed  with  red-line  borders 
neatly  bound  in  cloth  and  retails  at  50  cents.

from  Dickens"  and  "Chips  from  Thackeray." 
by  Thomas  Mason,  which  form  part  of  a  series 
called  Chips  from  Standard  Aulhon.

1883. 


NOTICE  TO  BOOKSELLERS. 

The  Literary  News  for  Christmas,  1S83,  will  embrace  thirty-two  pag^s 
f  reading  matter  and  illustrations,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superior  to 
ny  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore. 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  bookseller 
'ho  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby. 

The  chief  attraction  of  the  forthcoming  issue  will  be  the  numerous  speci- 
len  illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  books. 

The  most  valuable  feature,  besides  the  Literary  Notes,  will  be  a  special 
rice-list  of  the  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  Holiday  Lit- 
rature. 

To  those  who  desire  to  reach  a  wider  public  than  they  feel  they  can  aSord 
3  seek  through  the  more  expensive  Christmas  Number  of  the  Publishers' 
Veekly,  the  Christmas  Number  of  the  Literary  News  is  recommended  as  the 
est  possible  medium  to  cultivate  a  holiday  trade.

LITERARY   flRACIES. 
From  lit   N.   Y.  OSiirtir,  Oct.  iB. 

That  there  are  pirates  on  the  land  as  well  as  on 
the  sea.  is  a  fact  well  known  and  greatly  10  be  de- 
plored. The  Rev.  Dr.  Blaikie,  of  Edinburgh, 
convicts  one  of  our  New  York  religious  jouraals 
of  the  sin  and  shame  of  piracy.  And  what  is 
worse,  he  says  that  the  offending  journal  not  only 
took  one  of  the  papers  out  of  a  foreign  magazine 
but  it  took  ■■  Three  liberties  with  the  paper  :  (l) 
Thearticleiiclipped  and  curtailed  ;  (3)  No  refer- 
ence is  made  Co  ha  source  ;  and  (3)  No  reference 
is  made  to  its  occasion.  Consequently  the  name 
of  one  of  the  previous  writers  is  seen  flying  about 
in  it,  without  (he  slightest  indicadoa  where  the 
writing  nl  (hat  gentleman  is  10  be  found.  Anoth- 
er paper  in  the  journal  is  the  substance  of  a  New 
Year's  address  (hat  appeared  in  the  J-rtc  Church 
Monthly,  but  it  is  abridged  and  otherwise  '■  im- 
proved" in  a  way  somewhat  (tying  to  any  aulhor 
who  has  some  regard   to  style   and    to  a  natural 

These  ate  cruelties  inflicted  on  Ihe  victims  of 
Ihe  piratical  cruiser,  and  we  lift  up  our  hands 
against  such  practices.

British  religious  magazines,  and  especially  period- 
icals for  the  young.  We  would  gladly  copy  some 
uf  the  papers,  but  we  know  not  to  whom  we 
should  (five  credit  for  them.  Largely  (shall  we 
say  for  (he  mos(  pan)  ihey  are  stolen  from  Amer- 
ican sources,  and  were  we  to  quote  one  of  them 
crediting  it  (o  the  foreign  (hief.  we  should  do  in- 
justice to  the  owner  on  this  side  the  broad  Al- 

There  is  no  international  copyright  law  (O  pre- 
-vcnt  these  abuses-  Legally  it  is  tree  plunder  : 
morally  it  is  very  wrong.

publishes  a  list  o<  Uritish  books  which  it  reprjnis 
and  pays  full  copyiiKbi  for  every  copy  of  (hose

Great  Uriuin  than  we  have  republished  of  (heirs. 
but  we  have  never   received  one  penny  for  the

HEALTHY  COMPETITION. 

J^r,m  lit  Li*d,a  Bttit^lir.  W".  > 
Whatever  the  United  States  may  say  in  favor 
of  a  protective  tariS,  the  enterprising  citizi 
the  great  republic  have  no  objection  lu  pr< 
our  free  trade.  They  send  us  illustrated 
lines  and  books  in  increasing  numbers,  a 
must  say  that  if  the  reception  these  have  met 
with  has  been  cordial,  it  has  been  [ully  deserved. 
Harftr's  Afagadne  and  Tht  Century  have  already 
had  an  invigor-iling  effect  upon  our  own  pub- 
lishers, and  the  end  of  the  competition  has  not 
yet  been  reached.  Whatever  lessons  the  Ameri- 
cans have  to  leach  us  we  shall  learn,  slowly  per- 
haps, but  very  thoroughly.  It  might 
posed  that  sanitary  science 
thing  we  should  be  indebted  tunmvn-  .u. .  .■■ 
spile  of  Iheit  youth.  New  York  and  most  other 
American  cities  are  incomparably  behind  London 
Id  their  saniury  arrangements,  or  more  correclly, 
perhaps,  in  their  sanitary  administration.  How- 
ever this  may  be,  the  Americans  are  determined 
that  we  shall  share  all  they  know  on  the  subject, 
and  the  New  York  Sanitary  En^incr.  whose  mis- 
"sion  is  to  •■  raise  the  standard  ol  saniury  engi- 
neering, to  check  the  adulteration  of  food  and 
drugs,  and  secure  the  promotion  of  efficient  sani- 
tary work,"  will  be  published  regularly  every 
week  in  London.  If  it  has  anything  to  say 
which  is  worth  attention,  it  will  assuredly  find  it* 
circle  of  readers. 

SALE  OF  MAYNE  REIDS  BOOKS. 
The  following  details,  which  the  Pall  Malt 
Gazette  believes  to  be  correct,  concerning  the 
sale  of  Captain  Mayne  Reid's  novels  may  be 
interesting  :  In  i860  nine  books  of  [he  novelist 
came  into  the  hands  of  Messrs.  G.  Roulledge  & 
Sons,  namely,  "Tne  Desert  Home"  (of  which 
8748  were  primed),  "The  Boy  Hunters" 
(84S5I,"  The  Young  Voyageurs"  [6954),  "The 
Forest  Exiles"  (8315),  "The  Bush  Boy' 
(6710),  "The  Young  Yagers"  (5796).  "The 
Boy  Tar  "  (6S78),  "  The  Plant  Hunteis  "  {5000), 
and  "Ran  Away  to  Sea"  (6000).  In  Novem- 
ber. i860.  Captain  Mayne  Reiii  wrote  for  the 
same  firm  two  original  books,  called  "'  Bruin, 
or.  The  Great  Bear  Hunt,"  and  "  Odd  People  ;" 
of  the  former  9000  were  printed,  of  the  latter 
8000.  In  July,  1S79,  "Caspar,  the  Gaucbo " 
was  published,  ot  which  3000  copies  were  print- 
ed. In  February,  1S60,  iwoot  his  novels,  "  The 
WarTrair'and  "The  Quadroon,"  came  into 
their  possession,  of  the  former  14, S*'*'  at"*  of 
the  latter  ii,4«j  copies  being  printed.  In  Jan- 
uary, 1878,  twenty  more  of  his  novels  came  into 
their  hands.  Of  "The  Headless  Horseman" 
4QOO  copies  were  printed,  of  "The  Rifle  Ran- 
gers "  and  '■  The  White  Chief  "3000  copies,  and 
of  the  remaining  seventeen  2000  copies  each.

1883. 


NOTICE  TO  BOOKSELLERS. 

The  Literary  News  for  Christmas,  1S83,  will  embrace  thirty-two  pages 
reading  matter  and  illustrations,  and  we  intend  to  make  it  far  superior  to 
r  previous  year,  also  hoping  to  issue  it  earlier  than  heretofore. 

Its  sole  aim  and  object  is  to  increase  the  sale  of  books,  and  every  bookseller 
10  distributes  it  may  be  sure  of  being  benefited  thereby. 

The  chief  attraction  of  the  forthcoming  issue  will  be  the  numerous  speci- 
al illustrations  from  the  most  prominent  and  popular  new  holiday  books. 

The  most  valuable  feature,  besides  the  Literary  Notes,  will  be  a  special 
ce-list  of  the  Books  of  the  Season,  in  all  the  departments  of  Holiday  Lit- 
:ture. 

To  those  who  desire  to  reach  a  wider  public  than  they  feel  they  can  afford 
seek  through  the  more  expensive  Christmas  Number  of  the  Publishers' 
:eki.y,  the  Christmas  Number  of  the  Literary  News  is  recommended  as  the 
it  possible  medium  to  cultivate  a  holiday  trade.

Itle.  Brown  &  Co.),  with  all  the  steel  portrails 
ibe  lo-wolume  Library  cilifiea,  handsomely 
'und  in  new  cloth  binding,  with  white  title 
M»,  and  gilt  top.  This  Issue  will  be  called 
e  Unique  (dilioH.  Mr.  Worthington  will  short- 
issue  a  book  on  China  by  the  famous 
Chinese  Gardoa." 


DoDD.  Mi'AD  &  Co.  have  now  reader  the 
h&ndsome  "  History  of  Ancient  Sculpture."  by 
Lucy  M.  Mitchell,  of  which  we  gave  a  (oil  de- 
scription in  our  Christmas  nutnbcr ;  also,  "  The 
Wild  Tribes  of  the  Soudan  :  An  Account  of 
Travel  and  Sport  chiefly  in  the  Bas6  Counln'," 
being  personal  experiences  and  adventures  clur- 
ing  three  winters  spent  in  the  Soudan  t.y  F.  L. 
James.  This  latter  work  is  fully  illustrated  and 
contains  several  maps  in  colors, 

James  R.  Osnoon  &  Co.  have  published 
"  Raphael's  Madonnas  and  Holy  Families,"  by 
Mrs,  Julia  A.  Shedd,  illustrated  with  twenty 
full-page  heliotypes  of  Raphael's  most  famous 
and  popular  paintincis  ;  also  the  first  volume  of 
"The  World  as  Will  and  Idea,"  by  Artbiir 
Schopenhauer,  translated  from  the  German  by 
R.  B.  Haldane  and  John  Kemp.  This  verj-  im- 
portant and  able  philosophical  exposition  will 
be  completed  in  three  handsome  8vo  volumes. 

Roberts  Bkos.  hai-e  ready  "  New  EnRland 
Legends  and  Folk-Lore,"  by  Samuel  Adama 
Drake,  a  volume  which  brings  together  for  the 
first  time  the  legendary  stories  of  New  Eng- 
land. The  author  has  searched  out  the  origin 
of  the  tales  that  compose  the  volume,  and  has 
rewritten  them  in  an  entertaining  manner. 
Those  who  are  familiar  with  the  "  Scarlet 
Lerler,""The  Buccaneer,"  "  The  Skeleton  in 
Armor,"  "  Moll  Pitcher,"  and  the  stories  of 
wiicbcrafl,  may  here  have  as  ready  access  to 
the  tnith  as  heretofore  they  have  had  to  the 
romance  of  history.  The  work  is  enriched  by 
over  one  hundred  cbaracterisiic  illustrations, 
drawn  by  the  beat  artists.  It  is  sold  only  by 
subscription. 

Charles  ScttiBNBR's  Sons  will  publish,  on  the 
I5lh  inst..  an  important  work  by  Dr.  Henry  Clay 
Trumbull,  of  the  SundaySchool  Timri,  on  "  Ka- 
desh-Barnea  :  its  Importance  and  Probable  Site, 
with  tbe  Story  of  a  Hunt  for  it,  including  Studies 
nf  the  Route  of  the  Exodus  and  the  Southern 
Boundary  of  the  Holy  Land."  Kadesh-Barnea 
has  long  been  recognized  as  the  key  to  the  his- 
tory of  the  Israelites'  wanderings  in  the  wilder- 
ness, and  its  site  has  been  In  dispute  for  twenty 
centuries  or  more.  Twu  years  ago  Dr.  Trum- 
bull made  important  discoveries  in  the  East  as 
bearing  on  the  question  ;  and  of  this  he  now  for 
the  first  time  gives  a  full  account,  together  with 
the  result  of  his  studies  in  the  light  of  these  dis- 
coveries. The  result  of  the  work  is  a  practical 
determination  of  the  route  of  the  Exodus,  of  the 
main  outlines  of  the  wanderings,  and  of  the  south- 
ern boundary  of  the  Holy  Land.  The  description 
of  the  desert  hunt  for  the  lusl  site  of  Kadesh- 
Barnea  is  an  attractive  story  uf  adventure  ;  and 
the  incidental  information  which  it  gives  concern- 
ing) he  characteristics  and  customs  of  the  different 
tribes  of  iteseit  Arabs  enhances  its  value  in  the 
line  of  Bible  illustration.  The  work  will  have 
two  maps  and  four  lull-page  illustrations.  On  tbe 
same  day  they  will  issue  Dr.  H.  M.  Fields's  new 
book,  "  Among  the  Holy  Hills,"  a  record  in  Dr. 
Fields'sgraphic  style  of  travels  through  Palestine 
In  the  tracks  of  Jesus  Christ  ;  also,  "  A  Day  in 
Athcn<i  with  Socrates,"  hy  Ihe  author  of  "  Soc- 
rales,"  which  has  previously  been  announced  as 
"A  Morning  and  Evening  with  Socrates." 
Messis  Scribner  also  announce  that  they  will 
publish  large-paper  editions  of  Ik  Marvel's 
"  Reveries  of  a  Bachelor"  and  "  Dream  Life." 
These  editions  will  be  printed  in  the  veiy  besti 
style  on  Holland  paper  and  will  contain  portrrfm'^ 
of  Donald  G.  Mitchell  by  Smillie  and  Ritchie,^

The  Christmaa  number  of  Ihe  AmttUan  Boei- 
ir  has  reached  us.  Its  appearance  is  even 
're  imposing  ihan  last  year  And — abouc^ai 
:  1  Large  bodies  move  slowly.  Tbey  also 
,L  sbadoira  before.  Every  one  was  waLch- 
;  the  "coming  event"  long  after  the  little 
£EKLV  had  "  cleared  the  Ifaclt"  (by  "  standard 
le  ' ')  and  was  busy  at  work  in  the  book-stores. 
e  SoekstlUr  is  entitled  to  our  thanks  for  giv- 
{  the  Wkekly  a  fair  chance  to  bead  ofl  the 
iclipse  :"  on  the  other  hand,  the  Weekly  is 
tilled  10  the  thanks  of  the  Baotieller  for  hav- 
l  shed  light  on  the  boots  so  far  ahead  that,  now, 
•.fiiturei,  "astray"  in  the  BookiiUir,  can  be 
joyed  undetstandingly.  The  BuokselUr  is 
autiful,  and  can  be  made  useful  with  the 
EEKLV  taken  as  a  key. 

Americans  have  cause  to  be  proud  ol  such 
iblishiug  achievemcnls  as  find  their  due  recog- 
tion  in  the  citations  belov.  The  selected 
butes  paid  to  Hurfti's,  the  Onlury.  and  The 
iulh'i  Companion  fairly  represent  the  opinion 
>ih  of  the  press  and  the  public  ;  and  ours  requires 
)  wording  of  its  own  beyond  our  cordial  indorse- 
enl.  Yet.  in  tnaking  room  for  such  unstiuled 
■aise,  we  must  make  a  reservation  in  the  case 
:  Tki  Youth's  Cemfiinion.  Not  on  the  ground 
I  intrinsic  merit— (or  such  it  possesses— but  on 
le  ground  of  a  practice  nhlch  is  not  becoming 
■  a  companion  of  youth.  The  "  chromo"  and 
gift-book"  practice  is  an  American  invention, 
ul  none  o[  which  Americans  have  cause  to 
-  proud,  and  fortunately  one  that  lo-day  Is 
:arcely  resorted  Co  by  any  journal  of  lirst-class 
anding.  This  practice,  as  illustrated  in  the 
regular  issue  of  the  T/ie  Youth's  Companion, 
Ictobef  as" — a  mere  catalogue  of  premiums 
id  presents — would  supply  enough  illustra- 
nns  for  a  sermon.  If  this  issue  is,  as  many 
eluded  boys  are  led  to  believe,  the  most 
^mpiing  of  the  year  ;  and  if  the  walch-and- 
ocket-knife  persuasion  must  do  the  canvassing 
>r  Tlu  Youth'}  Companun,  then,  to  say  the  least. 


Tht  Youth's  Companion,  lays  itself  apen  to  the 
suspicion  that  moral  persuasion  inspired  by 
iu  merit  conld  not  do  its  woik  as  well.  TAf 
Youth's  Companion,  in  our  opinion,  can  ailord  to 
keep  above  the  "ttic.a  ol  the  trade  ;"  it  may 
well  stand  on  its  merit  and  reap  its  rewaid 
witboot  10  much  undoing,  in  one  issue,  of  Ihe 
good  work  done  In  fifty-one. 

We  take  issue  with  this  joutnSl  on  another 
point — viz.,  its  type.  The  money  spent  in 
premiums  on  "cheek"  and  smartness  would 
be  better  spent  in  protecting  the  eyesight  of  our 
rising  generation.  TAt  Youth's  Companion,  in 
far  100  large  a  proportion,  uses  type  which  is 
fit  only  for  notes,  quotations,  or  hook-titles,  and 
its  excellent  paper  and  print  cannot  remedy  tbe 
delect.  Witb  these  reservations  we  have  nothing 
left  but  praise  for  the  editorial  excellence  of  TAi 
YoulA's  Companion. 

AMERICAN  PUBLISHING  ENTERPRISE. 

■'HARPER'S"  ACHIEVEMENT. 
FrtmiktN.  Y.  Timii,  Die.  i. 
The  approach  of  a  new  year  and  the  consum- 
mate excellence  oi  this  month's  issue  make 
proper  and  agreeable  at  this  lime  a  few  words 
concerning  Harper's  Magatint,  the  prospectus 
of  which  for  1884  lies  before  us.  Harper  is  now 
somewhat  more  than  thirty  years  of  age,  and  its 
career  as  mere  matter  of  history  has  become  of 
the  first  importance  to  American  art  as  repre- 
sented in  wood-engraving,  Scarcely  any  prog- 
ress that  has  been  made  in  these  thirty  years  in 
any  department  of  human  endeavor  is  more 
striking,  or,  rightly  viewed,  perhaps  more  really 
'  iportant,  at  least  to  the  intellectual  andailislic 
side  of  American  life,  than  the  progress  repre- 
sented in  these  Harper  pages.  And  this  fact. 
apparent  as  it  is  to  all  men  who  ever  indulge  in 
thought,  needs  to  be  insisted  on  before  the  pub- 
large,  usually  slow  to  take  lull  cognizance 
of  such  facts  and  their  meaning.  But  lor  the 
true  importance  of  Harper's  art  history,  it  of 
course  is  not  necessary  to  go  so  far  back  as 
thirty  years,  since  within  verjf  recent  times— 
within  ten  or  even  five  years— it  has  made  steps 
forward  that  are  strikingly  impressive.  Already 
have  we  noted  the  high-water  mark  achieved  by 
the  Christinas  number,  the  highest  mark,  it 
seems  to  us,  that  it  has  ever  yet  achieved,  or 
perhaps  that  any  magarine  has  yet  achieved. 
and  it  would  bestow  upon  an  unthoughtful 
generation  lasting  good  and  some  credit,  were 
this  number  diligently  compared  by  it  with  Ihe 
December  number  ol  five  or  ten  years  ago.  And 
if  it  could  sometimes  be  rightly  said  that  the 
literary  department  has  not  kept  full  pace  with 
tbe  artistic,  it  is  to  be  remembered  also  that  in 
that  direction  there  was  not  the  same  opportunity 
for  advances,  although  very  considerable  ad- 
vances have  every  year  unquestionably  been 
made.  Much  comment  has  been  evoked  by  tbe 
success  ol  Harper's  in  a  foreign  market  (in  Eng- 
land alone  it  has  a  circulation  of  over  jo.ooo 
copies),  but  to  us  a  more  remarkable  thing  is  the 
fact  that  it  achieved  its  eminence  entirely  at 
home  before  it  was  tempted  to  seek  additional 
support  beyond  our  borders.  And  here  piob 
ably    lies    the  honor   that   chiefly    belongs 


I  .,L.oog  Ic

Harper's  Magatinr.  It  was  fouaded  and  for 
thinr  jttxa  it  was  mainiaiaed  as  an  American 
marine,  drawing  jls  malerial  from  all  lands 
and  all  wrilen,  but  slill  holding  its  essentially 
American  eminence  and  draving  its  support  en- 
tirely Trom  the  American  people,  until  finally  it 
went  over  the  water  to  extract,  within  a  brief 
time,  from  the  insular  and  stolid  Britisher  a  sale 
o(  jo.ooo  copie*  and  several  not  yet  successful 
attempts  to  rival  it  with  new  magsuines  of  the 
Britishers'  own  making. 


A  NEW  prospectus  of  the  Ctnlury  Magatit 
sets  forth  the  varied  and  Imporiant  features 
which  readers  of  that  periodical  will  enjoy  di 
ing  (be  coming  year.  It  is  a  long  and  noi 
worthy  list,  and  well  sustains  the  repute 
worthily  acquired  and  carefully  maintained  by 
the  management.  The  few  years  that  have  wit- 
nessed the  founding  and  growth  of  the  Cinlury 
to  its  present  eminence  have  been  memorable 
years  for  wood -en  graving,  and  the  pic 
honor  that  belongs  10  the  Century  as  it  n( 
and  as  it  was  under  its  old  name  could  m 
snatched  away  or  ignored  by  any  one  pretend- 
ing to  be  well  informed  or  fair-minded.  From 
the  very  start  this  magaiine  boldly  took  up  a 
forward  position,  and  it  has  boldly  and  splen- 
didly maintained  its  place.  With  each  number 
has  been  clearly  manifested  its  aggressive  and 
intelligent  enterprise  and  far-reaching  have  been 
the  resullB,  They  are  not  alone  to  be  seen  in 
the  periodical  literature  of  this  country  ;  they  are 
apparent  in  the  Old  World  as  well.  What  here- 
after is  to  become  of  {the  magaiines  that  still 
live  on  in  their  former  ways  of  stupidity  and 
dulness— that  more  than  tag  superfluous  on  the 
stage— we  cannot  see.  Their  day  of  wrath  is 
surely  near  at  hand.  During  the  past  year  the 
Cinlury  has  outdone  its  former  work  in  almost 
every  one  of  its  departments.  Some  of  its  en- 
gravings have  been  amazingly  fine  ;  several  of 
thase  in  the  December  number  are  particularly 
so,  the  portrait  of  Peter  Cooper  especially. 
And  its  reputation  bos  been,  to  our  mind,  as 
much  widened  by  its  improved  excellence  on  the 
literary  side  09  on  the  artistic.  The  time  was 
when  the  illustrations  were  first  and  the  letter- 
press second,  if  not  third,  in  importance.  But 
that  lime  for  a  year  or  more  has  been  passing 
surely  away.  America  sends  many  products  to 
England  of  which  she  is  deservedly  proud,  hut 
the  general  public — the  average  man,  we  may 
put  it — is  probably  less  proud  of  the  Century 
when  he  knows  it  goes  there  than  he  ought  ro 
be.  Each  month,  with  unmistakable  lorce,  it 
reminds  British  readers  that  a  large  proportion 
of  English-reading  men  and  women  now  read 
American  publications,  and  that  a  considerable 
number  of  thousands  of  them  .live  in  Sidney 
Smith's  own  native  land.

ScRiHNRR  &  Welford  have  published  a  new 
and  somewhat  extended  biography  ol  Henry 
Irving,  prepared  by  Austin  Bicteton,  one  of  Ihe 
editors  of  London  Thealrt.  The  story  of 
Irving's  life  \t  told  from  the  days  of  his  boyhood 
spent  in  Cornwall.  The  volume  will  contain

P.  Tilt.  All  more  or  less  cleverly  sketch  Irving 
in  his  best-known  oharacters. 

The  American  Unitarian  Association 
have  issued  "  Dr.  Orville  Dewey's  Complete 
Works,"  in  one  large  Svo  volume,  containing 
Soo  pages,  unilorm  in  style  and  price  with  their 
complete  edition  of  "  Dr.  Channing's  Works." 
The  prominent  position  occupied  by  Dr.  Dewey 
in  Ihe  early  annals  of  American  Uniiarianism 
have  made  it  desirable  that  his  great  power  as  a 
preacher  of  practical  truth  should  \k  more  gen- 
erally understood  and  recognized,  and  coming, 
as  they  will  now,  to  the  reader  at  a  moderate 
cost,  the  society  feels  conBdtnt  that  they  will 
command  a  wide  circulation.

John  E.  Potter  &  Co.,  Philadelphia,  have 
nearly  ready  "  The  Elements  of  Botany,"  a  new 
text-book  prepared  by  Prof.  W.  A.  Kellerman, 
of  the  Kansas  Stale  Agricultural  College.  It  is 
designed  for  either  school  use  or  for  independent 
study,  and  is  especially  adapted  to  ihose  students 
who  are  unable  to  give  several  years  to  this  sub- 
ject. An  important  feature  is  the  department 
called  "  Economic  Botany,"  in  which  the  princi- 
ples of  botany  are  applied  to  practical  uses. 
Prof.  Kelieiman  abolishes  the  old  heresy  that 
botany  is  a  dainty  accomplishment  for  ladies 
and  makes  it  a  valuable  practical  study.

NOTES  IN  SEASON. 
LNK  i  Wacnalls  have  published  "  A  Life 
wingli,"  in  season  for  the  fourth  centennial 
)e  birtli  ot  IhaC  great  relormer. 
AKrKR  &  Bros,  have  published  iri  their 
>i^/i«  ^uan  Library  the  "  Life,  Letters  and 
rary  Remaini  of  Edward  Bulwer,  Lord 
ion."  A  cloth  edition  with  portraits,  illus- 
ons,  and  fac-simlles  will  be  issued  at  once. 


Charles  Chipiei's  "  History  ot  Art  in  Chaldea, 
Assyria,  and  Phoenicia."  The  work  has  had  an 
unexpectedly  favorable  reception,  and  by^  the 
time  this  issue  leaves  the  press  the  Gfst  edition 
will  very  lik^']'  have  been  exhausted. 

D.  Appletos  &  Co.  have  published  "  Apple- 
tons'  Illustrated  Guide  to  Mexico,  including  a 
chapter  on  Guatemala,  and  a  complete  English- 
Spanish  Vocabulary,"  by  Alfred  R.  Conkling. 
The  work  is  highly  commended  by  General  Grant 
and  others.  It  ii  handsomely  gotten  up,  and 
contains  a  railway  map  and  numerous  illustra- 

James  R.  Osgood  &  Co.  have  issued  •'  A  Plea 
tor  the  Cure  o(  Rupture."  by  Joseph  H,  Warren. 
M.D.,  author  of  "A  Practical  Treatise  on 
Hernia,"  a  new  and  excellent  treatise,  giving  all 
the  essentials  of  the  operations  in  its  treatment ; 
also  an  /diliim  dt  luit  of  Thomas  i  Kempis's 
"Imitation  of  Christ,"  a  magnificent  octavo 
volume,  with  three  hundred  illustrations  on  wood 
and  numerous  full-page  etchings  from  new  de- 
signs.    The  edition  of  the  latter  is  limited. 

Charles  Scribnek's  Sons  have  published  a 
translation  of  the  nitty  and  incisive  French 
satire  on  England,  entitled  "John  Bull  and  his 
Island,"  which  is  the  sensation  ot  the  moment 
both  in  Paris  and  in  London.  The  volume  has 
reached  its  twentieth  large  edition  in  Paris. 
The  present  authoriied  translation — now  pub- 
lished siraullaneously  in  England  and  Ameri- 
ca—has been  preparing  under  the  supervision 
of  the  author.  The  London  publishers  write 
that  the  advance  orders  have  already  taken  up 
the  large  6rsc  edition  proposed  by  them. 

Wm.  Wooii  &  Co.  have  ready  the  fourth  vol- 
ume of  "The  Inlernational  Encyclopedia  of 
Surgery."  edited  by  Dr.  John  Ashhurst.  Jr.  ;  a 
new  and  enlarged  edition  of  Dr.  S,  Ringer's 
importatit  "Hand-book  of  Therapeutics:"  the 
first  American  from  the  latest  English  edition 
of  Dr.  William  Williams's  -  Principles  and  Prac- 
tice of  Veterinary  Medicine,"  revised  and  en- 
larged ;  "  A  Treatise  on  Bright's  Disease  of  the 
Kidneys  :  its  pathology,  diagnosis  and  treat- 
ment, with  chapters  on  the  anatomy  of  the  kid- 
ney," by  Dr.  Henry  B.  Millard,  who  gives  here 
the  result  of  the  experience  of  nearly  twenty-six 
years  of  hospital  and  and  private  practice  :  and 
a  "Manual  of  General  Medicinal  Technology, 
including  Prescription  Writing,"  by  Dr.  E.  Cur- 
tis, a  new  volume  in  Wna^i  Packif  Manuali. 
"The  Medical  Record  Visiting  List  for  1S84" 
is  also  ready. 

R.  WoRTHiNGTON  has  in  press  "The Story  of 
Chinese  Gordon,"  by  A.  Egmont  Hake.  The 
subjea  of  the  narrative  is  the  English  General 
Charles  G.  Gordon,  who  participated  in  the 
Crimean  War  as  Lieutenant  of  Engineers,  took 
part  in  the  investment  of  Peking  in  1B60,  and  in 
1S63  having  been  made  a  Mandarin,  he  took 
command  of  the  Chinese  forces  and  quelled  the 
rebellion,  which  was  headed  by  the  religious 
fanatic  Hung.  In  this  work  Gordon  achieved 
lasting  fame  and  it  was  at  this  time  that  he  first 
received  the  name  of  Chinese  Gordon.  It  wa* 
in  this  war  that  the  Americans  of  unenviable 
notoriety,  Ward  and  B  urge  vine  took  part, 
Burgevine  at  first  having  an  Imperial  command 
and  afterward  joining  the  rebels.  The  story  of 
the  war  as  told  in  this  volume  is  exceedingly  in- 
teresting to  the  general  reader  and  valuable  to 
the  historical  studenL

PuuusHERS  will  please  take  notice  that  the 
"  Supplement  to  the  American  Catalogue"  will 
include  all  books  issued  before  January  i,  1SS4. 
As  ihe  compilation  must  be  entirely  based  on  the 
record  of  the  Publishers'  Weekly,  bouks  Ihat 
have  not  been  brought  to  our  notice,  should  be 
sent  or  reported  to  this  office  before  January  5, 
else  we  cannot  be  responsible  [or  their  inser- 
tion. The  list  in  iha  Pi  blisicers'  Weekly 
January  13,  must  close  the  record  for  the  Sup- 
plement tu  the  American  Calalogur.  Although 
we  cannot  make  any  lurlher  search  for  boolcs 
thai,  either  from  oversight  or  lack  of  infurma- 
tion,  have  not  been  recorded  in  (he  Publisjiers' 
Weekly,  or  in  the  first  volume  of  the  American 
Catalogue,  we  shall  be  glad  to  include  in  the 
Supplement  any  such  books  provided  we  receive 
immediate  information,  giving  full  title  and  par- 
ticularly the  dale. 

As  there  will  he  scarcely  any  news  of  practical 
importance  next  week,  it  has  been  decided  Co 
devote  the  remaining  days  of  the  year  to  the 
completionof  ihe  book  record  of  1S83.  No  PfB- 
LiSHERs'  Weekly  will  there/ore  be  issued  under 
date  oi  December  29. 

So  many  more  books  have  been  received  this 
season  than  at  any  previous  one,  and  so  many 
were  received  at  Che  eleventh  bour,  that,  as  all. 
without  exception,  muse  be  recorded,  it  was  im- 
possible to  notice  them  in  good  season.  All 
;hose  books,  however,  which  have  not  as  yet 
ippeared  in  the  "Weekly  Record,"  with  a  few 
ixceptions,  have  already  been  brought  to  the 
lotice  of  the  trade  elsewhere  in  these  columns. 

The  Art  Age  for  Novemberhas  an  interesting 
tflicle  on  '■  Current  Book-Cover  Designs."  in 
*hich.  we  are  gtad  to  see.  (he  use  of  [he  "  nat-  I 
iral  plant  form  "  is  advocated  in  preference  Co  I 


gro(esque  and  meanlnglea 
(ies.  While  we  commend  tbis  number  as  a  model 
of  printing  as  far  as  pressnork  is  concerned,  we 
take  exception  (o  the  Indiscriminate  or  awkward 
use  of  red  in  subordinate  and  incidental  lines, 
and  particularly  In  solid  small-lype  paragraphs. 
The  use  of  red  for  column  and  head-rules  is  ques- 
tionable on  the  ground  of  its  disturbing  optical 
effect,  detrimental  to  the  best  printed  black. 
Red,  in  typography,  is  the  more  effective,  (he 
less  used  ;  and  when  used,  should  be  restricted 
to  single  and  striking  lines,  words,  or  leders.  As 
a  specimen  of  a  display  advertisement,  for  prac- 
tical purposes,  that  of  Gilliss  Brothers  is  the 
most  telling-  Cassell's.  on  the  same  ground. 
may  serve  as  a  sample  of  display  thai  defeats 
its  own  purpose. 


NEW  YORK. 
James  R.  Osgood  &  Co.  are  co  be  (he  pub- 
lishers of  a  magnificent  work  devoted  Eo  "  The 
Public  Service  of  the  State  of  New  York." 
The  edition  is  limited  to  five  hundred  copies, 
and  the  price,  per  set  of  three  royal  quarto  vol- 
umes, illustrated  with  487  large  plates  of  views 
and  portraits,  bound  in  full  levant  morocco, 
satin  lining,  gilt  edges,  is  $400. 

The  plan  o(  Ihe  work  is  to  give  a  complete 
history  of  the  Stale  of  New  York,  tracing  the 
growth  and  development  of  the  commonwealth 
from  the  beginning  ol  the  colonial  government 
to  the  present  :  to  give  a  perfect  represenialion 
of  the  Slate  of  New  York,  ils  institutions  and 
great  business  interests,  as  ihey  exist  to-day. 
To  this  end  the  most  competent  writers  in  the 
Stale  were  secured,  nearly  three  years  ago,  and 
their  cooiributions  have  been  carefully  super- 
vised and  prepared  for  the  press.  The  history 
of  each  department  of  the  government  is  fully 
recited,  logelber  with  thai  of  the  business  inter- 
est committed  to  its  charge.  In  this  way,  the 
history  of  canals  and  railroads  ;  of  trade  and 
commerce  ;  of  banking  and  insurance  ;  of  the 
control  of  criminals  and  the  gifts  of  charity  ;  of 
igricullurc,  education,  and  medicine  ;  of  the  sur- 
veys of  the  State — of  the  rocks  by  geologists, 
and  ol  the  surface  of  the  soil  by  engineers,  and 
of  other  leading  subjects  in  which  the  organized 
-  -  trgies  of  Ihe  people  of  the  Siale  have  l)een 
iployed— is  fully  set  forth.  The  executive, 
legislative,  and  judicial  departments  of  the  gov- 
nenlhave  passed  through  important  changes  ; 
these  changes  are  here  recorded  with  the  in- 
sight of  the  true  historian  dealing  rather  with 
the  life  of  the  Suie  than  with  the  lives  of  indi- 
viduals. The  work  is  unique  in  this,  (hat  it  is 
bundle  of  gossip  concerning  sia(esmen. 
leries  of  dramatic  incidents  in  public  life, 
ecord  of  the  deeds  of  parties  and  of  men 
in  moulditig  Ihe  Stale.  It  is  at  once  historical, 
descriptive,  statistical,  aud  biographical,  and  is 
printed  In  Ihe  most  sumptuous  manner,  with  ex- 
quisite type,  OTi  a  generous  page  of  heavy  and 
linted  paper,  and  with  titwral  margins. 

The  work  is  illustrated  with  143  full-page 
views,  among  which  are  interior  and  exterior 
views  of  Ihe  old  and  new  capitols  ;  of  all  SlalE 
buildings  :  and  of  all  college  buildings  U^'mC 


930 


Tht  Publisher^  Weekly.  [JVi..  6ij]  2fe.  i: 


Sute  oC  New  York,  as  nell  u  (amous  views  of 
nalurni  sceneij,  especially  of  the  Adirondaclcs 
and  Niagara  Falls  :  also  a  geological  map  of 
New  York,  prepared  under  the  personal  super- 
vision of  Prof.  James  Hall.  State  geologist,  and 
a  double-page  railroad  map  of  the  State  of  New 
York  prepared  expressly  for  this  work.  The 
work  is  also  illustruted  with  I02g  ponralts. 
printed  with  autographs,  among  which  arc  por- 
traits of  all  the  governors  of  New  York,  impor- 
tant State  officers  and  legislators,  presidents  of 
cotlEfies  and  notable  professors  and  educational 
benelactors.  and  presidents  of  railroads,  insur- 
ance companies  and  banks  :  also  doublc-page 
views  of  the  old  and  new  Senate  and  Assembly 
chambers,  with  Senate  and  Assembly  in  ses- 
sion :  of  the  Court  of  Appeals  chamber,  with 
bench  and  bar  ;  of  the  New  York  Slate  Medical 
Society,  anJ  the  Stale  Dental  Society. 

ARCHIVES  OF  MARYLASD. 
Frem  tht  Baltimnri  Daf. 
The  publication  of  "  Maryland  Archives"  un- 
der the  authority  of  the  Slate,  but  under  the  di- 
rection of  the  Maryland  Historical  Society, 
marks  an  epoch  in  the  printing  of  SlaCc  records, 
because  the  business  has  been  removed  from  all 
suspicion  of  political  jobbery  and  has  been  placed 
in  the  hands  of  an  honorable  record  commis- 
sion, with  no  interests  to  subserve  save  those 
of  science  and  of  the  honor  of  the  State.  The 
enterprise  has  not  been  the  outgrowth  of  party 
interest  or  o(  printers'  clamor  lor  more  copy, 
but  of  a  united  demand  of  all  f(ood  men  and  of 
all  parlies  who  really  cared  for  the  history  of 
this  Slate.  It  was  not  an  individual  or  sellish 
scheme ;  it  was  a  public -spirited  movement, 
supported  by  the  members  of  the  Historical 
Society,  by  friends  of  the  Johns  Hopkins  Uni- 
versity, and  by  the  Baltimore  press.  All  indi- 
vidual efforts  to  move  the  Legislature  toward  any 
adequate  provision  for  the  Stale  archives  had 
ignominiously  failed.  Corporate  eflurt  succeed- 
ed. The  plan  originated  jusC  two  years  ago 
while  Mr.  Edward  A.  Freeman  and  James 
Bryce,  M.P.,  were  lecturing  at  the  Johns  Hop- 
kins University.  These  gentlemen,  who  are 
both  of  an  historical  as  well  as  a  political  turn 
of  mind,  were  introduced  to  members  of  the 
Maryland  Historical  Society  ;  they  were  invited 
to  examine  certain  Slate  archives  there  pre- 
served ;  they  wrote  opinions  touching  the  value 
of  these  archives.  These  opinions  of  renowned 
specialists,  these  tespensa  prudenlum,  were 
brought  before  the  Maryland  Historical  Society 
by  the  Hon.  George  William  Brown,  together 
with  a  memorial  to  the  General  Assembly, 
drawn  up  by  Dr,  Wm.  Hand  Browne,  afterward 
cboseo  as  editor  ol  the  series.  The  society  look 
up  the  matter,  a  committee  pressed  it  forward, 
the  cily  press  supported  it,  the  memorial  evolved 
into  a  bill  for  the  transfer  of  the  colonial  and 
revolutionary  archives  of  Maryland  from  An- 
napolis to  Baltimore  with  an  appropriation  o( 
taoDO  to  be  expended  toward  their  publication 
by  Ihc  Maryland  Historical  Society,  into  vrbose 
charge  the  records  (still  the  property  of  the 
State)  were  intrusted  for  safe-keeping.  This 
bill  was  passed  and  was  finally  approved  March 
30.  i88a.  Since  then  the  society  has  built  by 
subscription  a  (ire-proof  vault  costing  about 
$1000,  and  under  the  efficient  direction  of  the 
librarian,  Mr.  J-  W.  M.  Lee,  the  records  have 
been  carefully  arranged  and  catalogued.  One 
of  the  most  noticeable  features  of  the  published 


volume  now  before  us  is  ao  claboralt  tir  ■ 
of  Che  State  archives  of  Mii7Un(l,  givl': :::  -■ 
uil  the  contents  ot  the  i-iiioiavolyiiin":cii- 
script  records  received  (roin  Annapoli'.iiif 
with  the  chrDnolO|{icaI  limits  of  ei:b  ^-f.-.  -" 
materials,  their  defect^  raising  p^tf.  ^

history  of  Maryland  dovn  to  the  loi  ."'. 
when  the  constitution  of  the  Uniml  J*>i;>  " 
into  operation.  The  roluine  dob  poi'-

legislation  of  Maryland  from  163;  Wp,

OBITUARY. 

WILUAM  PA  TTON. 
It  is  with  regret  that  we  note  the  death  of 
Villiatn  Paiton,  which  occurred  on  the  ad  inst. 
I  Waterbury,  Conn.  In  William  f'aHon  the 
ntire  trade  loses  an  old  friend.  Impulsive,  im- 
ctuo-js  at  tiin.es,  he  was  ever  a  sturdy  defender 
f  right  and  principle  whatever  the  cost  to  him- 
elf.  Of  a  clear  and  discriminating  mind,  and  with 
he  ability  to  discover  the  vulnerable  part  in  a 
ham,  he  bore  a  free  lance  for  what  he  thoufthl 
ight,  and  against  what  he  thought  falst^     He 

William  Pation  was  of  Scotch-Irish  descent. 
^is  father,  a  cotton  manufacturer  from  near 
Jelfasl,  came  to  this  country  and  settled  in 
Jiooklyn,  N.  Y.  Becoming  a  skilled  button 
baser  in  New  York  Mr.  Paiion  went  to  Waler- 
lury  to  pursue  the  industry  and  was  one  of  the 
irst  s.illed  chasers  in  Waterbury.  But  soon, 
lowevcr,  his  fondness  for  reading  turned  his  ai- 
ention  to  the  book  business,  in  whirh  he  was 
'Vaterbury's  p'oncer,  and  with  which  he  has  ! 
leen  identified  nowforaboul  forty  years.  As  his  ' 
lealth  began  to  fail  a  few  months  ago,  he  sold 
jut  his  business  in  Patton's  block  10  George  N. 
Ells.  His  decline  since  then  has  been  gradual 
m  sure.  His  old  complaint,  that  of  rhcuma- 
lism,  was  the  immediate  cause  of  his  demise. 
Had  he  lived, he  would  have  been  seveniy-four 
J-ears  old  the  17th  of  this  monlh. 

FRAffK    IV.    THOMPSON. 

Frank   W.  Thompson,  for  many  years  with 

Kobert  Clarke  &  Co.,  died  on  Ihe  loth  inst.  at 

nis  residence  in  Cincinnati,  at  the  age  of  thirtv. 

Mr.   Thompson,  we  Ica-n   from   the   Cincinnati 


during  which  time  he  made  himself  a  valued  ac- 
quisition to  the  retail  department,  fo[minf;many 
acquaintances  with  the  public  and  endearing 
himself  to  hli  associates  in  the  store.  His  face 
was  one  of  the  familiar  ones  at  Robert 
Clarke  &  Co.'s.  and  his  loss  will  be  greatly  re- 
gFetled  by  those  who  were  accustomed  to  deal 
with  him.  In  the  store  among  h.s  fellow-cierks 
his  death  seems  hard  10  be  redlized.  He  was 
with  them  but  last  week,  bright,  courageous, 
generous,  helpful,  sell-sacrificing.  The  loss 
which  his  mother  and  father  sustain  in  this 
bereavement  is  irreparable.  He  was  their  only 
son,  and  a  belter  one  never  lived.  He  was  a 
young  man  of  exemplary  private  character,  and 
a  consistent  member  of  the  Mt,  Auburn  Presby- 
terian Church." 

•JOHN  WILLYOUNG. 
John  Wii.lvol'ng,  proprietor  of  the  Detroit 
Book  Depository,  died  at  Detroit,  December  2. 
aged  forty  years.  Mr,  Willyoungwasbornin  Lan- 
caster, >1.  v.,  September  so,  1S43.  At  ihe  age 
of  twenty  he  left  home  and  engaged  in  farming 
in  Niagara  County.  He  remained  upon  the 
farm  about  two  years,  when  he  removed  to  Buf- 
falo, and  entered  the  Methodist  Book  Depos- 
itory, in  charge  of  H.  H.  Otis,  where  he  contin- 
ued for  about  twelve  years.  In  January,  1878. 
after  Ihoiough  deliberation,  he  purchased  a  half 
interest  in  the  Methodist  Hook  Depository  in 
Detroit,  then  owned  by  J.  M.  Arnold  4  Co., 
and  entered  into  p.-irtnership  with  Mr.  Arnold. 
In  March,  1B79,  he  purchased  the  entire  inteiesl 
and  good-will  of  Mr.  Arnold,  and  assumed  the 
control  of  the  Depository.
